out that the minimum feature size of cell walls in the 3D printed hierarchical honeycombs is about 400 μ m, while the maximum allowable sample size is 26 cm. These requirements allow us to fabricate samples with only 2 × 3 representative volume elements in this work. The hierarchical honeycombs consisting of 2 × 3 representative volume elements were fabricated having an overall dimension of 180 mm ×157mm×30mm(width×height×thickness) for samples with relative densities of 0.16, 0.32 and 0.55 and 252 mm × 219.8 mm × 30 mm (width × height × thickness) for samples with a relative density of 0.11. Within the limitation of 3D printing technology, the layer orientation wasfoundtoinfluence the mechanical properties of the material; therefore, all the samples were printed along the same orientation. For our uniaxial compression and cyclic testing we have fabricated 11 samples, which include one regular honeycomb with a relative density of 0.16, two hierarchical honeycombs with a relative density of 0.11, four hierarchical honeycombs with a relative density of 0.16, two hierarchical honeycombswitharelative density of 0.32, and two hierarchical honeycombs with a relative density of 0.55. The as-fabricated samples were kept at room temperature for seven days to allow for the saturation of the curing process.
2.3. Mechanical testing

To examine the mechanical response of the 3D printed hierarchical honeycombs, uniaxial compressive testing was performed using a universal mechanical testing machine (SHIMADZU AGS-X) with a 300 kN load cell. The experimental setup of the compression testing is shown in Fig. S3. All experiments including the cylinder and hierarchical honeycombs were conducted in a quasi-static regime with a constant strain rate of 0.001 s -1 . Compression was applied to reach the strain of the catastrophic failure of each sample. The load-displacement curves measured from the uniaxial compressive test were then transferred into nominal stress-strain behaviors based on the measured dimensions of the samples. The Young's modulus and the yield stress have been then obtained from the measured stress-strain curve directly (Fig. S1). Images of the samples at various loading conditions were taken at a rate of 1 FPS (VicSnap, Correlated Solution). It should be pointed out that due to the large-scale feature of the proposed hierarchical honeycombs, wehaveonlyconducted repeated tests for the hierarchical honeycombs with a relative density of 0.16 (Fig. S4). We have achieved some very good repeatability within the elastic range, while some differences are observed in the nonlinear domain. These differences may be attributed to the limitation of the 3D printing technique used. In all of our experiments, however, we have recorded the whole deformation process and did not notice any abnormal behaviors, such as out-of-plane deformation.

To study the recoverability and energy dissipation behaviors of 3D printed hierarchical honeycombs, we performed cyclic loadingunloading tests using a universal mechanical testing machine (SHIMADZU AGS-X) with a 300 kN load cell (Fig. S3). The engineering strain rate of 0.001 s -1 was applied for both compressive loading and unloading processes. All tests were taken at a room temperature of 23 °C. In the loading-unloading uniaxial compression tests, each sample was compressed to a target strain endpoint for each loadingunloading cycle. The engineering strain targets were 20%, 40%, and 60% for hierarchical honeycombs with a relative density of 0.16. By contrast, hierarchical honeycombs with a relative density of 0.11, 0.32, and 0.55 were compressed to a strain of 20%. Each sample experienced five loading-unloading cyclic tests.

During the unloading procedure, we notice that the samples can recover to 50% of the applied strain immediately and the recovery of the remaining strain took 72 h. To accelerate our experiment procedure, we harness the shape memory effect of the VeroWhite to recover the compressed samples in a more efficient manner. This method was used for the cyclic testing of all of the hierarchical honeycombs. Each cycle includes three stages: (1) loading-unloading compression testing, (2) heating, and (3) cooling.

Heating recovery experiments with 3D printed hierarchical honeycombs after loading-unloading uniaxial compression tests were carried out in an MTS Advantage ™ Environmental Chamber (Thermcraft LBO24-10-10-11-5001) with temperature control (Fig. S5). Samples were immersed at room temperature into the chamber at 75 °C. They
transformed into their original permanent configuration within 10 min. After that, samples were taken out of the chamber and placed in room temperature for 10 min to cool to the room temperature.
2.4. Finite element analysis

The numerical simulations related to the mechanical response of the hierarchical honeycombs under uniaxial compression are conducted using commercial FE package ABAQUS/Standard (Simulia, Providence, RI). The properties of VeroWhite are characterized by Young's modulus Es =1.56GPa,Poisson's ratio ν =0.33,ayield stress σ y =46.3MPa,and density ρ s =1174 kg/m 3 . The true stress-true strain relation is implemented as the constitutive equation for the mechanical behavior simulations. To save computational time, a smallest repeating representative volume element (RVE) together with periodic boundary conditions is employed in this work (Fig. S6) [55,56], as each representative volume element has 200,000 triangular elements (each beam has 6 elements along thickness direction). All models are generated by plane strain elements CPE4R and meshed after a convergence test. In view of the large deformation of the hierarchical honeycombs, both geometric and material nonlinearities are considered in the finite element simulations.

The constituent phases are taken to be either a representative glassy polymer. The stress-strain behavior of the glassy polymer is captured using an elastic-plastic model which is exported from the true stressstrain relation of VeroWhite. Simulated stress-strain relations of regular and hierarchical honeycombs are plotted in Fig. S7.
3. Results and discussion
3.1. Mechanical behaviors under uniaxial compression

Uniaxial quasi-static compression tests have been performed on both regular and hierarchical honeycombs. For direct comparison, we have considered the case in which the two classes of honeycombs have the same relative density ( ρ / ρ s =0.16) (refer to Videos 1 and 2 in the supporting information for details). Fig. 2(a) shows the measured nominal stress-strain relations. Quantitative comparisons of the effective stiffness and energy absorption between regular and hierarchical honeycombs are plotted in Fig. 2(b) and (c), respectively. The stiffness and energy absorption of the hierarchical honeycombs are 6.6 times and 7.5 times those of the regular honeycombs. The improved mechanical performance can be explained by the unusual behavior of the

hierarchical honeycombs. The regular honeycomb shows a sudden stress drop immediately after the first peak stress (~ ε y = -0.12). This is due to brittle failure of the laminated plastic 3D printed elements, which are subjected to bending under uniaxial compression (Fig. 2(d)). Subsequently, multiple stress drops arise, mainly attributed to the progressive failure of the cell walls. By contrast, the stress decreases gradually in the hierarchical honeycombs. This gradual and smoother plateau-like behavior is associated with the local buckling of the triangular cell walls (Fig. 2(e) -(f)). The global response tends then to become stiffer because the buckled cell walls increasingly contact with each other and provide additional support to carry the compressive load. One can notice after 15% of compressive strain a recurrent rise and decrease of the stress, which may be attributed to the progressive buckling, fracture, and contact of the triangular cell walls. It is noticeable that densification phenomenon emerges at a larger strain (~ ε y = -0.6). Compared with the catastrophic failure of the regular honeycomb, the hierarchical honeycombs tend to produce a global deformation through local buckling and fracture of cell walls, leading to a progressive failure behavior and further facilitating the energy absorption. From a general perspective, the introduction of a cellular structure at a lower hierarchical level provides the equivalent effect of embedding an open cell foam, which produces an elastic-plateau-densification behavior similar to classical foam materials. Moreover, the unusual mechanical responses in these hierarchical honeycombs are attributed to the introduction of the stretched-dominated triangular lattice structure.

It is evident that the enhanced stiffness, energy absorption, and progressive failure mode in the hierarchical honeycombs are all associated with the introduction of the internal triangular lattice structure. The slenderness ratio of the triangular lattice controls these unusual mechanical behaviors, such as buckling. To better understand this, we have fabricated and subjected to flatwise compression hierarchical honeycombs with relative densities of ρ / ρ s =0.16( l t / t t =13.3), ρ / ρ s =0.32( l t / t t =6.5), and ρ / ρ s =0.55( l t / t t =3.2). Hierarchical honeycomb with slightly thicker cell walls ( ρ / ρ s =0.32) exhibit a stress drop similar to one observed for thinner cell wall topologies, which is associated to the local buckling of the cell walls (Fig. 3(a)). At a relatively large strain range, the progressive multiple stress drops become prominent. With the further decrease of the slenderness ratio of the triangular cell walls ( ρ / ρ s =0.55), multiple sudden drops of stress peaks are present and the local buckling is not observed (Fig. 3(c)). Instead, one can observe a global catastrophic failure similar to the one shown by the regular honeycomb.
These analyses suggest that the slenderness ratio of the triangular lattice is critical to control the deformation behavior of the hierarchical

honeycombs. To understand this point from a quantitative perspective we have performed finite element analyses to evaluate the uniaxial compression behavior of the hierarchical honeycombs with different relative densities. The predicted normalized stress-strain relations are displayed in Fig. 3(b). Compared with the measured normalized stress-strain relations, one can observe a good agreement for compressive strains lower than 3%. At relative larger strain ranges our numerical model does not however capture completely the detailed deformation behaviors, because failure modes and contact friction are not included in our simulations. Yet, the numerical simulations still can provide some insightful information. As shown in Fig. 3(d), the local buckling of the triangular lattice can be predicted, which agrees well with the experimental observation. By contrast, the plastic deformation-induced failure is the dominant mechanism responsible for the sudden drop in the stress-strain relation of the hierarchical honeycomb with a relative density of ρ / ρ s =0.55. These experimental and numerical results suggest that the transition between progressive and catastrophic failure modes in the hierarchical honeycombs can be indeed tuned by tailoring the cell wall slenderness ratio.
3.2. Mechanical behavior under cyclic loading

We have shown so far results related to uniaxial compression tests, and have highlighted the mechanism behind their unusual mechanical behavior. We now proceed to examine the mechanical response of the hierarchical honeycombs under cyclic loading conditions. Fig. 4(a) displays the loading ( ε y =0~ -0.6) and unloading ( ε y = -0.6~0) behavior of the hierarchical honeycombs with a relative density of ρ / ρ s =0.16. Upon unloading, the densified hierarchical honeycomb recovers to a strain of ε y = -0.3 (refer to Video 3 in the supporting information for details). This is due to the release of the stored elastic energy in the buckled cell walls of the triangular lattice. The remaining (and incomplete) compression recovers very slowly, and it took about 72 h to fully restore the honeycomb to its initial form. The slow and incomplete recovery is due to irreversible effects, such as cell wall fracture and plastic deformation.

To accelerate the cyclic tests, each compressed sample after the first cycle was put in a chamber at 75 °C and heated for 10 min. This temperature is higher than the nominal 48 °C of the Tg of the polymer, and after this treatment, the samples can recover their initial form due to shape memory effect. We then performed cyclic tests and shape memory assisted recovery on samples under maximum compressible strains of ε y = -0.2 and , respectively. The measured loading-unloading stressstrain relations are plotted in Fig. 4(b) -(d). The effective stiffness, maximumstress, and loss factors are calculated from these measured stressstrain relations, as shown in Fig. 4(e) -(g). The effective stiffness of each hierarchical honeycombs under compressive cyclic strains decreases rapidly after each cycle because the above-cited non-recoverable effects

In contrast to the significant decrease of effective stiffness, the maximum stress of the hierarchical honeycombs with a compressive strain of ε y = -0.2 and ε y = -0.4 has a 35% decrease after the first cycle (Fig. 4(f)), but tends then to stabilize. Quite remarkably, the maximum stresses in the hierarchical honeycombs with a compressive strain of ε y = -0.6 tend to increase by 35% after the first cycle. The decreased and subsequent constant maximum stresses at smaller compressive strains are due to the failure and plastic deformation in the cell walls. On the other hand, the increased maximum stresses at larger compressive strains are attributed to the contact, slip-stick, and densification of the triangular lattice. These different deformation mechanisms are also responsible for the energy dissipation behavior of the hierarchical honeycombs. As can be seen in Fig. 4(g), after the first cycle all hierarchical honeycomb exhibit a high average loss factor of 0.34. Wehaveshownthattheproposedhierarchical honeycombs mayexhibit some excellent structural and shape integrity under large compression strains, which is aided by the release of the stored elastic energy in the buckled cell walls. The structural integrity is also correlated to the slenderness ratio of the triangular lattice. To confirm this feature, we have run cyclic loading tests up to a strain of ε y = -0.2 for hierarchical honeycombs with relative densities of ρ / ρ s =0.16, 0.32, and 0.55. The measured loading-unloading normalized stress-strain relations are plotted in Fig. 5(a) and extracted mechanical responses are summarized in Fig. 5(b). As expected, the specific effective stiffness and maximum stress increase with the relative density, while a very slight increase of the loss factor (tan δ ) is observed. It is also evident that shape memory treatment adopted allows recovering the shape of the hierarchical honeycombs, albeit to a different degree depending on the slenderness ratio. For a smaller slenderness ratio ( ρ / ρ s =0.16), the compressed hierarchical honeycomb tends to conserve its overall shape with no visible damage or crack within its structure. No shape integrity can be however observed in the hierarchical honeycombs with a large slenderness ratio ( ρ / ρ s =0.55) (Fig. 5(c)). These observations further confirm that the excellent structural integrity and shape preserving characteristics are intrinsically governed by the amount of stored elastic energy in the buckled cell walls.
3.3. Scaling laws of the hierarchical honeycombs

The simulated and experimental stiffness and strength of the hierarchical honeycombs with relative densities from 0.02 to 0.32 are plotted in Fig. 6(a) and (b). For the sake of comparison, the stiffness and strength as a function of the relative density for the regular honeycombs case are also displayed. One has to notice that the theoretical predictions for regular honeycombs are derived from the use of the Euler beam theory for truss structures, for which the shear deformation is neglected. Euler-Bernoulli beam theory is only valid for honeycombs with thin cell walls (relative density smaller than 0.25) [20]. In the case of thick cell walls, the analytical solutions for stiffness based on Timoshenko beam theory should be adopted [57,58]. One can notice that the simulated stiffness and strength of the regular honeycombs with relative densities smaller than 0.25 agree well with the linear-elastic and the brittle failure solutions [20]. In the case of regular honeycombs with a relative density of 0.55, the finite element analysis provides however aneffective stiffness of 0.13, which agrees very well with the Timoshenko beam theory-based solution (0.12). These results indicate that our numerical modeling can predict the main mechanical response metrics of regular honeycombs.

Compared with regular honeycombs, the hierarchical honeycombs show a remarkably improved stiffness, and a comparable strength to the one of regular honeycombs. At a relatively low density of 0.02, the hierarchical honeycombs exhibit a ~160 times higher stiffness than the regular honeycombs. We have fit the relative stiffness and strength as a function of relative density using a scaling law, E / Es = B ( ρ / ρ s ) m and σ / σ s = C ( ρ / ρ s ) n , where E and σ are the effective stiffness and strength of the hierarchical honeycombs, respectively. The parameters B and C are geometry-dependent proportionality constants, m and n are the scaling exponents. Here we have B =0.37, C =0.83, m =1.1, and n =2. One can notice a nearly linear relation between the non-dimensional stiffness and the relative density for hierarchical honeycombs. Compared with stochastic materials having a poorly scaling relation between stiffness and relative density, generally following E / Es ~( ρ / ρ s ) 3 [20], the proposed hierarchical honeycombs demonstrate great potential to be designed as ultralight mechanical metamaterials. This finding also suggests that the introduction of an inner triangular lattice enables us to change the bending-dominated behavior into a stretching-dominated one. The relative density dependency of the compressive strength for the hierarchical and the regular honeycombs is almost the same, with the hierarchical honeycombs exhibiting a slightly higher relative strength.
4. Conclusions

Wehave presented a new type of hierarchical honeycomb structure that exhibits a nearly linear relation between stiffness and relative density. At large uniaxial compression and cyclic loading conditions, these hierarchical honeycombs show improved energy absorption and recoverability. These anomalous mechanical behaviors are enabled by introducing a structural hierarchy into regular honeycombs, thereby resulting in the coexistence of local buckling, cell wall fracture, and plastic deformation. The recoverability is also aided by a specific heating process that activates the shape memory effect of the core polymer used in the 3D printing of these cellular structures. Slender cell walls can be critical in structural engineering applications for the potential onset of mechanical instability and catastrophic failure. Here we have however demonstrated that slender cell walls can be efficiently used to tailor the energy absorption and further provide recoverability. We have given evidence, both from an experimental and numerical standpoint that the slenderness ratio of the hierarchical triangular lattice is critical to tune the mechanical responses. The thermal heating applied to the compressed honeycomb above the Tg of the constituent polymer allows preserving the shape of the hierarchical structures under large deformations, at avoiding visible cracks or damages inside the honeycombs.

The hierarchical honeycombs structures are fabricated using commercially available 3D printers and a brittle plastic polymer. It should be pointed out that other advanced manufacturing techniques, such as two-photon lithography can also be employed to create hierarchical cellular solids at a smaller length scale and with a broad choice of materials. As such, the mechanical response of the as-fabricated multiscale hierarchical honeycombs under different conditions, such as low velocity impact and shock wave loadings, can be investigated accordingly. The findings in this work not only provide a better understanding of the critical interplay between material architectures and mechanical properties, but also offer new opportunities to create new mechanical metamaterials with anomalous behaviors for a wide range of potential applications.

Crushing of vertex-based hierarchical honeycombs with triangular substructures
Dahai Zhang a,b , Qingguo Fei a,b,* , Jingze Liu a,b , Dong Jiang a,c , Yanbin Li a,b

A R T I C L E I N F O
Keywords: Hierarchy Honeycomb Vertex-based Crushing Energy absorption
1. Introduction

Thin-walled cellular structures are widely used as energy absorbers in kinds of industrial fields, such as aerospace, transportation and package engineering. This is due to their excellent mechanical properties and outstanding performance in energy absorption [1,2]. As one of the most common cellular structures, honeycombs have been studied extensively [3]. Honeycombs with various cell configurations including hexagon, circle, square and triangle have been explored by means of experimental, theoretical and numerical methods [4 -7]. In order to improve the crashworthiness and energy absorption capacity of honeycombs, several optimization strategies were introduced and examined [2,8]. Accordingly, honeycombs with novel configurations were constructed and explored, such as graded honeycombs [9] and multi-cell honeycombs [10]. These improvement approaches enhanced or changed the mechanical properties of the honeycombs variously.

It has been proved repeatedly that multi-cell thin-walled structures perform superiorly in achieving better energy absorption capacity [11 -13]. Recently, the multi-cell structures with hierarchical characteristics drew a lot of attention due to their superior performance in
A B S T R A C T

Triangular lattices were hierarchically incorporated into a regular hexagonal honeycomb to substitute each vertex of the regular honeycomb. To investigate the crushing behaviors of the newly constructed vertex-based hierarchical honeycombs, finite element (FE) analyses were carried out subsequently for both in-plane and out-of-plane crushing. Effect of the hierarchical organizational parameter on deformation mode, crushing response and energy absorption capacity were discussed respectively. The results showed that the hierarchy significantly affected the crushing behaviors of the honeycomb differently for in-plane and out-of-plane crushing. The hierarchy had a far greater influence on the deformation mode for in-plane crushing than out-of-plane crushing. Compared with that of the regular honeycomb, hierarchy not always improved the honeycomb in terms of plateau stress and specific energy absorption ( SEA ). The maximum plateau stress and SEA of the hierarchical honeycombs increased by up to approximately 127% and 109% respectively under crushing along L direction (in-plane ribbon direction); 122% and 108% respectively under crushing along W direction (in-plane width direction); and 30% and 34% respectively under crushing along T direction (out-of-plane direction).

achieving high structural efficiency [14 -17]. Hierarchically functional adaption of the structures at all levels makes their mechanical properties exceptional. A variety of hierarchical structures, such as hierarchical tubes [13,18], lattice structures [19,20], composites [21,22], and honeycombs [23 -40] were developed and followed by numerous works in exploring their mechanical properties over the recent years.

Sun et al. [18] designed hierarchical lattice tubes with lattice-core sandwich walls and explored their crushing behaviors. They proved that a hierarchical tube had a much higher compressive strength than an equal mass single-cell tube. More specifically, 3 to 5 times higher for in-plane crushing and 3 to 4 times higher for out-of-plane crushing. Fan et al. [19] and Yin et al. [20] designed and manufactured hierarchical composite pyramidal lattice trusses. Experiments were carried out to explore the compressive strength and deformation mode of the structures. They found that hierarchy made the lattice composite much more ductile and weight-efficient in energy absorption. Hierarchical woven lattice composites were developed by Zhao et al. [21] and Zheng et al. [22]. They found hierarchy significantly improved the energy absorption capacity and weight efficiency.
The hierarchy has also been introduced into the design of

honeycombs. There were two common methods in the hierarchical arrangement of honeycombs. One was to substitute the solid cell walls with different types of micro-structures. Fan et al. [23] and Taylor et al. [24] substituted the solid cell walls with sandwich substructures and found that their mechanical properties were significantly improved on an equal density basis. Other hierarchical honeycombs were developed by replacing the solid cell walls by micro-cells of various configurations [25 -27]. Taylor et al. [28] combined hierarchy and functional grading together in the design of honeycombs and found it surpassed up to 2 times in-plane Young ' s moduli of conventional honeycombs. Another hierarchical arrangement was to replace vertexes of a regular honeycomb by smaller substructures. Ajdari et al. [29], Oftadeh et al. [30], Mousanezhad et al. [31] and Zhang et al. [32] constructed fractal-like hierarchical honeycombs. They replaced each vertex of a regular hexagonal honeycomb with a smaller self-similar hexagon. By repeating this process, honeycombs of higher hierarchical orders were constructed. Ajdari et al. [29] found that the hierarchy of first and second orders improved the in-plane stiffness up to 2.0 and 3.5 times, respectively. Oftadeh et al. [30] explored the mechanics of the hierarchical honeycombs with higher orders and found that hierarchical honeycombs of first to fourth orders can be 2.0 -8.0 times stiffer and up to 2.0 times stronger than regular ones. Mousanezhad et al. [31] investigated the

effect of hierarchy and imposed deformations on band structures. In addition, investigations on the out-of-plane crushing of the honeycombs with self-similar hierarchy were also conducted by Sun et al. [33] and Zhang et al. [34]. They found structural hierarchy could significantly improve the energy absorption capacity of honeycombs under the same relative density. Specifically, the specific energy absorption of the hierarchical honeycombs of first and second orders was up to 1.8 and 2.9 times than regular honeycombs.

All the vertex-based hierarchical honeycombs mentioned above were constructed by substituting vertexes with self-similar substructures. However, hierarchical materials in biological tissues are much more complicated and diversified. A few vertex-based hierarchical honeycombs with non-self-similar substructures were also introduced by Chen et al. [35] and Mousanezhad et al. [36]. Besides, the abovementioned studies largely focused on the hierarchical hexagonal honeycombs. The hierarchical honeycombs with triangular substructures have not been reported to our best knowledge. Furthermore, most of the hierarchical honeycombs investigated have specific configurations rather than with a wide range of hierarchy. Effects of the hierarchical parameters on crashworthiness and energy absorption capacity are not well addressed, especially for the non-self-similar vertex-based ones. Hence, it is of important engineering significance to construct more types of

hierarchical structures and detect their crushing behaviors.

In this paper, vertex-based hierarchical honeycombs with triangular substructures are developed. Crashworthiness and energy absorption properties of the hierarchical honeycombs under in-plane and out-ofplane crushing are respectively investigated. In Section 2, hierarchical arrangement and configurations of the hierarchical honeycombs are introduced. In Section 3, the finite element (FE) models are established and validated. In Section 4, the numerical results are presented and discussed. Deformation mode, crushing response and energy absorption of the hierarchical honeycombs are analyzed and discussed, respectively. Comparisons with the regular honeycomb are also carried out. In Section 5, some remarkable conclusions are presented.
2. Hierarchical configurations

Based on the concept of hierarchical design of materials, smaller equilateral triangular lattices are hierarchically introduced to replace each three-edge vertex of a base hexagonal honeycomb, as shown in Fig. 1. In this paper, the most common hexagonal honeycomb with an expanding angle of θ ¼ 120 ∘ is investigated. A structural organizational parameter, γ , is defined to determine the configuration of a hierarchical honeycomb, which is the ratio of lengths of the newly introduced triangle edge to the base hexagon edge.
where Lh and L 0 are respectively the lengths of the triangle edge and the base hexagon edge.

3. Numerical models
3.1. Finite element modeling

To explore the crushing behavior of the hierarchical honeycombs, numerical analyses are conducted. The explicit FE code ABAQUS/ Explicit is employed to simulate the crushing processes of the hierarchical honeycombs with various geometrical parameters. As typical anisotropic structures, honeycombs have three orthotropic directions: two in-plane (named as L and W, where L is the ribbon direction and W is the width direction) and one out-of-plane (named as T), as shown in Fig. 1. Therefore, simulations of crushing along the three directions are carried out respectively. A honeycomb consists of a large number of micro-cells, which makes it always meet the computational challenge for simulation based on a complete model with all details considered [41]. Many simplified modeling methods have been introduced and employed in literature, such as the equivalent homogeneous model, row model and cell model [42]. Considering anisotropy of the honeycombs, different simplified modeling approaches are used to establish the models for in-plane and out-of-plane crushing.
3.1.1. FE model for in-plane crushing

As shown in Fig. 2(a), the FE model for in-plane crushing consists of two rigid walls and a honeycomb sandwiched between the rigid walls. For calculation efficiency, the top and bottom rigid walls are respectively positioned 1 mm above and below the honeycomb. During the crush, the top rigid wall moves toward the honeycomb with a constant velocity of 5 mm/s, while the bottom rigid wall is fully fixed. The honeycomb is composed of 17 cells in L direction and 16 cells in W direction. Symmetric boundary conditions are applied to each side (LW plane) of the structure to prevent out-of-plane buckling and eliminate the

Fig. 4. Schematic diagram of the X-shaped cell model: (a) basic X-shaped cell model; (b) modified X-shaped cell model.

boundary effects [43]. The honeycomb is meshed using hourglass controlled, four nodes, reduced integration shell element S4R with five integration points across the wall thickness. The contacts between the rigid walls and the honeycomb are simulated using surface-to-surface
Fig. 5. Effect of element size on the mean crushing force and computing time for the base honeycomb under crushing along L direction.

contact. A general contact algorithm is defined to simulate the self-contact of the unit cells. The friction coefficient is set as 0.2. The cell wall material is assumed to be elastic, perfectly plastic. It is a common constitutive model widely used in numerical analysis for crushing of aluminum honeycombs [7]. Typical aluminum alloy with Young ' s modulus of E ¼ 68 : 2 GPa , the yield stress of σ ys ¼ 350MPa Poisson ' s ratio of ν ¼ 0 : 3 and density of ρ s ¼ 2700kg = m 3 is assigned [44].
3.1.2. FE model for out-of-plane crushing

It is shown in Fig. 1 that the hierarchical honeycombs become much more complicated compared with the regular one. It meets a big computational challenge in the simulation process if using a detailed model. Therefore, unit cell models are used for out-of-plane crushing simulations, which is a common technique employed in literature [45, 46]. A Y-shaped cell model [42] as shown in Fig. 3(a) has been widely used to explore the out-of-plane crashworthiness of honeycombs and is also employed in this study. Symmetric boundary conditions are imposed on the non-intersecting edges of the unit cell, as shown in Fig. 3 (a). After the triangular edges hierarchically introduced, the Y-shaped cell model should be modified. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the modified Y-shaped cell model consists of three full-length triangular edges and three half-length remaining edges of the base regular honeycomb. However, the remaining edges of the base honeycomb are much shorter when γ is large. It may bring errors to impose symmetric boundary conditions on the much shorter remaining edges. Herein, another more reasonable cell model, X-shaped cell model, is established to overcome
D. Zhang et al.

this problem for hierarchical honeycombs with a larger γ . Fig. 4(a) shows the X-shaped cell model extracted from the honeycomb of γ ¼ 0 : 866 (Kagome honeycomb). Likewise, the X-shaped cell model is also modified with γ changing. As shown in Fig. 4(b), the modified X-shaped cell model consists of four half-length triangular edges and one full-length remaining edge of the base honeycomb. Symmetric boundary conditions are imposed on the non-intersecting edges of the unit cell as well. As shown in Fig. 2(b), the FE model for out-of-plane crushing is the same as the above FE model for in-plane crushing except that the whole model sandwiched between the rigid walls is replaced by the unit cell model.
3.2. Validation of FE models

Convergence tests on element size are conducted firstly. Fig. 5 shows the effect of element size on the calculated mean crushing force and computing time for the base regular honeycomb under crushing along L direction. It is seen that the simulated mean crushing force gradually stabilizes and converges when the element size reduces to 0.8 mm. Meanwhile, the computing time increases exponentially with the decrease of the element size. Therefore, 0.4 mm is chosen for both computing efficiency and results accuracy conservatively. Convergence tests are also conducted on the unit cell models and element size of 0.1 mm is used.

Validation analyses are conducted subsequently to validate the accuracy of the numerical models. There are many analogous studies on the regular hexagonal honeycombs available in the literature. Gibson et al. [3] theoretically deduced the crushing strength of the regular hexagonal honeycomb. (10) and (11) may have problems when they are used in large deformation analyses. The cross-section area A is changing with crushing proceeds. So, the stress and strain defined here are nominal ones. It is reasonable because the nominal stress-strain curve is physically another representation of the force-displacement curve. By doing this, the effect of the specific size of a honeycomb structure can be eliminated. Crashworthiness of different sized structures can be compared with each other directly. Hence, this approach is widely used in crushing analyses of honeycomb, even for those auxetic honeycombs with negative Poisson ' s ratio [47,48].

Fig. 6 illustrates the verification of the numerical model for in-plane crushing. The simulation results are compared with the theoretical value from Eq. (6) and the numerical results reported by Ruan et al. [7] Besides, deformation pattern of the honeycomb is also compared with that reported by Ruan et al. [7] Good agreements for both crushing response and deformation pattern are observed. The numerical cell model for out-of-plane crushing is also validated by comparing with the theoretical value from Eq. (7) as well as result obtained from a whole model with 7 � 6 cells, as shown in Fig. 7. The collapse mode and crushing response are almost the same by using the whole model and unit cell model. It is noted that both the theoretical solutions in Eqs. (6) and (7) are idealized and simplified. For in-plane crushing, the theoretical solution of plateau stress is derived based on the plastic collapse of a single hexagon cell. While for out-of-plane crushing, the theoretical solution of plateau stress is derived from analysis of the plastic collapse of a single cell flange. The simulated crushing process is closer to the real situation. Hence, a stress-strain curve is obtained rather than only a value. Although the theoretically simplified calculations ignore the necessity of compatibility at the cell edges and contact of the cell walls [3]. They still can reasonably illustrate the level of plateau stresses and be used to validate the simulated crushing responses.

Moreover, to validate the accuracy of the presented FE modeling method, simulation results are compared with experimental results of hierarchical honeycombs conducted by Fang et al. [45] as well as their simulation results. The honeycomb material is aluminum alloy with a density of ρ ¼ 2580kg = m 3 , Poisson ' s ratio of ν ¼ 0 : 28 and Young ' s modulus of E ¼ 70GPa. The honeycomb has a height of 100 mm and a wall thickness of 0.55 mm, and the cell edge length of the hexagons is 8 mm [45]. The modeling approach is the same as described in section 3.1. The force-displacement curves and deformation patterns are plotted in Fig. 8. It is seen that both the simulated force-displacement curves and the crushing behaviors are in good agreement with those reported by Fang et al. [45]. Figs. 6 -8 imply that the modeling approach is reliable.
4. Results and discussions
4.1. In-plane crushing of the hierarchical honeycombs

Crushing along the two in-plane directions are simulated and explored respectively. Various hierarchical honeycombs with γ ranged from 0.1 to 0.866 are considered here. All the hierarchical honeycombs are developed based on the same base honeycomb with cell length of L 0 ¼ 3 : 666 mm, wall thickness of t 0 ¼ 0 : 1 mm and the height of the honeycomb (T direction) is set to be 1.0 mm. As a comparison, the base honeycomb is also simulated, which can be seen as a special hierarchical honeycomb of γ ¼ 0. Configurations of the hierarchical honeycombs and the base honeycomb are listed in Table 1.
4.1.1. Deformation characteristics

Figs. 9 and 10 show the comparisons of deformation histories of the hierarchical honeycombs of γ ¼ 0, γ ¼ 0 : 3, γ ¼ 0 : 6 and γ ¼ 0 : 866 under crushing along L and W directions, respectively. Fig. 11 shows the deformation histories of 8 cells extracted from the typical hierarchical honeycomb with γ ¼ 0 : 6 under crushing along L direction. The crushing behaviors for both the hexagon framework and the triangular substructures are illustrated clearly in Fig. 11, the other cases are similar.

As shown in Figs. 9(a) and 10(a), the localized band initially generates at the waist of the base hexagonal honeycomb. For crushing along L direction, the localized band is V-shaped while it is X-shaped along W direction. With the increase of compression, the initially localized band is stacked up layer-by-layer until densification. As shown in Figs. 9(b) and 10(b), deformation pattern of the hierarchical honeycomb of γ ¼ 0 : 3 is similar to that of the regular honeycomb overall. One evident difference is that the localized band is slightly wider in the crushing direction, which indicates that the localized band is compressed less thoroughly compared with that of the regular honeycomb. The main reason is that the hierarchically introduced triangular substructures are much difficult to be crushed compared with the basic hexagon cells due to their much smaller size. As a result, the crushing process of the hierarchical honeycomb can be divided into two sub-stages: I) preliminary crushing, where the hexagonal cells are collapsing and no obvious crushing is observed for the triangular substructures; II) further crushing, where the triangular substructures are crushed predominantly till densification. This phenomenon can be easily concluded from Fig. 11. With increasing of γ , sub-stage I becomes shorter and sub-stage II becomes longer. Meanwhile, the boundary of the two sub-stages is blurring, as shown in Figs. 9(c) and 10(c) for γ ¼ 0 : 6. This is because when γ is larger, γ ¼ 0 : 6 for example, the triangular substructures becomes easier to be crushed. A few triangular substructures are crushed accompanying with collapsing of the hexagonal framework, results in intersection and overlapping of sub-stages I and II. Also, less significant localized band is observed. Instead, the honeycomb is crushed globally in the whole process. With further increasing of γ , the crushing process turns into only one stage again for γ ¼ 0 : 866 due to all the basic hexagon walls of the regular honeycomb have been replaced by triangular substructures. As shown in Figs. 9(d) and 10(d), an I-shaped localized band generates from both the top and bottom edges of the honeycomb. With crushing proceed, the I-shaped localized bands propagate forward layer-by-layer from both top and bottom edges to the middle.
4.1.2. Crushing response
Fig. 12 illustrates the nominal stress-strain curves for the four typical hierarchical honeycombs of γ ¼ 0, γ ¼ 0 : 3, γ ¼ 0 : 6 and γ ¼ 0 : 866 under crushing along L and W directions, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 12(a), the nominal stress-strain curve for the base honeycomb under crushing along L direction contains three distinct regimes: a linear elastic regime initially, a long plateau regime in the middle and a compressive densification regime in the end. It is a common trend which has been reported and discussed widely in the literature. For the case of γ ¼ 0 : 3, two sub-stages are distinctly observed in
D. Zhang et al.
Fig. 9. Deformation histories for different hierarchical honeycombs under crushing along L direction: (a) γ ¼ 0; (b) γ ¼ 0 : 3; (c) γ ¼ 0 : 6; (d) γ ¼ 0 : 866.

the plateau regime: the former one is much longer and slightly lower than that of the base honeycomb; the latter one is very short and significantly larger than that of the base honeycomb. However, it performs significantly differently for the case of γ ¼ 0 : 6, where the plateau regime can also be divided into two sub-stages. It is basically flat and slightly lower than that of the base honeycomb when the strain is small ( ε < 0 : 4), and increases nonlinearly and becomes significantly larger than that of the base honeycomb with increasing of strain ( ε > 0 : 4) until densification. The curve for the case of γ ¼ 0 : 866 is similar in trend with that of γ ¼ 0 but with different magnitude. Accompanying with the deformation pattern, the honeycombs of γ ¼ 0 and γ ¼ 0 : 866 being crushed layer-by-layer generally, which leads to three distinct regimes of the stress-strain curves; while the two sub-stages in the plateau regime are respectively corresponding to the two sub-stages for the honeycombs of γ ¼ 0 : 3 and γ ¼ 0 : 6. Same conclusions can be drawn for the hierarchical honeycombs under crushing along W direction, as shown in Fig. 12(b). It is also seen that with γ increases, the densification strain decreases compared with that of the regular honeycomb. The main reason is that the hierarchically introduced triangular substructures are much difficult to be crushed compared with the basic hexagon cells due to their much smaller size. With γ continues increasing, the densification strain increase again. It is because the micro-structures becomes simple again due to all the basic hexagon walls of the regular honeycomb have been replaced by triangular substructures.
Fig. 13 shows the plateau stresses of the hierarchical honeycombs with different γ under crushing along L and W directions. It is seen that

Fig. 10. Deformation histories for different hierarchical honeycombs under crushing along W direction: (a) γ ¼ 0; (b) γ ¼ 0 : 3; (c) γ ¼ 0 : 6; (d) γ ¼ 0 : 866.

the trends of the plateau stresses with γ for crushing along L and W directions are similar. The results show that σ pl decreases slightly when γ is small ( γ � 0 : 2), then becomes significantly greater when γ increasing from 0.3 to 0.5. In the region of 0 : 5 � γ � 0 : 6, the plateau stress reaches its maximum. With further increasing of γ ( γ � 0 : 6), the plateau stress decreases again until the maximum possible value of γ . The change of plateau stress can be obtained from observation of the crushing process of the honeycombs. It is well known that the plateau stress is contributed by the collapse of the cell walls. For the hierarchical honeycombs with a small γ , γ < 0 : 2 in this case, the plateau stress is mainly contributed by the collapse of the hexagonal framework. It is lower than that of the regular honeycomb and decreases with the increase of γ , which is due to the continuous decrease of wall thickness. The contribution of the triangular substructures is very small because they are too small to be crushing before densification, as shown in Figs. 9 -11. With γ increasing, from 0.3 to 0.5 in this case, the triangular substructures become easier to be crushed. A few triangular substructures are crushed accompanying with collapsing of the hexagonal framework, results in a significant increase of the plateau stress. All the plateau stresses are listed in Table 1. According to the simulations, the largest plateau stresses are observed in the case of γ ¼ 0 : 5 under crushing along L direction and in the case of γ ¼ 0 : 6 under crushing along W direction, which are 0.359 MPa and 0.343 MPa, respectively.

4.1.3. Energy absorption
Specific energy absorption ( SEA ) is the most important indicator widely used to evaluate the energy absorption capacity of structures, which is defined as

where EA and M are respectively the energy absorbed during the crushing process and the total mass of the structure. The EA of the honeycomb can be calculated by integration of the crushing force along crushing displacement, as

Fig. 14 illustrates the histories of EA and the ultimate SEA for different hierarchical honeycombs under crushing along both L and W directions. As shown in Fig. 14 (a), almost overlapped curves are observed for the same honeycomb under L and W directions, which indicates that the hierarchical honeycomb has almost the same energy absorption capacity in L and W directions. The EA for the hierarchical honeycombs of γ ¼ 0 and γ ¼ 0 : 866 increase linearly with strain until densification. For the case of γ ¼ 0 : 6, two sub-stages are evidently identified corresponding to the two sub-stages in the plateau regime of the stress-strain curve: I) in the first sub-stage, the EA increases linearly with the strain; II) in the second sub-stage, the EA increases nonlinearly and sharply with strain. The EA for γ ¼ 0 : 3 increases linearly and no other sub-stages are significantly observed. The main reason for this phenomenon is that the second sub-stage of the stress-strain curve for γ ¼ 0 : 3 is too short and too high as shown in Fig. 12, which makes the second sub-stage hard to be distinguished from the transition region of the EA curves. It is also seen that the EA for hierarchical honeycomb of γ ¼ 0 : 3 and γ ¼ 0 : 6 are slightly lower than that of γ ¼ 0 and γ ¼ 0 : 866 at first but rapidly climb and become much higher than that of γ ¼ 0 and γ ¼ 0 : 866 in the end. From Fig. 14(b), it is seen that SEA for crushing along L and W directions are similar, too. The results show that SEA is almost the same when γ is small, then becomes greater when γ increasing from 0.3 to 0.5, and decreases again with further increasing of γ ( γ � 0 : 6) until the maximum possible γ . The calculated SEA are listed in Table 1.

According to the simulations, the largest SEA are obtained in the case of γ ¼ 0 : 5 under crushing along L direction and in the case of γ ¼ 0 : 6 under crushing along W direction, which are 2.999 kJ/kg and 2.803 kJ/kg, respectively.

In order to better understand the crush responses and energy absorption characteristics of the hierarchical honeycombs under in-plane crushing, schematic diagrams of the stress-strain curve and history of EA are drawn in Fig. 15. As shown in Fig. 15, both illustrations are simplified based on observations of simulation results. The initial and densification regimes make no significant sense to energy absorption, so the stress-strain curves of the hierarchical and the regular honeycombs with only the plateau regime considered in Fig. 15(a). The stress for the regular honeycomb is constant throughout, while it contains two substages for the hierarchical honeycomb. The stress-strain curve of the hierarchical honeycomb in sub-stage I is contributed mainly by the collapse of the hexagonal framework. It is lower than that of the regular honeycomb and decreases with the increase of γ , which is due to the continuous decrease of wall thickness. The stress-strain curve in substage II is much complicated, which is contributed by the collapse of the triangular substructures dominantly. Generally, it increases with strain in sub-stage II. Also, it decreases with increasing of γ mainly because of reduction of the ratio of wall thickness to edge length of the triangular substructures, i.e., t h = Lh . Besides, with increasing of γ , the durations of sub-stages I deceases due to decrease of the reminding edges of the base honeycomb. The shaded areas are the EA . Fig. 15(b) shows the histories of EA for both the regular and hierarchical honeycombs. The EA increases linearly with crushing proceeds for the regular honeycomb. For the hierarchical honeycomb, it also increases linearly in sub-stage I, but increases nonlinearly and rapidly in sub-stage II.
4.2. Out-of-plane crushing of the hierarchical honeycombs

The hierarchical honeycombs considered for the out-of-plane crushing are the same as those for the in-plane crushing, i.e., γ ranged from 0.1 to 0.866. All the unit cells are extracted from hierarchical honeycombs developed based on the same hexagonal honeycomb with cell length of L 0 ¼ 3 : 666mm, wall thickness of t 0 ¼ 0 : 1mm and initial height of 25 mm. As a comparison, the base hexagonal honeycomb is

also simulated, which can be seen as a special hierarchical honeycomb with γ ¼ 0. Configurations of the hierarchical honeycombs and the base honeycomb are listed in Table 2. Considering when γ is sufficiently large, γ � 0 : 6 in this case, the remaining edges of the base honeycomb are much shorter. It may bring errors to impose symmetric boundary conditions on such shorter remaining edges. The Y-shaped cell model is no longer appropriate. So the X-shaped cell model is used to overcome this problem for 0 : 6 � γ � 0 : 866, and the Y-shaped cell model is used for 0 � γ � 0 : 5.
4.2.1. Deformation characteristics

The deformed cell models of the honeycombs at the nominal strain of ε ¼ 0 : 9 are presented in Fig. 16. It is seen that the walls are crushed layer-by-layer in general for all cases considered. The number of complete crushing folds increases first then decreases with the increase of γ . Specifically, there are 8, 14 and 12 complete crushing folds for γ ¼ 0, γ ¼ 0 : 6 and γ ¼ 0 : 866, respectively.
4.2.2. Crushing response

Fig. 17 shows the nominal stress-strain curves and plateau stresses of the hierarchical honeycombs with different γ . As shown in Fig. 17(a), the trends of the nominal stress-strain curves for all cases are similar and demonstrate three regimes as well: a linear regime, a plateau regime and a densification regime. It is seen that the fluctuations are much heavy in the plateau regime for the hierarchical honeycombs compared with that of the base hexagonal honeycomb. Actually, this phenomenon is not newly observed, which have been reported in both experimental and numerical investigations [4,43]. The crushing force curves show periodically fluctuations in the form of alternate peak and troughs, corresponding to the collapse of honeycomb cells in layer by layer. The hierarchical honeycombs have many irregularities in their shape compared with the base hexagonal honeycomb, causing complexity of the crushing behaviors. There are two constitutive elements in a hierarchical honeycomb, which are the remaining wall segments of the base honeycomb and the triangular edges. The different wavelengths of the

As shown in Fig. 17 (b), the plateau stress becomes greater when γ increases from 0 to 0.2, then decreases when γ increases further till the maximum possible value. The specific data are listed in Table 2. According to the simulations, the largest plateau stress is 5.759 MPa in the case of γ ¼ 0 : 2.

Fig. 18 illustrates the histories of EA and ultimate SEA for different hierarchical honeycombs. It should be noted that the EA for γ ¼ 0 : 6 and γ ¼ 0 : 866 are corrected because the X-shaped cell model is only twothirds of the Y-shaped cell model in terms of mass. As shown in Fig. 18(a), the EA increases linearly with strain until densification for all cases. It is also seen that the slopes of the curves are different, which indicates that the rates of increase of EA are different. Among which, the curve of γ ¼ 0 : 3 has the largest slope and curve of γ ¼ 0 : 866 has the smallest slope. According to the simulations, the largest SEA is obtained in the case of γ ¼ 0 : 2, which is 45.99 kJ/kg.
4.3. Comparisons with regular hexagonal honeycomb

To explore how much improvement of crashworthiness and energy absorption of the hierarchical honeycombs over regular honeycomb, comparisons between them are conducted. Fig. 19 shows the comparisons between hierarchical honeycombs and regular honeycomb in terms of normalized plateau stress ( σ pl ) and normalized SEA ( SEA )
where σ γ pl and σ 0 pl are the plateau stresses of the hierarchical and regular honeycombs, respectively; SEA γ and SEA 0 are the SEA of the hierarchical

and regular honeycombs, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 19(a), the normalized plateau stress σ pl is not always larger than 1, which indicates that the hierarchical honeycomb in terms of σ pl is not always better than that of the regular honeycomb. For inplane crushing, only when γ � 0 : 4, the hierarchical honeycomb has a significantly better performance. Specifically, the largest plateau stresses are obtained in the case of γ ¼ 0 : 5 under crushing along L direction and in the case of γ ¼ 0 : 6 under crushing along W direction, which are 127% and 122% higher than that of the regular honeycomb, respectively. For out-of-plane crushing, σ pl is larger than 1 in the region of 0 : 1 � γ � 0 : 6 but smaller than 1 when γ is too large ( γ � 0 : 7 in this case). According to the simulations, the best crashworthiness in terms of σ pl for crushing along T direction is achieved with γ ¼ 0 : 2 for all the γ considered, where the plateau stress improves by approximately 30% compared with that of the regular honeycomb.

As shown in Fig. 19(b), the normalized SEA is not always larger than 1 either, which means that the energy absorption capacity of the hierarchical is not always improved compared with that of the regular honeycomb. For in-plane crushing, the SEA is close to 1 when γ is small ( γ � 0 : 3), but becomes significantly larger than 1 when γ � 0 : 4 and reaches its maximum in the region of 0 : 5 � γ � 0 : 6. The largest SEA are achieved in the case of γ ¼ 0 : 5 and γ ¼ 0 : 6 under crushing along L direction and W directions, respectively, which are 109% and 108% higher than that of the regular honeycomb. For out-of-plane crushing, the hierarchical honeycomb has a better performance when 0 : 1 � γ � 0 : 6 in terms of SEA . According to the simulations, the largest SEA for all γ considered is observed in the case of γ ¼ 0 : 2, which is 34% higher than that of the regular honeycomb. In summary, the hierarchy can improve the plateau stress and energy absorption capacity of the honeycomb with a selected hierarchical organizational parameter. On the whole, it performs better when γ is large for in-plane crushing and when γ is small for out-of-plane crushing, the critical γ is named as γ here. And the specific value of γ can be calculated from Fig. 19(a) by extracting and averaging the two points of intersection of the trend lines of the plateaus stress for the three directions, γ ¼ 0 : 352 in the present cases. Moreover, the maximum improvement performs significantly larger for in-plane
D. Zhang et al.
crushing than out-of-plane crushing.
5. Conclusions

By replacing each three-edge vertex of a regular hexagonal honeycomb with a smaller triangular substructure, a class of hierarchical honeycombs was constructed. Crashworthiness and energy absorption characteristics of the hierarchical honeycombs under both in-plane and out-of-plane crushing were revealed subsequently. Effects of the hierarchical organizational parameters on the deformation mode, crushing response and energy absorption were discussed, respectively.
The main conclusions can be summarized as follows:

For in-plane crushing, it performs almost the same under crushing along the two in-plane directions, L and W. Hierarchy significantly changes the deformation pattern. Both the plateau stress and SEA decrease with γ slightly when γ is small ( γ � 0 : 2 in this case) and increase rapidly until γ ¼ 0 : 6, and then decrease promptly again with increasing of γ . In the region of 0 : 5 � γ � 0 : 6, both the plateau stress and SEA reach their maximums.

For out-of-plane crushing, the deformation pattern is similar for all hierarchical honeycombs, the only difference is that the number of complete folds is different due to the variation of wall thickness. When γ � 0 : 2, both the plateau stress and SEA increase with γ and then decrease until the maximum possible γ . Both the plateau stress and SEA reach their maximums in the region of 0 : 1 � γ � 0 : 3, γ ¼ 0 : 2 in the present paper.
Compared with the regular honeycomb, the hierarchy is not always advantageous to crashworthiness and energy absorption. It

A novel star auxetic honeycomb with enhanced in-plane crushing strength
Lulu Wei, Xuan Zhao * , Qiang Yu , Guohua Zhu
School of Automobile, Chang ' an University, Xi ' an, 710064, China
A R T I C L E I N F O
Keywords: Auxetic honeycomb Deformation model Crushing strength Densification strain Negative Poisson ' s ratio
1. Introduction

Recently, the pursuit of lightweight design and safety in the aerospace, automobile, packaging, and human protection industries has driven the need for new lightweight materials to meet higher performance demands [1,2]. As typical honeycomb metamaterials, auxetic materials have drawn extensive attention due to their excellent performance, including lightweight [3], high shear modulus [4,5], indentation resistance [6,7], and excellent acoustic [8] and energy absorption properties [9 -11]. These superior characteristics are attributable to the negative Poisson ' s ratio (NPR) effect of auxetic materials, which expand (contract) when they are stretched (compressed) [12,13]. The mechanical properties of auxetic materials are affected by their microstructure, matrix materials, and relative density, among which the microstructure is the most critical factor [14]. Therefore, considerable work has been conducted to improve the mechanical properties of the auxetic honeycomb by designing new topological structures.

After auxetic material was first manufactured by Lakes [13] in 1987, various topological structures of auxetic honeycombs were designed and studied, such as arrow structures [14], re-entrant auxetic structures [15,
* Corresponding author. E-mail address: zhaoxuan@chd.edu.cn (X. Zhao).
A B S T R A C T

Auxetic honeycombs exhibit low weight, shear stiffness, and excellent energy absorption capacity and thus have great potential for achieving the requirements of crashworthiness and lightweight in automotive fields. This work presents a novel auxetic structure called the star-triangular honeycomb (STH), in which the horizontal and vertical ligaments of the star honeycombs (SH) are replaced with triangular structures. The dynamic crushing behaviors of the STH under three different crushing velocities were investigated using 1D shock theory. The results show that the STH has a more obvious negative Poisson ' s ratio effect than the SH and that transverse contraction mainly occurs in the first plateau stage. Theoretical models were deduced based on the collapse mechanism of the typical unit revealed by numerical simulation for STH crushing strength prediction. The theoretical predictions agreed well with the simulation results, and two different plateau stresses appeared under low-velocity crushing. In addition, the influences of the STH geometric parameters and crushing velocity on the energy-absorbing capacity and densification strain were systematically explored. The parameter analysis indicated that the effects of the cell-wall thickness and incline angle on the dynamic response and energy absorption capacity of the STH under low-and medium-velocity crushing are more significant than those under high-velocity crushing. Moreover, the STH showed better energy absorption capacity than the SH. Thus, this design is expected to provide a novel means of improving the mechanical properties of honeycombs.

16], and chiral honeycombs [17], among which the double arrow and re-entrant structures have superior mechanical properties [18]. Scarpa et al. [19] revealed the relationships between the geometric and mechanical properties of auxetic honeycombs via finite element analysis. Furthermore, Masters and Evans [20] predicted the Young ' s modulus, shear modulus and Poisson ' s ratio of the re-entrant auxetic honeycomb using plastic deformation theory. Theocaris et al. [21] explored the effects of the material properties and microstructure on the Poisson ' s ratio by numerical homogenization approach. Gao [22,23] analyzed the effects of cell-wall angles and thicknesses on the dynamic crushing behavior of a 3D double-arrowed structure. The results showed that increasing the cell-wall angle, cell-wall thickness and number of cells could effectively improve the energy absorption capacity of the 3D double-arrowed structure.

In fact, the dynamic crushing performance is an essential parameter for evaluating the material mechanical properties of auxetic honeycombs, especially in the aerospace and automotive industries. Therefore, researchers have studied the dynamic deformation mode and crushing performance of auxetic honeycombs extensively. H € onig and Stronge [24] derived an analytical formula for the critical crushing velocity

journal homepage: http://www.elsevier.com/locate/tws
L. Wei et al.

under a uniaxial load using ' wave trapping ' theory. Based on this theory, Zhao et al. [25] discussed the deformation mode and established a theoretical model for the dynamic crushing strength of double-arrowed auxetic structures in the z - and x -directions. Qiao et al. [26,27] investigated the quasi-static and dynamic crushing deformations of both uniform and functionally graded double-arrowed auxetic structures and established theoretical formulas for the plateau stresses under low- and high-velocity loads. Simultaneously, Zhang et al. [28] analyzed the dynamic crushing response of re-entrant auxetic honeycombs with different cell-wall angles and crushing velocities, showing that the crushing strength is proportional to the square of the crushing velocity. Moreover, Hu et al. [29] predicted the Poisson ' s ratio and plateau stress of re-entrant auxetic honeycombs via theoretical analysis and numerical simulation. In addition, a dynamic sensitivity index was proposed to assess the enhancement capacity of the plateau stress of the honeycombs caused by the crushing velocity. More recently, Qi et al. [30] deduced the effective Poisson ' s ratio and crushing strength of tetra-chiral honeycombs under quasi-static and dynamic crushing via theoretical analysis and numerical simulation.

Among the traditional honeycomb structures, the triangular honeycomb has a unique structural stability, which makes it excellent mechanical properties. Gu et al. [31] measured the tensile strength and Young ' s modulus of the triangular honeycomb and evaluated the accuracy of the current measurement methods used for mechanical properties. Liu et al. [32] systematically revealed the influences of the micro-structure and arrangements on the energy-absorbing capacity and deformation mechanism of triangular honeycombs. They found that triangular honeycombs have higher crushing strength and excellent energy-absorbing capacity compared to other honeycomb structures. To enhance the crushing strength of triangular honeycombs, hierarchical triangular honeycombs were constructed to explore the collapse modes and deformation mechanisms under in-plane uniaxial compression. A similar method involving hierarchical triangular honeycombs was employed by Sun[33], who found that hierarchical triangular honeycombs have enhanced crushing strength compared to traditional hexagonal honeycombs, especially under low-velocity crushing. Generally, the above-mentioned analyses indicated that the triangular structure can effectively improve the mechanical properties of honeycombs.

To enhance the in-plane stiffness and strength of general auxetic porous structures, many new methods have been conceived [34]. For example, sinusoidal, rhombic and vertical ligaments have been embedded into the classical auxetic structure to improve the stiffness, strength, and energy absorption capacity of the honeycombs [35 -38]. Wang et al.[39,40] proposed two types of novel auxetic structures by adding a re-entrant configuration and an arrow structure into the star-shaped structure and predicted the plateau stress and Young ' s modulus. They found that the crushing strength and energy absorption capacity were significantly improved, but the NPR effect was weakened. Thus, considerable work has been performed with the objective of designing a new auxetic structure with enhanced in-plane crushing strength, but the structures developed thus far have the drawback of a reduced NPR effect. To the knowledge of the authors, the further application of auxetic materials has been hindered because of the compromise between mechanical properties and auxetic performances [35]. Consequently, extensive research is necessary to study the dynamic crushing deformation behaviors of auxetic honeycombs further, to design a novel auxetic structure to achieve both excellent mechanical properties and a strong NPR effect simultaneously.

In this study, we propose a novel star-triangular auxetic structure by replacing the ligaments in the star-shaped structure to improve the energy absorption capacity. The deformation behaviors of the startriangular honeycomb (STH) and star honeycomb (SH) under different crushing velocities are studied using 1D shock theory and numerical simulation. Analytical formulas are deduced to predict the crushing strength of the STH under both low- and high-velocity loading, then verified by conducting numerical simulations. Moreover, numerical simulations are performed to reveal the effects of the geometric parameters and crushing velocity on the STH crashworthiness. Finally, the mechanical properties of the STH and SH were compared. This research is expected to improve understanding of the dynamic crushing behaviors and crushing strength of auxetic honeycombs and to provide design guidance for auxetic structures.
2. In-plane dynamic crushing behaviors
2.1. Star-triangular structure
2.1.1. Geometric description

The star-shaped auxetic structure is composed of four arrow structures, horizontal and vertical ribs, which are periodically arranged to form an SH. Fig. 1(a) shows a star-shaped periodic auxetic structure. A novel auxetic structure can be obtained by replacing the horizontal ribs of the star-shaped structure with a triangular-shaped structure, creating what is referred to as an STH, as shown in Fig. 1(b). The cell-wall lengths of the star-shaped and triangular-shaped structures are equal in the STH. Four geometric parameters are critical: l is the length of the inclined cell wall, θ is the angle between the cell wall and vertical direction, t is the thickness of the cell wall, and h is the length of the horizontal and vertical ribs. The mechanical properties of the auxetic honeycomb structure depend primarily on the relative density, and the weight of this parameter exceeds those of the other parameters. According to the geometric parameters of the auxetic structure, the relative densities of the SH and STH can be described as follows [41,42]:
Where ρ SH and ρ STH are the relative densities of SH and STH, respectively. ρ * SH and ρ * STH are the densities of SH and STH, respectively. ρ s is the density of the matrix material.
2.1.2. Finite element models

To reveal the dynamic deformation behavior and mechanical properties of the STH systematically, a finite element model was established using the explicit code of the nonlinear finite element software package LS-DYNA. In this model, the STH is located between the upper and lower rigid plates to simulate in-plane dynamic crushing. The rigid bottom plate is fixed, and the rigid upper plate is crushed in the y-direction with a constant crushing velocity [43,44]. Three crushing modes can be defined according to the crushing velocity: low-, medium-, and high-velocity modes. In this model, the length of the cell is l ¼ 5 mm, the angle is θ ¼ 30 � , the length of the horizontal and vertical ribs is h ¼ 4 mm, the out-of-plane thickness of the STH b is 0.5 mm, and the cell wall thickness t is determined based on the relative density. Fig. 1 depicts the in-plane dynamic crushing models of the SH and STH.

The matrix material of the auxetic honeycomb is aluminum alloy 6061O, and it is assumed that the cell wall is elastic-perfectly plastic with Young ' s modulus E ¼ 68 GPa , yield stress σ s ¼ 80 MPa , density ρ s ¼ 2700 kg = m 3 and Poisson ' s ratio γ ¼ 0 : 33 [45]. The out-of-plane (the z -direction) degrees of freedom of all of the nodes in the honeycomb were constrained to prevent the honeycomb from undergoing out-of-plane expansion. In addition, failure of the cell materials was ignored during the simulation. To prevent interpenetration of cell walls, automatic single-surface contact was applied to define the cell walls. Additionally, the contact between the honeycomb and two rigid plates was defined as automatic surface-to-surface contact. In this finite element model, both the static and dynamic coefficients of friction were taken to be 0.2. To improve the calculation efficiency while ensuring the accuracy of the numerical simulation, convergence tests were conducted

With decreasing element size, the convergence trends of the force-displacement curves are obvious. The results show that using 10 elements for each cell-wall of the STH can balance the computational efficiency and numerical convergence. Thus, four-point Belytschko-Tasy shell elements with sizes of 0.5 mm were adopted for the cell wall mesh.

It is found that both the numerical and the verification model results exhibit local deformation near the impact end.

2.2. Critical crushing velocity

The crushing velocity is an important parameter that affects the dynamic crushing deformation behaviors of honeycomb. Under varying crushing velocities, different stress waves are generated at the crushing end of the honeycomb. As the crushing velocity increases and the stress amplitude exceeds the yield stress of the honeycomb, which the deformation begins to change from the overall model to local model. In the critical stage, the crushing velocity that causes plastic deformation is known as the yield velocity or the first critical velocity. Based on the 'wave trapping ' theory, H € onig and Stronge [24] defined the analytical formula for the critical velocity under uniaxial loading, which can be obtained as:

Where ε cr is the initial strain of the honeycomb (the strain at which the stress reaches the initial peak), σ ' ð ε Þ ¼ d σ = d ε is the Young ' s modulus of the honeycomb in linear elastic phase, and ρ 0 is the density of the honeycomb; When the relative density ρ ¼ 0 : 15, the first critical crushing velocity of the SH and STH are 7 m/s and 7.5 m/s, respectively.

With the increase in crushing velocity, the local deformation of the honeycomb becomes even more pronounced. The honeycomb collapses from the crushing end in a progressive manner under the influence of the 'compaction wave ' , which is referred to as the 'steady-wave ' [46]. The critical velocity for the occurrence of the steady-wave in honeycomb is given by

Where σ 0 is the static yield stress of the honeycomb, ε d is the densification strain; When the relative density ρ ¼ 0 : 15, the second critical crushing velocity of the SH and STH are 35 m/s, 48 m/s, respectively.
2.3. Dynamic crushing deformation mode

In this section, the relative density ρ of the SH and STH is 0.15. In addition, according to the critical velocities of the two honeycombs, the following velocities corresponding to the three crushing modes were applied in the numerical simulation: 2 m/s, 20 m/s, and 100 m/s.
2.3.1. Low-velocity crushing

Similar to the conventional auxetic material, the STH and SH exhibit different deformation modes as the crushing velocity increases in the y - direction. For two honeycombs of the same relative density, Fig. 5 shows the in-plane crushing deformation processes of the SH and STH in the y -direction under a crushing velocity of 2 m/s. In the initial deformation phase (Fig. 5(b)), the deformation of the STH first appears at the crushing end, consisting mainly of rotation and bending of the cell wall of the star-shaped structure, and rapidly spreads to the entire honeycomb. The crushed and fixed ends of the honeycombs exhibit a V-shaped deformation band, and the V-shaped opening is oriented towards both ends. With increasing crushing displacement (Fig. 5(c)), the crushed end of the honeycomb still presents a V-shaped deformation band, but the deformation band at the fixed end becomes X-shaped. Every star cell deforms homogeneously with lateral shrinkage along the vertical direction. Thus, boundary necking forms during crushing. In addition, the V-shaped deformation band gradually expands from the crushed end to the fixed end (Fig. 5(d)). Eventually, the STH changes into another classic triangular grid structure (Fig. 5(e)). The triangular grids collapse in a layer-by-layer manner from the fixed end to the crushed end until the honeycomb becomes denser (Fig. 5(f) -(g)). The deformation of the SH first appears at the fixed end. Similarly to the deformation mode of the STH, the rotation and bending of the cell wall of the SH cause transverse shrinkage in the y -direction and exhibit a significant NPR effect (Fig. 5(a) -(d)). Additionally, the horizontal and vertical beams of the SH buckle under uniaxial compression and are expanded towards the x -direction (Fig. 5(e) -(h)), which is similar to the conventional honeycomb. Compared with the deformation process of the two honeycombs, the deformation of the STH is more uniform, and the problem of premature densification of the honeycomb can be effectively remedied.

Fig. 6 shows the stress-strain curves of the STH under a crushing velocity of 2 m/s. It is evident that the stress-strain curve consists of two stages with the same change pattern but different values. In the first stage, certain inclined cell walls of the star-shaped cell rotate and bend, plastic hinges form at both ends of the cell walls, and the stress oscillates around a constant value and forms a plateau in the stress-strain curve (Fig. 6). In the second stage, the geometric parameters of the triangular grid honeycombs are different from those of STH. The equivalent wall thickness of the inclined cell wall is 3 t , and that of the vertical cell wall is still t . As the compression continues, the inclined and vertical cell walls buckle, more plastic hinges appear at both ends of each cell wall and in the middle of the vertical cell walls. Therefore, the peak and plateau stresses are significantly increased, and the plateau stress is approximately 10 times larger than that in the first phase. After most of the cells are squeezed together, the stress-strain curves increase rapidly, which implies final densification of the honeycomb.
(relative density ρ ¼ 0 : 15).
2.3.2. High-velocity crushing

Under a crushing velocity of 100 m/s, the collapse of the STH starts from the crushed end and propagates in a layer-by-layer manner to the fixed end, and an I-shaped deformation band appears at the crushed end, as shown in Fig. 7. With increasing crushing velocity, the inertia effect becomes dominant, and the collapse of the honeycomb first appears at the crushed end. Therefore, the deformation process of the STH is similar to that of the SH. In addition, in the high-velocity model, the honeycombs do not have sufficient time to reach the phase of full shrinkage, so the SH and STH do not form boundary necking. Since the cell walls of the honeycomb collapse in a layer-by-layer manner through a periodic process, the stress of the STH fluctuates around a constant value until the cell walls are entirely packed together and the crushing stress increases rapidly. The high-velocity dynamic crushing stressstrain curves of the honeycombs are shown in Fig. 8.
2.3.3. Moderate-velocity crushing

Fig. 9 shows the deformation processes of the STH and SH under a crushing velocity of 20 m/s. With increasing crushing velocity, the cells near the crushed end do not have sufficient time for transverse contraction, as in the high-velocity crushing model (Fig. 9(a)). As the

Therefore, both the low- and high- velocity deformation modes emerged during medium-velocity crushing of the honeycombs, indicating the transition from the low-velocity mode to the high-velocity mode. In addition, the stress-stain curves of the honeycomb include the above two deformation modes in the medium-velocity crushing mode, as shown in Fig. 10. During the initial stage, due to the localized collapse at the crushed end, the stress of the honeycomb displays more significant oscillation. The deformation gradually enters a low-velocity collapse mode, and the stress gradually increases and oscillates around a constant value, which is approximately equal to the plateau stress under low-velocity crushing. The deformation mode of the SH is similar to that of the STH, but the crushing strength is significantly less than that of the STH.
2.4. Deformation modes map

As the crushing velocity increases, the deformation modes of the STH changes from the global deformation mode to the local deformation mode. The critical crushing velocities represent the critical transition boundaries between the critical deformation modes for the honeycomb, as described in Section 2.2. The deformation modes are affected by relative density variation. Through numerical simulations of STHs with different relative densities under crushing velocities, empirical formulas for the critical crushing velocities were deduced based on linear regression theory. When the crushing velocity v < vcr 1, the deformation of the honeycomb is described by the global deformation mode (Gmode). When the crushing velocity vcr 1 < v < vcr 2, both global and local
