4.4.2. Numerical study on the deformation mechanisms of gyroid lattice structures

The deformation mechanisms of the uniform, I2O-2 and O2I-2 gyroid lattice structures under compressive loading were analysed based on the number of elements in tension (NET), the number of elements in compression (NEC), and the total number of the elements both in tension and compression (NET + NEC). If the volume of an element increases from increment n to increment n + 1 , the considered element is under tensile loading in the n + 1 increment. The element with the volume change (i.e., Δ V ) of zero or near zero indicates a non-zero deviatoric state of stress or that the element volume is changing less than 0.01%. Therefore, such elements were not considered in the calculation. A full description of this classification can be found in [36]. The number of failed elements (NFET) for each increment was also determined.

The dominant deformation and failure mechanisms that occurred in some key stages during compression (as marked with numbers on the compressive stress -strain curves) were studied in terms of the JohnsonCook damage model. The Johnson-Cook damage model is defined by the strain for fracture, which is given by [35]:

where σ ∗ is the normalized flow stress and is defined as σ ∗ = σ m / σ (where σ m is hydrostatic pressure stress and σ is the von Mises equivalent stress). ˙ ε ∗ = ˙ ε / ˙ ε 0 represents the normalized plastic strain rate for ˙ ε 0 = 1.0 s 1 . T ∗ is the normalized temperature and is defined as T ∗ = ( T T 0 ) / ( Tm T 0 ) (here, T 0 is the reference temperature and T m is the reference melt temperature). D 1, D 2, D 3, D 4 and D 5 are materialdependent damage constants. These constants were obtained by fitting the experimental results and are listed in Table 4. The Johnson-Cook damage initiation criterion (JCCRT) as a colour contour plot, in which damage initiates when the value is equal to or higher than unity, is used to investigate the facture behaviour inside the lattice structures. In-situ imaging of the deformation processes at various strain levels, which was recorded by a video camera, is provided to compare with the FEA.

For the uniform lattice structure as shown in Fig. 13, when the lattice structure starts yielding (Point 1), (NET + NEC) is around 90%, which indicates that 90% of the elements are involved in deformation, either under tension or under compression (Fig. 13a). With further straining up to Point 2, both NEC and (NET + NEC) tend to decrease while NET starts increasing. Between Point 1 and Point 3, the compressive stress -strain curve shows a hardening behaviour. Since the contact between struts has not yet taken place, this hardening behaviour is unlikely attributed to the structural response to compression, but mostly like results from the strain hardening of CP Ti upon deformation. From Point 3 to Point 4, NEC decreases by almost 22% whereas NET increases by around 15%, resulting in a significant decrease of (NET + NEC). Besides, elements start to fail, and the number of failed elements increases sharply, as shown in Fig. 13b. The reduction in (NET + NEC) suggests that plastic strain localization takes place [36]. According to the JCCRT contour plot (Fig. 13c: 3 and 4 in the XZ-plane), the shear region at around 45 ◦ to the compression axis and the middle region exhibit higher JCCRT values and locally show the red colour, which confirms that strain localization occurs. In addition, in-situ images captured by the video camera also show the occurrence of shear bands (Fig. 13d: 4). This is consistent with other studies [21,58,59], which have reported that the occurrence of shear bands is responsible for the post-yielding softening or collapse behaviour.

The O2I-2 lattice structure exhibits a similar deformation behaviour to the uniform type (Fig. 14). When the lattice structure is strained from Point 3 to Point 4, (NET + NEC) decreases by about 10%, which is slightly higher than that of the uniform lattice structure. Similarly, the failure of element occurs in this strain range. From both JCCRT contour plots (Fig. 14c: 3 and 4 in the XZ-plane) and the in-situ imaging (Fig. 14d: 3 and 4), it can be observed that the shear and middle regions of the O2I-2 lattice structure becomes highly localized. As expected, the I2O-2 design is not capable of resisting the post-softening behaviour.

Unlike the uniform and O2I-2 lattice structures, the I2O-2 design does not show any post-yielding softening. It can be seen from Fig. 15a that after Point 3 NEC is decreasing while NET is increasing. This trend is similar to those in the uniform and O2I-2 lattice structures. However, (NET + NEC) remains almost constant and no failed element can be found when the strain level is less than 0.3 (Fig. 15b). This implies that the compressive loading is uniformly shared among all elements. The JCCRT contour plots confirm that a relatively homogeneous strain distribution across the I2O-2 lattice structure (Fig. 15c: 3 -6). Additionally, in-situ imaging (Fig. 15d: 3) shows that the lattice sample is less strain localized compared with the uniform and I2O-2 lattice samples.
J. Zhang et al.

Fig. 13. FEA of the uniform lattice structure under compressive loading: (a) the NET, NEC, and (NET + NEC) are plotted against the compressive stress-strain curve, (b) the number of failed elements in each increment is plotted against the compressive stress-strain curve, (c) the Johnson-Cook contour plot during compression at the corresponding number points as marked in (a) or (b), and (d) the in-situ images captured by a video camera at various deformation stages.

From FEA, it can be found that deformation and failure initiate from the inner region in the lattice structures, regardless of the architectures (Fig. 13c: 1 -4 in the XY-plane, Fig. 14c: 1 -4 in the XY-plane and Fig. 15c: 1 -4 in the XY-plane). In the I2O-2 design, the sheet thickness of the inner region is larger than those of the outer region. The higher relative density in the inner region enables the resistance of strain localization in the initial stage and the occurrence of shear band localization with further straining. Hence, this gives rise to the continuous hardening behaviour, rather than the post-yielding softening in the uniform and O2I-2 lattice structures.

It is noted that in this work the FEA was performed on the ideal lattice structures in view of simulation efficiency. Although the attached unmelted powders lead to an increase in the relative density (Fig. 5 and

Table 1), it is shown that such attached powders do not neither significantly contribute to the strength [60], or act as the critical failure locations during deformation [61]. Besides, the manufacturing defects in the lattice structures used for FEA are kept at relatively low values ( < 0.09%), as shown in Fig. 6a-c. Hence, the simulation results agree qualitatively well with the experimental results. Such a good agreement suggests that the modified VHM is capable of capturing the dominant characteristics that govern the deformation behaviour.

While the focus of this work is on improving the mechanical performance, the design and experimental work also directly endows the lattice structures with biological functions. Again, from the structural perspective, high strength and graded porosity of lattice structures are both required for long-term load-bearing bone implants. The I2O-2
J. Zhang et al.

Fig. 14. FEA of the O2I-2 lattice structure under compressive loading: (a) the NET, NEC, and (NET + NEC) are plotted against the compressive stress-strain curve, (b) the number of failed elements in each increment is plotted against the compressive stress-strain curve, (c) the Johnson-Cook contour plot during compression at the corresponding number points as marked in (a) or (b), and (d) the in-situ images captured by a video camera at various deformation stages.

design shows higher strength compared with the uniform type. Additionally, the inner-to-outer pore gradient has been shown to enhance bone ingrowth [62]. From the material perspective, both CP Ti and Ti 6Al 4V do not show the ideal material properties, either mechanical or biocompatible, for biomedical applications. CP Ti shows excellent biocompatibility without toxic effects but exhibits inferior strength as compared with titanium alloys. In contrast, Ti 6Al 4V yields higher strength and fatigue resistance. However, the presence of Al and V might cause long-term health problems [17]. In this work, MgO nanoparticles were introduced to CP Ti as the trace additive for enhancing the mechanical performance. It has been shown that MgO nanoparticles are biocompatible and even enable improving the integration of implanted biomaterials with bone [63,64]. Hence, the addition of MgO

nanoparticles, whether melted or not during AM, does not introduce any adverse cytotoxicity. Additionally, compared with the biocompatible β -stabilizing elements such as Mo, Nb, and Ta, MgO is more cost-effective, making it attractive as the additive material to develop biomedical titanium lattice structures.
5. Conclusions

Gyroid lattice structures with uniform and graded sheet thickness were designed and fabricated via L-PBF from CP Ti and MgO-doped CP Ti feedstocks. The morphologies, microstructures and mechanical properties were investigated by means of SEM, Micro-CT, EBSD and compressive tests, respectively. The deformation behaviour and failure
J. Zhang et al.

Fig. 15. FEA of the I2O-2 lattice structure under compressive loading: (a) the NET, NEC, and (NET + NEC) are plotted against the compressive stress-strain curve, (b) the number of failed elements in each increment is plotted against the compressive stress-strain curve, (c) the Johnson-Cook contour plot during compression at the corresponding number points as marked in (a) or (b), and (d) the in-situ images captured by a video camera at various deformation stages.
mode were studied by FEA which was based on the modified VHM. The following conclusions can be drawn from this work:

Micro-CT characterizations show a small volume of manufacturing defects in the as-built samples. Either tailoring the sheet thickness gradient or adding MgO to CP Ti does not considerably affect the manufacturing quality. Owing to the introduction of oxygen through MgO, the irregular shaped α phases of CP Ti lattice structures transform into acicular α ′ martensite in the MgO-doped types.

Both the uniform and O2I lattice structures exhibit the typical postyielding softening behaviour under compressive loading. The O2I designs result in negligible or even negative contribution to the strength enhancement compared to the uniform type. In contrast, the

I2O designs show higher strength and a continuous hardening behaviour, with complete elimination of the post-yielding softening. The continuous hardening behaviour is intrinsic to the I2O designs and the addition of MgO does not change the deformation behaviour.

The I2O-2 lattice structure made from MgO-doped feedstock shows superior SEA to the uniform CP Ti type and outperforms most of metallic and polymeric lattice structures, due to the combination of high strength and the continuous strain hardening behaviour achieved through architecture engineering and material design.

FEA results demonstrate that the I2O-2 lattice structures can resist the strain localization in the initial stage and the occurrence of shear band localization in the strain range, where post-yielding softening occurs in the uniform and O2I-2 lattice structures. FEA based on the
J. Zhang et al.
modified VHM could capture the features that govern the continuous hardening behaviour and shows good agreement with experimental results.

Comparative study on the process, anisotropy, and mechanical performance of laser powder bed fusion fabricated truss-lattice structures with different unit cell designs
Jiankai Yang, Weidong Li
School of Mechanical Engineering, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, Jungong Road 516, Shanghai 200093, China
A R T I C L E I N F O
Keywords: Lattice structure Additive manufacturing Laser powder bed fusion Bone implants Energy absorption
1. Introduction

After millions of years of natural selection and evolution, bone tissue has constructed complex porous structures with non-homogeneous anisotropic properties [1 -3]. The specific porosity of bone tissue not only provides mechanical strength, but also ensures the transportation of nutrients, creating a favorable biological environment for organs, muscles, and other body tissues [4]. According to statistics, nearly 15 million fractures occur every year, and bone is currently one of the most transplanted tissues [5]. Bone implants are used when bone tissue replacement is necessary to address biological problems [4]. In fact, an optimal bone implant should replicate the properties of natural bone at all levels, to have similar mechanical properties and nutrient diffusion [5]. For instance, the flow of nutrients will stop when the local bone tissue is not under an appropriate stress, which leads to the retraction of bone tissue. Therefore, mechanical stimulation is necessary to ensure the vitality of the bone tissue [4]. Metals and alloys, such as stainless steels, titanium (Ti) and its alloys, cobalt (Co) based alloys, and 316 L stainless steel, have been applied as bone implants for a long history owing to their good biocompatibility and corrosion resistance [6]. However, the stiffness of these bulk materials is much higher than that of bone. Using solid materials as bone implants will cause the stress shielding effect, which means that other bones around the implanted bone are not subjected to stress and lead to degradation. Moreover, the inability of

A B S T R A C T

Lattice structures to fabricate bone implants can avoid stress-shielding effects and promote bone-in-growth. However, the performance of bones varies in different body parts, creating a barrier to manufacture an appropriate lattice structure for bone implant. Here, the formability, anisotropy, energy absorption abilities, stress distribution, and deformation mode of the laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) processed face-centered cubic (FCC), Octet, and Kelvin lattice structures were systematically compared through experiments and finite element analysis. The results show that the Kelvin lattice structure had the optimal comprehensive mechanical performance. This research has potential value for the design and manufacturing of specific bone implants.
vascularization in bulk implants leads to a lack of nutrition in the surrounding bones.

Lattice structures have opened a door for fabricating a new class of bone implants that can simultaneously reduce stiffness and maintain bone health. Pores in lattice structures provide an integrated interconnecting structure that allows bone tissue to penetrate deeply into implants [7,8]. In recent years, various lattice structures have been created used for making bone implants [9 -12]. The focus of the most previous studies has been on the characterization analyses of individual lattice structures. Reed et al. [13] studied the effect of size and volume fractions on the stiffness and the collapse load of triple periodical minimal surfaces (TPMS) lattice structures. Experimental results confirmed the suitability of the TPMS to match the stiffness and strength of human bones. Zhang et al. [14] designed a functionally graded scaffold (FGS) to mimic the natural bone and found that the deformation behavior and failure mechanisms of FGS were strongly affected by porosity in the FGS. Liu et al. [9] fabricated a biomimetic porous ceramic scaffold based on the voronoi tessellation method. In this research, the relationship between pore parameters and the compressive strength of the scaffold was investigated. However, in the previous research, the performance comparison of lattice structures with different geometries has not been studied. To bridge the gaps, in this research, three typical lattice structures with different geometries will be selected to study their mechanical properties.

Due to the complexity of lattice structures, they are difficult to be formed using conventional methods. Thanks to the development of the additive manufacturing (AM) technology, such as laser powder bed fusion (LPBF), the fabrication of lattice structures has achieved high precision and controllable porosity [15 -17]. By limiting the metal materials as the bone implants, the 316 L stainless steel was selected as the basic material. As compared to Ti-based alloys and Co-based alloys, 316 L stainless steel has higher forming accuracy during the LPBF process to minimize the impact of forming defects on mechanical properties [18]. Furthermore, the very commonly investigated AM lattice structures are strut-based lattice structures, where nodes located at the vertex or edge of the units are connected by struts [19]. For strut-based lattice structures, such as the face-centered cubic (FCC) lattice structure [20], the Octet lattice structure [18,21], and the Kelvin lattice structure [22], have been vastly investigated. For example, Yu et al. [20] investigated the struts fracture and internal defects damage mechanisms of FCC lattice structure from the macro view (geometric configuration) to the micro view (pores, crack, and stair-stepping defects). It found that the edge vertical struts and diagonal struts of FCC lattice structure play an important role in energy absorption performance during compression. Tancogne-Dejean et al. [18] manufactured an octet truss lattice by LPBF. The research demonstrated that the specific energy absorption capability of the octet lattice structure was a monotonically increasing function of the relative density. Duan et al. [23] investigated the effect of relative density and loading rate on the mechanical properties and deformation mechanisms of the Kelvin foams. Experimental results indicated that the elastic modulus and plateau stress of Kelvin foams were proportional to the relative density, and deformation modes were affected by different relative densities. In the previously mentioned studies, the mechanical properties of FCC, Octet, and Kelvin lattice structures were investigated. However, the differences in mechanical properties and deformation modes among the above three structures at the same material usage have not been systematically studied.

In this research, FCC, Octet, and Kelvin lattice structures were fabricated using the LPBF technology. A comprehensive comparison was conducted on the formability, anisotropy, and mechanical properties of three types of lattice structures through scanning electron microscope (SEM), microcomputed X-ray tomography (micro-CT), compression experiments, and finite element analysis methods. Finally, the stress distribution and deformation mode of lattice structures were revealed. Due to the different mechanical performance requirements of bone implants in different parts of the body, the research in this work is expected to provide a reference for matching the performance of lattice structures with that of bone implants.
2. Materials and methods
2.1. LPBF process

The powder material in this work is plasma atomized 316 L stainless steel with the size range of 16 -45 µ m and the mean size of 30 µ m. The CAD models of lattice structures were established in NX UG software. Subsequently, the lattice structures were fabricated by using the inhouse made LPBF system equipped with a YLR-500 ytterbium fiber laser (IPG Laser GmbH, Germany). The preheating temperature of the substrate was 200 ℃ . The contour scanning parameters were set as: laser power of 500 W, and scanning speed of 3000 mm/s. In the LPBF process of lattice structures, the laser beam diameter of 70 µ m was kept constant for all scans. The offset distance is the gap between the hatch scan zone and the contour scan zone, and the offset distance in this work was set as 50 µ m. Based on previous research [24], the laser parameters were set as follows: laser power of 300 W, hatch spacing of 50 µ m, scanning speed of 2000 mm/s, and layer thickness of 30 µ m. The scanning strategy during the LPBF process was partition island (5 × 5 mm 2 ), and the rotation angle between two adjacent layers was 37 degrees.
2.2. Characterizations

The LPBF fabricated lattice structure samples were then used for formability characterization, including surface morphology, metallography, forming accuracy, and internal pores. The surface morphology of powder material and LPBF fabricated lattice structures were characterized using the field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) (TESCAN LYRA3, Czech Republic). Then, the samples were cut, ground, and polished according to the standard procedures and the optical microscope (OM) (PMG3, Olympus, Japan) was used for metallography characterization. The forming accuracy and internal pores of LPBF fabricated lattice structures were characterized by microcomputed X-ray tomography (micro-CT) (d5, Diondo, Germany) with a 90 kV voltage and a 5 µ m voxel size. VGStudio MAX software was used to render the 3D models and analyze the surface deviations and internal defects.
2.3. Mechanical tests

The compression tests of LPBF fabricated lattice structures were conducted by using the CM5205 testing machine (MTS System, China) according to the ISO 13314: 2011 standard [25]. The crosshead velocity during the compression was set as 2 mm/min, and each structure was tested three times for the reproducibility of the results. To capture the deformation process, a video camera (SONY, Japan) was applied. The relative density ( ρ ) is defined as the value of nominal density ( ρ ) divided by basic material density ( ρ s). In this work, all lattice structures have the same ρ , which means that all the lattice structures have the same amount of material, to ensure a fair comparison of mechanical properties. To evaluate the mechanical properties of lattice structures, the specific energy absorption ( W s) and the energy absorption efficiency ( W e) were applied. The W s denotes the energy absorbed per unit mass, the calculation equation is defined as:
where W denotes the energy absorption per unit volume, which is defined as:
where e 0 represents the upper limit of the compressive strain, which is 50 % according to the ISO 13314: 2011 standard. The calculation equation of W e is as follows:
where σ 0 is the stress when the strain is e 0.
2.4. Numerical simulation

The stress distribution and deformation modes of lattice structures were analyzed through Abaqus software. First, the CAD models of lattice structures were imported into Abaqus software, and the upper and lower surfaces of the lattice structures were endowed with unidirectional displacement and fixed constraints respectively. A general contact was applied for the surfaces between lattice structures and rigid bodies with tangential frictionless and normal ' hard ' contact. The elastic -plastic model of non-linear 316 L stainless steel material properties were given to the lattice structure models. Second, a 10-node modified quadratic tetrahedron element (C3D10M) was applied to mesh the models. To ensure high accuracy and computational efficiency, a mesh convergency analysis was conducted. Third, compression displacement was set as the height of the models. The historical data of force -displacement curves were recorded, and the stress distribution was obtained.

3. Results and discussions
3.1. LPBF fabricated lattice structures

The inside of cancellous or trabecular bone contains many pores for the growth of marrow and blood vessels (Fig. 1a, b) [1]. Three different lattice structures that can be applied for bone tissue engineering are considered in this work: FCC lattice structure (Fig. 1c), Octet lattice structure (Fig. 1d), and Kelvin lattice structure (Fig. 1e). The dimensions of all the three lattice structures are shown in Table 1. All lattice structures have the same relative density ρ (15 %) to study the effect of geometries on formability and mechanical properties.

Fig. 2. LPBF process and samples of lattice structures. (a) SEM morphology of 316 L stainless steel powder. (b) Interaction between laser and powder bed. (c) LPBF processed lattice structures with the substrate. (d) FCC lattice structure. (e) Octet lattice structure, (f) Kelvin lattice structure.
Fig. 3. SEM surface morphologies and OM images of lattice structures. (a-c) FCC lattice structure, (d-f) Octet lattice structure, (g-i) Kelvin lattice structure.

The SEM morphology of 316 L stainless steel powder used in the LPBF process is shown in Fig. 2a. Fig. 2b is the interaction between laser and powder bed during the LPBF process. Fig. 2c shows the as-fabricated lattice structures with the substrate. The images of the FCC lattice structure, Octet lattice structure, and Kelvin lattice structure fabricated by LPBF are shown in Fig. 2d-f, respectively. Three types of lattice structures have the same size, with the length, width, and height of 10 mm.
3.2. LPBF formability analysis

The SEM upper surface morphologies and OM images of three types of lattice structures are shown in Fig. 3. The surface morphology of the FCC lattice structure is shown in Fig. 3a, and Fig. 3b is the high magnification SEM image of Fig. 3a. It can be observed from Fig. 3b that the upper surface of FCC lattice structure is smooth, but there is a lot of powders bonding at the edge of the struts. The representative OM image in Fig. 3c shows the node and strut portions of the FCC lattice structure. Fig. 3d and e are the low and high-magnification SEM surface morphologies of the Octet lattice structure. The strut surface of the Octet lattice structure, with a lot of bonding powders. The Fig. 3f is the OM image of a node cross-section of the Octet lattice structure. It was found that the shape of the strut was irregular and there were many micropores inside the strut. Similarly, the surface of the Kelvin lattice structure also had many bonding powders (Fig. 3g and h). The OM image of a node of the Kelvin lattice structure is shown in Fig. 3i.

In the LPBF process, the contact between the edge of the molten pool and the powder bed causes the surrounding powder to partially melt and bond to the surface of the formed layer, thus forming the bonded powder [24,26]. The bonded powder increases the surface roughness and reduces the dimensional accuracy of LPBF-processed lattice structures. The upper surface of the three lattice structures fabricated by LPBF was smooth with only a small amount of bonded powder. This is because the upper surface of the molten pool is not in direct contact with the powder bed, and the small amount of bonded powder on the upper surface is formed by the gas expansion and thermal fluid dynamics during the interaction between the powder bed and laser [27]. The strut diameter of the Octet lattice structure was smaller than that of the FCC and Kelvin lattice structure, which made it easier for balling phenomena to occur during LPBF processing of the Octet lattice structure [28]. Thus, the OM image (Fig. 3f) showed that the strut edge of the Octet lattice structure was irregular.
Through OM analysis (Fig. 3c, f, i), we found that the formability of the Octet lattice structure was poor compared to FCC and Kelvin lattice

Fig. 4. Micro-CT dimensional analysis for the LPBF processed Octet lattice structure. (a) Surface deviation map. (b) Statistic of surface deviation distribution. (c-e) Top (c), middle (d), and bottom (e) cross-sectional slices of the 3D reconstructed model with theoretical boundaries (blue curves) of struts.

structures. Therefore, we selected the Octet lattice structure for further Micro-CT experiments to analyze the dimensional accuracy and internal defect distribution. If the dimensional accuracy and internal defects of the Octet lattice structure do not significantly affect the mechanical properties, it can also ensure the good formability of the FCC and Kelvin lattice structures. Furthermore, it can be proved that the differences in the mechanical properties of the three lattice structures are caused by different structural forms, not by differences in formability. Fig. 4a shows the surface deviation between the micro-CT reconstructed 3D model and the CAD design model, where red, green, and blue indicate that the dimensions of the LPBF processed sample are larger, equal, and smaller than the design values. The statistical surface deviation of internal defects is shown in Fig. 4b. The surface deviation curve exhibited a Gaussian distribution, with deviation values ranging from 0.2 mm to 0.4 mm, and the corresponding deviation value for the Gaussian distribution trough was 0.04 mm. Fig. 4c, d, and e are cross-sectional slices of the top, middle, and bottom parts of Fig. 4a, with blue lines representing the outline of the CAD design model.

The overall dimensional accuracy of LPBF processed Octet lattice structure was high, and the main reason affecting dimensional accuracy was dross defects (red part in Fig. 4a). Lattice structures have a large number of overhanging struts, which lack support during the LPBF process, resulting in direct contact between the melt track and the powder bed [29]. Due to the poor thermal conductivity of the powder bed, heat accumulation occurs. Under the effect of gravity, the metal liquid in the molten pool invades the powder bed and solidifies thus forming the dross defects [30].

The distribution of internal defects throughout the Octet lattice structure is shown in Fig. 5a, and the statistical histogram of internal defects is shown in Fig. 5b. The inset in Fig. 5b is the morphology of the internal pore. As shown in Table 2, the total defect volume and relative density of LPBF processed Octet lattice structure were 0.37 mm 3 and 99.89 %, respectively. Fig. 5c-d are the local distribution of internal defects in the top (Fig. 5c), center (Fig. 5d), and bottom (Fig. 5e) of the Octet lattice structure. It could be observed that the top and bottom parts of the Octet lattice structure had more pores, while the center part had fewer pores. This may be due to the differences in temperature gradients at different locations. Internal defects are mainly composed of micropores, which are formed by elements evaporation or the shielding gas pre-existing in the metal powder [31]. Through SEM, OM, and micro-CT analysis, it can be concluded that the LPBF processed lattice structure has good formability so that the influence of forming defects on the mechanical properties can be ignored.
3.3. Anisotropy of lattice structures

One important consideration in the design of lattice structures for bone tissue engineering is the anisotropic behavior. The mismatch of anisotropy between bone and implant may lead to stress shielding effect. Unlike solid metal materials, which are generally isotropic at the macro level, the anisotropy of lattice structures usually changes with the arrangement of the struts [32]. The anisotropic requirement for bone implants depends on the anatomical location of the application, such as trabecular bone requiring isotropy, while cortical bone requires strong anisotropy along the local loading direction [4]. With this in mind, we evaluated the effective stiffness matrix of three lattice structures using the numerical homogenization method [33]. According to Hook ' s law, σ ij = C ijkl ε kl, unknown elastic constants of the fourth-order elastic tensor C are reduced to 9 and the matrix form is as follows [34]:

Fig. 5. Micro-CT internal defects analysis of LPBF processed Octet lattice structure. (a) Distribution of internal defects throughout the whole structure, the internal defects are in color, and the 316 L stainless steel phase is in transparent gray. (b) Histogram of defect volume and the morphology of internal pore. (c-e) Local distribution of internal defects in the top (c), center (d), and bottom (e).
Table 2 Internal defect volume and relative density of LPBF processed Octet lattice structure.

In this approach, the strain is the specified displacement on the boundary, so that the corresponding stress can be determined based on the reaction force in the finite element analysis (FEA). For the involved lattice structures, a total of 9 FEA were carried out to determine the elastic matrix. The computed elastic matrix is then used to calculate Young ' s modulus of the lattice structures along an arbitrary direction. Plotting the three-dimensional (3D) spatial effective Young ' s modulus surface in an orthogonal coordinate system, so that the strong and weak directions can be demonstrated. The spherical surface indicates that the lattice structure is close to isotropic.

The unit cells and 3D spatial effective Young ' s modulus surfaces for the three different lattice structures are presented in Fig. 6. Results showed that the FCC and Octet lattice structures had higher anisotropy than the Kelvin lattice structure. The FCC lattice structure had much higher stiffness in axial directions ([001], [100], [010]) than in diagonal directions ([111]) (Fig. 6b), while the Octet lattice structure had much higher stiffness in [111] direction than [001], [100], and [010] directions (Fig. 6d). Kelvin lattice structure was close to isotropic (Fig. 6f). For trabecular bone implants requiring isotropy, the Kelvin lattice structure is suitable. For cortical bone implants requiring anisotropy, FCC and Octet lattice structures are more suitable.
3.4. Compressive properties

The compressive behaviors of the three types of lattice structures are shown in Fig. 7. Fig. 7a shows the compressive stress-strain curves of FCC, Octet, and Kelvin lattice structures, where lines are the average values of 3 independent experiments and the shaded area represents the deviations. The stress-strain curves of FCC and Kelvin lattice structures were similar in shape. First, the curve rose rapidly in the elastic stage, then the curve rose slowly, and finally, the curve rose rapidly again due to the densification stage. The difference between Octet and the other two lattice structures was that the stress-strain curve of the Octet lattice structure entered the plateau stage after the elastic stage, where the stress remained unchanged with the increase of strain. Before the densification stage, the FCC, Octet, and Kelvin lattice structures could withstand maximum stress of 250 MPa, 90 MPa, and 175 MPa, respectively. Thus, the FCC lattice structure had the optimal load-bearing performance.
To calculate Young ' s modulus of three lattice structures, the

Fig. 6. Unit cells of lattice structures and the corresponding 3D spatial effective Young ' s modulus surfaces. (a, b) FCC lattice structure, (c, d) Octet lattice structure, (e, f) Kelvin lattice structure.

enlarged stress-strain curves with a strain range of 0 -6 % are shown in Fig. 7b. As shown in Fig. 7b, the Young ' s modulus was obtained by calculating the slope of the stress-strain curves between the strain of 1.4 % and 2.5 %. The Young ' s modulus of FCC, Octet, and Kelvin lattice structures were 2.14 GPa, 2.04 GPa, and 2.58 GPa, respectively. The Young ' s modulus values of these lattice structures were in the range of 2.04 -2.58 GPa, which was similar to that of human trabecular bone (0.1 -4.5 GPa) [35,36]. Thus, the above three lattice structures can effectively avoid the stress shielding effect when used as bone implant structures. Moreover, considering the impact resistance requirements of bone implants, it is necessary to study the energy absorption performance of lattice structures [37]. Fig. 7c shows the specific energy absorption ( W s)-strain curves of three lattice structures, in which the strain range is 0 -50 %. The W s of all lattice structures increased monotonically with the increase of strain, among which the FCC lattice structure had the highest increase rate, followed by the Kelvin lattice structure and Octet lattice structure. When the strain reached 50 %, the W s of FCC, Octet, and Kelvin lattice structures were 31.9 J/g, 18.1 J/g, and 25.3 J/g, respectively. The W s denotes the energy absorption ability per unit mass. Thus, the FCC lattice structure has the optimal specific energy absorption ability. The variations of energy absorption efficiency ( W e) with the strain of three lattice structures are shown in Fig. 7d. In the initial stage of compression, the indenter just contacted the sample, and the reaction force fluctuated greatly, leading to the calculated W e curve fluctuated significantly. Thus we excluded the values in the initial stage and calculated W e from the strain of 2 %. With the increase of strain, the W e of all three lattice structures first increased to maximum values and then gradually decreased. Among them, the Octet lattice structure had the highest W e (89 %) at the strain of 28 %, while the FCC lattice structure had the smallest growth rate. At the strain of 50 %, the W e of FCC, Octet, and Kelvin lattice structure were 60 %, 74.2 %, and 62 %, respectively. Although the FCC lattice structure had high W s , its W e was low. Meanwhile, the Octet lattice structure had high W e, but low W s .
Based on the comprehensive performance of specific energy absorption and energy absorption efficiency, the Kelvin lattice structure had the optimal energy absorption performance.
3.5. Deformation behaviors

To reveal the deformation modes and stress distribution of three lattice structures, we took snapshots of experimental and simulated compression deformation processes at strains of 0, 30 %, and 60 %, as shown in Fig. 8. The FEA deformation processes of three lattice structures were consistent with the experimental results. As indicated by the white dashed lines in Fig. 8a, the FCC lattice structure exhibited interlayer deformation mode during compression. Each layer of unit cells experienced buckling deformation, and the joint locations between the upper and lower layers of unit cells were not easily deformed. This can also be confirmed by the stress distribution of FCC (Fig. 8b). When the strain was 30 %, the stress was concentrated in the middle of the unit cells, and the stress at the junction of the upper and lower layers of the unit cells was low. For the Octet lattice structure, the deformation mode was layer-by-layer failure (white dashed lines in Fig. 8c). During the compression process, the uppermost unit cells first buckled, and then gradually expanded to the lower layers. Fig. 8d shows the stress distribution of the Octet lattice structure during compression. The Kelvin lattice structure exhibited global collapse deformation mode in the experimental compression (Fig. 8e), which matched with the corresponding FEA results. It can be seen that the stress was concentrated in the center of the Kelvin lattice structure and the stress at the junction locations between unit cells was low (Fig. 8f).

The unit cell stress distributions of three lattice structures during the period of the simulated compression are shown in Fig. 9, and the strain range was 10 % 50 %. For the FCC lattice structure (Fig. 9a), the stress on the horizontal struts was low, while the stress on the diagonal and vertical struts was high. Among them, the vertical struts exhibit

(a) Stress-strain curves of lattice structures, the lines represent the average value of 3 repeat experiments and the shaded area represents the deviations. significant bending deformation when the strain exceeded 20 %. For the Octet lattice structure (Fig. 9b), the stress at the node between the side and internal diagonal struts was low, while the stress on the diagonal struts was high. In addition, the diagonal struts exhibited bending deformation during the compression process. For the Kelvin lattice structure (Fig. 9c), the stress concentration on the diagonal struts was obvious, while the stress on the horizontal struts was relatively low. As the compressive displacement increased, the struts of the unit cell of the Kelvin lattice structure did not show significant bending, and the deformation mode of the Kelvin lattice structure was overall deformation. Maxwell [38] divided porous structures into two categories, stretch-dominated and bending-dominated structures. The stiffness-to-weight ratio of stretch-dominated structures is high, while the specific energy absorption of bending-dominated structures is higher than that of stretch-dominated structures. The Maxwell equation is:

where m is the Maxwell index, s is the strut number, n is the node number. The lattice structures are stretch-dominated and bendingdominated structures when m > 0 and m < 0, respectively. After calculation, the FCC, Octet, and Kelvin lattice structures are all bendingdominated lattice structures.

To thoroughly analyze the deformation behavior and stress distribution on the surfaces of lattice structures, the struts on one side of the unit cell were extracted. Fig. 10 presents the schematic of stress distribution and deformation mode of the struts under the compressive strain of 10 %. As shown in Fig. 10a, the surfaces of the intersecting struts had four compressive stress regions and four tensile stress regions, while the surfaces of the vertical struts had one compressive stress region and one tensile stress region. Otherwise, the horizontal struts of the FCC lattice structure did not deform. The stress distribution and deformation mode of the two intersecting struts in the Octet lattice structure (Fig. 10b) were the same as those in the FCC lattice structure. For the Kelvin lattice structure, there were two compressive stress regions and two tensile stress regions on each of the four struts. 4. Conclusions

In this research, three types of lattice structures (FCC, Octet, and Kelvin) for bone tissue engineering were designed and fabricated by the LPBF process. The SEM and micro-CT were applied to analyze the

Fig. 10. Schematic of stress distribution and force analysis diagrams of the struts. (a) FCC lattice structure. (b) Octet lattice structure. (c) Kelvin lattice structure. (d) Vertical strut. (e) Diagonal strut.

formability. Then, the anisotropy of lattice structures was investigated. Mechanical properties, including Young ' s modulus, specific energy absorption, and energy absorption efficiency were compared among three types of lattice structures. The finite element simulations were carried out to reveal the stress distribution and deformation mode of lattice structures. In sum, this work systematically studied the forming characteristics, mechanical properties, and deformation mechanisms of FCC, Octet, and Kelvin lattice structures, providing an important basis for the selection of lattice structures for bone implants. The main conclusions are as follows:

The bonded powder increases the surface roughness and reduces the dimensional accuracy of LPBF-processed lattice structures. The strut diameter of the Octet lattice structure was smaller and easier to produce balling phenomena.

The surface deviation between a micro-CT reconstructed 3D model and its CAD design model ranged from 0.2 mm to 0.4 mm, and the corresponding deviation value for the Gaussian distribution trough was 0.04 mm. The total defect volume and relative density of LPBF processed the Octet lattice structure were 0.37 mm 3 and 99.89 %, respectively.

The FCC and Octet lattice structures had higher anisotropy than the Kelvin lattice structure. The Kelvin lattice structure is more suitable for trabecular bone implants requiring isotropy, while FCC and Octet lattice structures are more suitable for bone implants requiring anisotropy.

The Young ' s modulus of FCC, Octet, and Kelvin lattice structures were 2.14 GPa, 2.04 GPa, and 2.58 GPa, respectively. The FCC lattice structure had the optimal specific energy absorption ( W s) ability (31.9 J/g), and the Octet lattice structure had the high energy absorption efficiency ( W e) (74.2 %). Based on the
comprehensive performance of W s and W e, the Kelvin lattice structure had the optimal energy absorption performance.

Keywords: Energy absorption Finite element analysis Crashworthiness optimization
Triply periodic minimal surface (TPMS)
1. Introduction

The cellular structure is a class of lightweight structures constructed by random or periodic microstructures [1]. According to the dimension of the microstructures, cellular structures can be divided into two types [2], one is composed of two-dimensional cells which have a common surface such as honeycombs, and the other is made up of three-dimensional microstructures, like aluminum foams, strut-based lattice structures and so on. Due to the excellent mechanical properties, cellular structures were used for energy conversion (e.g., energy absorption, heat exchange, sound insulation) [3 -8], tissue engineering scaffolds [9 -11], structural components [12], etc. in aerospace, machinery, medicine, and other fields.

The studies on mechanical properties of cellular structures have attracted a lot of attentions in recent years. For example, Zhang et al. [13,14] investigated the crashworthiness performance of cellular
A B S T R A C T

Sheet-based 3D periodic cellular structures attract great attentions due to their lightweight and excellent mechanical properties. Unlike other traditional honeycombs and lattice structures, sheet-based cellular structures consist of triply periodic minimal surface (TPMS) cores, which are continuous through space with a porous cavity surrounded by continuous surfaces. In this study, the crashworthiness of four types of TPMS sheet structures (i.e., Primitive, FRD, IWP, and Gyroid) under axial loading was investigated. According to the results obtained by the nonlinear finite element analysis, the level-constant in the implicit form of TPMS and the shell thickness of TPMS sheet structures were found to affect the crashworthiness significantly. To achieve an optimal design, a metamodel-based multi-objective optimization method was developed to optimize the four types of TPMS sheet structures. Three different metamodels, i.e., Kriging (KRG), polynomial response surface (PRS) and radial basis function (RBF), were compared to identify the most accurate model, which was then utilized for the optimization. Followed by the multiobjective optimization, four Pareto fronts of these TPMS sheet structures were plotted and compared, of which the FRD-sheet structure was found to have the best energy absorption capacity. Moreover, the crashworthiness of the TPMS sheet structures was compared with that of the other materials or structures in nature and engineering, and an Ashby plot was given. Overall, TPMS sheet structures possess excellent specific energy absorption and specific strength and show great potential for engineering applications.

structures under the axial load by experimental method combined with theoretical analysis and FE simulations. The results offered a new route for designing novel structures with high energy absorption capacity. Sun et al. [15,16] and Zhang et al. [17] investigated the axial crushing behaviors in self-similar regular hierarchical honeycombs using an explicit finite element code. The results showed that the hierarchy could improve the energy absorption capability of honeycombs compared to that of traditional honeycombs. Besides, Yin et al. [18] and Tao et al. [19] also investigated the in-plane crashworthiness of hierarchical honeycombs. The focus of the above research was 2D cellular structures. Recent advances in additive manufacturing (AM) make it possible to manufacture cellular structures with complex geometries. This further promotes the development of research work on the design of the cellular structure [20,21]. For example, Duan et al. [22] investigated the mechanical properties and deformation mechanisms of 3D printed Kelvin foams under quasi-static and dynamic loadings. Tancogne-Dejean et al.

H. Yin et al.

[23] designed a new type of octet truss lattice material, which had exceptionally high specific energy absorption, constant plateau stress, and zero plastic Poisson ' s ratio. Many energy-absorbing mechanical metamaterials [24 -27] were designed and fabricated using AM technology. Based on the above research [13 -19,22 -27], it can be found that the architecture of the unit cell has a significant influence on the overall mechanical performance of the cellular structure. In other words, different mechanical properties can be achieved by modifying and optimizing the unit-cell geometries of the cellular structure. Thus, lots of research focused on the effect of the unit-cell topology on enhancing the overall mechanical performance, had been carried out [28 -32].

As a new type of three-dimensional lattice structures inspired by nature [33], triply periodic minimal surface (TPMS) structure has recently given extensive attention in the field of material science and tissue engineering for its novel, complex and symmetrical topology structure [11,34 -36]. TPMS is mathematically defined as a continuous and non-intersecting surface through space with mean curvature of zero and replicated in three perpendicular directions infinitely [37,38]. Also, it represents a surface with the minimum surface area in space for a given boundary [39]. TPMS divides the space into two intertwined labyrinths on both sides [40]. As multifunctional applications have been found by using TPMS structures in different disciplines, the excellent properties of TPMS structures are attracting researchers to study it more deeply. Currently, there are two different groups of TPMS structures being investigated. Namely, two approaches were found to turn TPMS into structures with specific volumes [41,42]. The first method of forming TPMS structures is to solidify one of the volumes separated by the minimal surfaces, and this group of TPMS structures is referred to as skeletal-based TPMS structures. The second is to thicken both sides of the minimal surfaces, by which the sheet-based TPMS structures can be obtained. In this study, the second method is utilized to evolve TPMS into sheet-based structures.

Several researchers have investigated the mechanical properties of TPMS sheet structures. For instance, Al-Ketan et al. [43 -45] investigated the mechanical properties of four types of TPMS sheet structures fabricated via selective laser melting (SLM) through compressive experiments. The results showed that the deformation behavior of TPMS sheet structures was nearly stretching-dominated and sheet-TPMS based structures had a better mechanical performance comparable to the skeletal-TPMS based and strut-based cellular structures. Also, it was found that the mechanical properties can be varied by changing the volume fraction of core, shell thickness and internal architecture of the cellular structure [44,45]. Many investigations about the elastic properties (e.g., uniaxial, shear and bulk moduli and Poisson ' s ratio) of the TPMS sheet structure were made by finite element (FE) analysis [46 -48]. Zhang et al. [49] investigated the energy absorption ability of Primitive, Diamond, and Gyroid sheet structures by experimental method combined with the FE simulations. The results revealed that three types of TPMS structures exhibited a superior energy absorption ability compared to BCC lattices. Through the experimental and numerical stress-strain curves, it was found that the D- and G-type structures had uniform stress in the stress plateau region. However, stress fluctuations were observed for P-type in the stress plateau region, which is bad for energy absorption. According to the study of Maskery et al. [50], the stress fluctuation was caused by the brittle fracture mechanisms of the materials, which can be improved by increasing the ductility of the materials.

Previous works [43 -50] showed that the energy absorption ability of sheet-based TPMS structures was better than that of skeletal-based and strut-based cellular structure. Despite there are several studies on TPMS sheet structures, it is still a challenge to obtain the optimal design of unit-cell geometry for TPMS sheet-based cellular structures. The above studies considered only one single factor among the effect parameters such as volume fraction of core, shell thickness or internal architecture for the TPMS sheet structures. Few designers look into the effect of two or more factors on the crashworthiness of these structures.

In this study, four types of TPMS sheet structures (Primitive, FRD, IWP, and Gyroid) under axial loading conditions were investigated by employing the nonlinear finite element code LS-DYNA. The effects of two design parameters (the level-constant of TPMS and the shell thickness) on the crashworthiness and the deformation modes of TPMS sheet structures were investigated. To obtain the optimal design of the sheet structures, a multi-objective optimization method was used combining with the multiple alternative metamodels and the NSGA-II algorithm.
2. Geometry description of sheet-based 3D periodic cellular structures
2.1. Triply periodic minimal surface (TPMS)

TPMS is a surface whose mean curvature vanishes at every point on the surface and is periodic in three independent directions. The geometrical configuration of TPMS can be described as a continuous and non-intersecting surface with two sides that divide the space into the porous region and non-porous region. 2.2. Structural configuration

In this study, the unit cell of TPMS sheet structures is a cubic with a length of 4 mm. Due to the boundary effect of the structure, it should be determined how many numbers of unit cells of the sheet structures are enough to investigate the mechanical property. Thus, lattice configurations from 4 � 4 � 4 to 7 � 7 � 7 tessellating cells were investigated. Each TPMS structure had a cell size of 4 mm, a shell thickness of 0.30 mm and a level-constant value of zero. The numerical results were shown in Fig. 3. From Fig. 3, it can be found that the effect of the number of unit sizes on the crushing stress of TPMS structures was relatively little. Especially for FRD-sheet and G-sheet structures, the nominal stress-strain curves with different numbers of unit cells were almost coincident. Therefore, to reduce the computational expense, a 4 � 4 � 4 cell arrangement is considered enough to investigate the mechanical properties. Fig. 4 illustrates the loading condition and the crosssection of the TPMS sheet structure (here take the P-sheet structure as an example). One end of the sheet structure is fixed to a rigid wall, and the other end is compressed under a quasi-static load. The side length of the investigated model is 16 mm. The level-constant C and the shell thickness t are chosen to study the effects on the crashworthiness of the TPMS sheet structures.

4. Crashworthiness simulation of sheet-based 3D periodic cellular structures
4.1. Finite element (FE) modeling method

Because of the complex configuration of the TPMS sheet structure, it was difficult to establish its geometry model by the general CAD software. In this study, the geometries of the TPMS were first created using the mathematical software K3DSurf [30] together with the reverse design software Geomagic. Then, these geometries were transferred to the FE pre-processing software HyperMesh to construct the finite element model of TPMS sheet structures, which would be solved in the explicit nonlinear FE software LS-DYNA.

The finished FE models of the four TPMS sheet structures were shown in Fig. 5. The base material of TPMS sheet structures was 316 L stainless steel, which would be used in the following crashworthiness evaluation and optimization design. For the quasi-static loading process, the effect of the strain rate was not considered here. The detailed material parameters were density ρ ¼ 7930 kg/m 3 , Young ' s modulus E ¼ 190 GPa, Poisson ' s ratio μ ¼ 0.3 and yield strength σ y ¼ 529 MPa [49]. The pairs of effective plastic stress versus plastic strain of 316 L stainless steel were listed in Table 1. In our work, material failure of TPMS sheet structures made of 316 L stainless steel was not considered [49].

Belytschko-Lin-Tsay thin shell elements were used to discretize the TPMS sheet structures. The elastic-plastic constructive model was employed to express the mechanical property of the base material. In LSDYNA, the piecewise linear plasticity material model (MAT_24) which is a representative elastic-plastic model was used. To simulate the contacts between two rigid plates and TPMS sheet structures, two ' automatic node to surface ' contacts were defined with the static and dynamic friction coefficient of 0.3 and 0.2, respectively. In addition, the

Since the total CPU time for an explicit solution was too long if the quasi-static loading velocity (2 mm/min) was set in LS-DYNA, a constant faster speed of 1 m/s was adopted in the simulation [51].

To investigate the effect of mesh size on the accuracy of the numerical results, a convergence test with six different mesh sizes was carried out. The load versus displacement curves for these different element sizes are shown in Fig. 6. From Fig. 6, it can be seen that the error between element sizes 0.15 � 0.15 mm and 0.20 � 0.20 mm was small. Simulation with even smaller element size would not significantly improve accuracy. However, the computational cost would be highly increased if we use a smaller element size in the simulation. Thus, the element size with 0.15 � 0.15 mm was adopted in the FE modeling for all TPMS sheet structures.
4.2. Validation of the FE model

To validate the accuracy of FE models built via the method described in Section 4.1, the modeling results of four types of TPMS sheet structures under quasi-static load were compared with the experimental data. The TPMS sheet structures used in the experiment consisted of 64 (4 � 4 � 4) unit cells in a cube with a side length of 60 mm and the shell thickness was 1 mm.
Two samples of each type of TPMS sheet structure along with two tensile samples were fabricated using an EOS M290 machine. The machine was equipped with a build platform of
H. Yin et al.
Fig. 6. Load versus displacement curves for TPMS sheet structures with different element sizes.

250 mm � 250 mm � 325 mm, a 400 W Yb-fiber laser and a laser beam spot size of 100 μ m. All samples were printed using EOS stainless steel CX powder, where the chemical composition of CX powder is given in Table 2. The processing parameters were set as follows: laser power 285 W, scanning speed 1500 mm/s, layer thickness 30 μ m and hatch spacing 100 μ m. Strip scanning strategy was used to fabricate these samples. One of the final P-sheet structures is shown in Fig. 7(b). Fig. 7 (b) revealed that no macroscopic cracks or pores could be found from the surface of the component. The material property of EOS CX stainless steel was acquired by the standard tensile experiment with SLMprocessed tensile samples, which was constructed in accordance with the ASTM E8/E8M standard. The true stress-strain curve of the CX stainless steel sample was illustrated in Fig. 7(a). From this curve, the mechanical properties can be obtained, which are summarized in Table 3. The quasi-static compress test was conducted using an Instron universal test machine with the speed of the upper indenter fixed at 2 mm/min.

The comparison of the FE simulations with the experimental data can be seen in Fig. 8. One can notice from the load-displacement curves that the simulation results were generally in good agreement with experimental results in both plateau regions and densification regions. According to Fig. 9, it can be seen that the deformation patterns of the numerically simulated models also correlated well with those of the experiment. For P-sheet structures, the cells on the diagonal of the tested structure firstly deformed and collapsed after yielding, forming double shear bands on each face. The corresponding numerical models also showed double shear bands on the diagonal layers. The failure of the equivalent numerical model was the same as the tested structure. Besides, the crack failure of the EOS CX stainless steel was not observed in the compressed test. Therefore, the results show the capability of the current FE modeling method in simulating the crushing behavior of TPMS structures, which can be further employed to analyze the crashworthiness of TPMS sheet structures.
4.3. Crashworthiness evaluation of TPMS sheet structures
4.3.1. Effect of the design parameters

In this section, the effects of the two design parameters, i.e. level constant C and shell thickness t were considered in the design of TPMS sheet structures (Primitive, FRD, IWP and Gyroid). Five level constant C were chosen as 0.8, 0.4, 0.0, 0.4 and 0.8, and five shell thicknesses t

were set as 0.15 mm, 0.30 mm, 0.45 mm, 0.60 mm and 0.75 mm. Figs. 10 -13 show the effects of these parameters on PCF, SEA, CLE, and MCF, respectively.

Fig. 10 shows the effect of the two design variables (i.e. shell thickness t and the level constant C ) on PCF. According to Fig. 10, it can be seen that the PCF of four sheet structures increased with the increase of t and the FRD-sheet structures had the highest PCF value in each case. The PCF of G-sheet structures decreased with the increase of C and the PCF of FRD-sheet structures increased first and then decreased with the increase of C . For P-sheet structures, the PCF first increased and then decreased in the cases of t ¼ 0.15 mm, 0.30 mm and 0.45 mm and it generally decreased in the case of t ¼ 0.60 mm and 0.75 mm when C increased. For IWP-sheet structures, with the increase of C , the PCFs first increased and then decreased when t ¼ 0.15 mm and 0.30 mm and PCFs decreased when t ¼ 0.45 mm, 0.60 mm and 0.75 mm. It can be found that the PCF of P- and IWP-sheet structures show different variation tendencies in different thickness ranges. This is because that the PCF existed at different positions of the load-displacement curves. The PCFs

While, for t ¼ 0.6 mm -0.75 mm, the PCFs existed at the point of the crushing force where the compressed displacement was the largest. For IWP-sheet structures, the initial peak force was chosen as the PCF when t ¼ 0.15 mm and 0.30 mm. In addition, it can be found that C had little effect on PCF when t � 0.30 mm. As shown in Fig. 10, PCF values for the four TPMS sheet structures all varied in a small range for the cases of t ¼ 0.15 mm and 0.30 mm.
The SEAs of four TPMS sheet structures with different C and t were plotted in Fig. 11. From Fig. 11, it was found that the SEAs of four TPMS

sheet structures increased when t increased and the FRD-sheet structures had the highest SEA value in most cases. It was also found that the SEAs of P- and G-sheet structures generally decreased and the SEA of FRDsheet structures first increased and then decreased when C increased. For IWP-sheet structures, the SEA generally decreased in the case of shell thickness t ¼ 0.15 mm and 0.30 mm, while it first increased and then decreased in the cases of shell thickness t ¼ 0.45 mm, 0.60 mm and 0.75 mm when C increased. Besides, From Fig. 11, it can be noticed that the SEA of IWP-sheet structures was not very sensitive to the change of C . And, the variable range of SEA of IWP-sheet structures with different t

was narrow compared to those of the P-, FRD- and G-sheet structures.

MCF can also be used to represent the energy absorption capacity of structures. In some studies, the researchers suggested high levels for MCF to increase the energy absorption capacity. It was clear that FRDsheet structures had the highest MCF value among four TPMS sheet structures with the same shell thickness t . This indicated that FRD-sheet structures had the highest energy absorption (EA), according to Equation (6).

The effects of two design variables on CLE were given in Fig. 13. From Fig. 13, it can be found that the CLE changed irregularly with the increase of C and t . For P-, FRD-, IWP- and G-sheet structures, the peak force point could be obtained at different positions of load-displacement curves. In some cases, the peak crushing force was obtained at the initial force peak of the curve. In other cases, the PCF was obtained at the position where the compressed displacement was the largest on the curve. Therefore, according to Equation (5), the change of CLE was irregular.

If the shell thickness t increases, the strength of the components (i.e. the support parts) of the unit cell of the TPMS sheet structure would increase. Then, the structural strength of the whole TPMS sheet structures would definitely improve with the increase of t . Thus, the PCF increased with the increase of t as shown in Fig. 10. Similarly, the SEA and MCF also increased with the increase of t due to the same reason as shown in Figs. 11 and 12.

The effect of the level constant C on the structural strength of TPMS sheet structures was more complicated than that of the shell thickness t . The influence of C on the crashworthiness indicators of TMPS sheet structure was not always monotonous. Fig. 14 illustrates the angles between the faces of the unit cells and the direction of compression loading. The blue regions in the unit cell are the faces parallel to the loading direction, which means that these faces are the main supporting parts for the unit cell of each TMPS sheet structure. Fig. 15 shows the initial Mises stress distributed on the unit cell of TPMS sheet structures under the axial compression loading. Based on Figs. 14 and 15, it can be found that high stress was mostly distributed on the supporting parts of the TPMS sheet structures. Therefore, it can be concluded that the strength of TPMS sheet structures was related to these supporting parts in the unit cell. As shown in Figs. 1 and 2, the supporting parts of a unit

This was the essential reason why level constant C could affect the crashworthiness of the TPMS sheet structure. However, the change of the supporting parts of a unit cell for each TPMS structure was complex when C increased. Thus, the influence of C on the crashworthiness indicators of TMPS sheet structure was not always monotonous, which was different from that of the shell thickness t .
4.3.2. Deformation modes

The deformation modes of four TPMS sheet structures (Here selected the case of C ¼ 0.8, 0.4, 0, 0.4, 0.8 and t ¼ 0.30 mm because it is a representative one) at different nominal compressive strains are given in Figs. 16 -19, respectively. And, the nominal stress-strain curves are given in Fig. 20. It was found that the deformation modes of P-sheet and IWPsheet structures were quite different from those of FRD-sheet and Gsheet structures. The deformation modes of FRD-sheet and G-sheet
H. Yin et al.
Fig. 15. Mises stress distribution for TPMS sheet structures under the axial compression loading ( C ¼ 0 and t ¼ 0.3 mm): (a) Primitive, (b) FRD, (c) IWP and (d) Gyroid.

structures looked similar. Specifically, it can be seen from Fig. 16 that the cells on the diagonal first collapsed and then the shear bands developed in the middle two layers of P-sheet structures. The entire deformation pattern looked like a transverse x . As shown in Fig. 18, the deformations of IWP-sheet structures were a little different from that of P-sheet structures. In this case, the collapse first occurred in the intermediate layer and the shear bands were found to be horizontal. The configuration of each layer of the IWP-sheet structures was similar to an induce indentation which triggered a stable collapse mode in each layer. With the increase of impact displacement, IWP-sheet structures were crushed layer by layer until they were compacted. From Fig. 20(a) and (c), it can be found that the stress fluctuated in the plateau region of the nominal stress-strain curves of P-sheet and IWP-sheet structures due to the localized buckling of the unit cells.

The collapse mode of FRD-sheet and G-sheet structures was continuous. And, the two structures both collapsed layer by layer successively. The collapse mode of each layer was the same as that of its adjacent layers. Based on Fig. 17, it can be found that two small shear bands of the FRD-sheet structures firstly appeared at both the impact side and the support side. From Fig. 19, it can be seen that the collapse of the G-sheet structures initiated in the layer which is close to the center section of sheet structures. With the increase of compression distance, the layers together with adjacent layers collapsed gradually with the similar strains in the FRD-sheet and G-sheet structures. This brought about relatively smooth hardening behaviors without stress fluctuations in the plateau region, which can be seen in Fig. 20(b) and (d).

Based on the FE analysis results of four TPMS sheet structures, it was observed that the deformation modes of the TPMS structure with the same unit-cell topology configuration were generally consistent. This indicated that the deformation modes mainly depended on the unit-cell topology and were insensitive to the two design parameters of C and t .
4.3.3. Dynamic impact behavior

In the dynamic simulation, the Cowper-Symonds equation was used to describe the strain rate sensitivity. According to Ref. [52], Cowper-Symonds coefficients can be set to D ¼ 240 s 1 and q ¼ 4.74, respectively. In the study, four kinds of initial impact velocities were considered, which were 5 m/s, 15 m/s, 25 m/s, and 50 m/s. The shell thickness t and level-constant value C were set as 0.30 mm and 0, respectively. The crashworthiness criteria were summarized in Table 4. These crashworthiness indicators of SEA, PCF, MCF, and CLE were determined at the compressed displacement of 9.6 mm.

As shown in Table 4, it can be found that the SEA, MCF and PCF of the dynamic loading cases were higher than those of the quasi-static case. Besides, the value of SEA, MCF and PCF increased with the increase of impact velocity. This was due to the strain rate of the base material of stainless steel together with the mass inertia of structure under the dynamic impact. The simulation results indicated that the crashworthiness of TPMS sheet structures under dynamic impact was better than that of the quasi-static case. This was the same for the traditional porous structure such as metal honeycomb and metal foam. However, the CLE of dynamic impact was not always better than that of the quasi-static compress for each kind of TPMS sheet structure, because both the MCF and PCF increased.

Based on the discussion above, two design parameters, i.e. levelconstant C and shell thickness t , were found to have a significant effect on the crashworthiness of TPMS sheet structures. The level-constant C and shell thickness t had a strong effect on SEA, MCF and PCF. However, it was still not clear which type of the TPMS sheet structure had the best crashworthiness among the considered cases. In order to find the optimal design of TPMS sheet structures, four types of TPMS sheet structures were optimized in the next section. In addition, in order to make clear which type of the TPMS sheet structure has the best crashworthiness, the optimal design of the four structures was compared.
5. Optimization design of the sheet-based 3D periodic cellular structure
5.1. Optimization methodology
5.1.1. Definition of the optimization problem

The TPMS sheet structure was expected to absorb a large amount of impact energy per unit mass when it was used as an absorber, for which the SEA was chosen as an objective function to be maximized in the crashworthiness optimization problem. On the other side, the PCF is another important safety indicator for cushioned objects needed to be controlled under a certain level. Therefore, it was chosen as another objective function to be minimized in the optimization problem. Thus, the multi-objective optimization (MOO) problem of TPMS sheet structures was formulated as
where C is the level constant, t is the thickness of the TPMS sheet structures. Both the PCF and SEA are the functions of C and t .
5.1.2. The procedure of the optimization design

In order to solve the MOO problem of Equation (7), a multi-objective optimization method using the metamodels and the non-dominated sorting genetic algorithm II was developed. Firstly, the design points used for establishing the metamodels were obtained according to the design of experiment (DOE). Secondly, the FE models of the TPMS sheet structures corresponding to these design points were established and then the simulations were carried out in LS-DYNA to get the objective responses. Three different kinds of metamodels were constructed and compared to choose the most accurate one for the SEA and PCF of four TPMS sheet structures. Finally, the NSGA-II algorithm was used to obtain the Pareto optimal solutions by employing the metamodels.
5.1.3. Design of experiment (DOE)

To generate reasonable design sampling points, some typical DOE methods including full factorial design, Latin hypercube, central composite design, and Taguchi orthogonal array can be used. Due to the advantage of its uniformity, the full factorial design method was used in
H. Yin et al.
Fig. 16. FE predicted deformation modes of P-sheet structures with. C ¼ 0.8, 0.4, 0, 0.4, 0.8 and t ¼ 0.30 mm.

this study. 25 sampling points were generated by the full factorial design method for the four types of TPMS sheet structures. In addition, to evaluate the accuracy of the metamodel, 9 test sampling points were generated by the method of Latin hypercube design method because it can generate the sampling points evenly in the design space. The design points and test points are shown in Fig. 21.
5.1.4. Metamodel and error metrics

To reduce the computational cost and time in the process of optimization design which usually needs expensive simulation, metamodels are widely used to calculate the values of the objective function. The metamodels used in optimization design include Kriging (KRG), polynomial response surface (PRS), support vector regression (SVR), radial basis function (RBF) and et al. [53]. However, it is hard to say which metamodel is the most suitable for a particular problem. Each metamodeling method has its pros and cons. Therefore, three different metamodels, KRG, PRS and RBF, are all established and the most accurate one was used for the crashworthiness optimization design in this study. The core functions of these metamodels are listed in Table 5.
The accuracies of the metamodel can be evaluated by the square

5.2. Optimization results
5.2.1. Accuracy evaluation of the metamodels

In this study, the KRG, PRS and RBF metamodels of SEA and PCF of four types of TPMS sheet structures were built based on the 25 design sampling points, and their corresponding responses were obtained by the numerical simulation through LS-DYNA.

Using the 9 test points, the accuracy of the three metamodels was evaluated. The accuracy estimators of three metamodels were listed in Table 6. From Table 6, it can be found that the RBF metamodels are the most accurate ones for the P-sheet and FRD-sheet structures, and the PRS metamodels are the most accurate models of the IWP-sheet and G-sheet structures. Thus, the RBF was selected to model the P-sheet and FRDsheet structures, and the PRS was used to model the IWP-sheet and Gsheet structures in the following optimization design.
5.2.2. Multi-objective optimization results
In this work, the multi-objective optimization for four TPMS sheet
H. Yin et al.
Fig. 19. FE predicted deformation modes of G-sheet structures with C ¼ 0.8, 0.4, 0, 0.4, 0.8 and t ¼ 0.30 mm.

structures was implemented by employing the method described in Section 5.1. The Pareto fronts of the P-sheet, IWP-sheet, FRD-sheet and G-sheet structures for the optimization problem defined as Equation (7) was obtained and plotted in Fig. 22. The Pareto front on the left side generally had better SEA than others when having the same PCF. According to the comparison of the Pareto fronts in Fig. 22, it can be seen that the FRD-sheet structures had the best SEA among the four types of TPMS structures at the same level of PCF (when PCF � 15 kN) and then it followed by IWP-type, G-type and P-type successively. The value of SEA increased when the PCF level became larger. When PCF < 15 kN, the Gsheet structures were observed to have better SEA than other TPMS sheet structures and hence had the highest energy absorption efficiency. Based on the above observations, the FRD-sheet structures were recommended. According to the Pareto front together with the design requirement, the designer can determine which type of structures is the optimum design.

In order to check the accuracy of the optimization results, the optimal design of FRD-type sheet structures was selected when the PCF should be constrained under 100 kN. The specific design parameters of FRD-type selected from the Pareto front were marked by a black solid circle in Fig. 22 and the detailed design variables were given in Table 7. Consequently, the FE model of the optimal design of FRD-sheet structures was created. As shown in Table 7, the errors between the FE simulation results and the predicted results obtained by metamodels were within 1%, which was a reliable optimal result in engineering design.

In addition, according to the Ashby plot in the study of Zhang et al. [54], the SEAs of four types of TPMS sheet structures were compared with those of other typical engineering and natural materials as shown in Fig. 23. It can be found that the TPMS sheet structures investigated in this study have high energy absorption capacity as well as high specific strength, which indicated that the TPMS sheet structures had better energy absorption performance than the previous studied natural and engineering materials. Besides, it should be noted that the FRD-sheet structures performed the highest SEA and specific strength among four types of TPMS sheet structures. Based on the results of this study, FRD-sheet structures were found to possess the best ability of energy absorption under axial impact, making it a potential material in future engineering applications.
6. Conclusions
In this study, four types of TPMS sheet structures (P-type, IWP-type,

crashworthiness. Specially, PCF, SEA and MCF of the TPMS sheet structures all increased with the increase of shell thickness t . However, PCF, SEA and MCF did not change monotonously with the increase of level-constant C . The variation of CLE was irregular against the variation of C and t . In addition, it was found that FRD-sheet structure had the highest PCF, SEA and MCF among four TPMS sheet structures with the same C and t . And, the SEA, MCF and PCF of the each TPMS sheet structure all increased with the increase of impact velocity.

The multi-objective optimization design for four TPMS sheet structures was implemented using numerical simulations, metamodels and NSGA-II algorithm. In the process of establishing metamodels, three different metamodels, i.e. Kriging (KRG), polynomial response surface (PRS) and radial basis function (RBF), were established and compared.
