The results indicated that the RBF was the most accurate model for the Psheet and FRD-sheet structures and the PRS was the best approximation model for the IWP-sheet and G-sheet structures. Based on the metamodels of SEA and PCF, the Pareto optimum design of four TPMS sheet structures was obtained and the FRD-sheet structure was found to have the best crashworthiness. In addition, a comparison between TPMS sheet structures and other natural and engineering energy absorbed structures showed that TPMS sheet structures, particularly the FRD-sheet structure, possess higher SEA and specific strength than other traditional structures and have outstanding potential in future engineering applications.

On vibration isolation performance and crashworthiness of a three-dimensional lattice metamaterial
Linwei Zhang a , Zhonghao Bai a , Qiang Zhang a , Yao Jin b , Yafeng Chen c,*
a State Key Laboratory of Advanced Design and Manufacturing for Vehicle Body, Hunan University, Changsha, Hunan 410082, China
b College of Engineering and Design, Hunan Normal University, Hunan 410081, China

A R T I C L E I N F O
Keywords:
Vibration isolation performance Crashworthiness Three-dimensional lattice metamaterials
Multi-objective optimization
1. Introduction

Lattice metamaterials [1], consisting of periodically arranged unit cells, are artificial structures possessing exceptional properties. By carefully designing the unit cells, the properties of the lattice metamaterial can be customized according to different engineering requirements [2]. To date, numerous lattice metamaterials have been proposed to obtain ultra-light and ultra-stiff [3], acoustic damping [4], tunable thermal expansion [5,6], vibration isolation [7 -9], energy absorption [10 -12] properties, etc. The advancement in manufacturing technology, such as 3D printing technology, makes it possible to manufacture a variety of complex lattice metamaterials with various properties [13]. With the development of metamaterials, moving towards complex engineering application scenarios, the trend gradually emerges in incorporating multifunctional design into a single metamaterial [14 -17]. Some efforts have been made to address this issue recently. For instance, An et al. [18] established a 3D lattice structure with chiral microstructures for vibration isolation and sound absorption. Li et al. [19] created lattice metamaterials with struts and plates based on face-centered cubic for integrating the characteristics of acoustic and
* Corresponding author. E-mail address: yfchen@hnu.edu.cn (Y. Chen).
A B S T R A C T

This work investigates the vibration isolation performance and crashworthiness of a novel three-dimensional (3D) lattice metamaterial, whose unit cell is constructed by combining a hollow rhombic dodecahedron and six cylindrical tubes. The transmission of elastic waves in the 3D metamaterial can be suppressed for the existence of band gaps in the metamaterial. Meanwhile, the 3D metamaterial can absorb the crushing energy through plastic deformation when a collision occurs. The influence of structural parameters on the band gap features and crashing behaviors of the novel 3D metamaterial are examined. It is shown that the structural parameters play a vital role in determining the band gap features and crashing behaviors. Thus, the required vibration isolation performance and crashworthiness can be obtained by adjusting the structural parameters reasonably. Finally, multi-objective optimization is performed from the comprehensive aspects of vibration isolation performance and crashworthiness to obtain the optimal design of the novel 3D metamaterial. This work provides a new possibility for the development of multifunctional metamaterials with vibration isolation performance and crashworthiness simultaneously.

mechanical energy absorption. Zhang et al. [20] designed a 3D metamaterial to obtain tunable negative thermal expansion and phononic bandgap properties. These studies present the possibility of integrating multiple functions into one lattice metamaterial. Inspired by these studies, this paper proposes a novel 3D lattice metamaterial to simultaneously achieve the capability of vibration isolation and crashworthiness.

Vibration is harmful to people ' s health and vibration-sensitive precision equipment [21,22]. Hence, the vibration isolation of metamaterials has drawn the remarkable attention of researchers [23 -25]. The vibration isolation of metamaterials can be realized via band gaps: elastic waves with frequencies within band gaps will be blocked [26 -28]. Numerous investigations have been conducted on the band gap features of lattice metamaterials. For example, Jiang et al. [26] presented a class of 3D single-phase metamaterials with bandgaps for lowfrequency and broadband vibration attenuation. Lu et al. [29] introduced 3D lattice metamaterials with hollow spheres to tune the frequency ranges of vibration inhibition and indicated that the triple tunability of bandgaps can be obtained through modifying stiffness contrast. McGee et al. [30] studied the band gap characteristics of 3D-
L. Zhang et al.

printed architected structures consisting of hollow spheres and binders showing that the band gap characteristics can be significantly altered by adjusting the thickness of hollow spheres and the size of binders. An et al. [9] examined the low-frequency bandgaps of a new type of 3D truss-lattice structures. The results showed that the locations of the bandgaps are related to the geometrical parameters of lattice structures. Elmadih et al. [31] analyzed the stopband frequency range of 3D metamaterials with internal resonators and demonstrated that the stopband frequency range of the metamaterial can be tuned by modifying its design. The above examples reveal the great potential of lattice metamaterials in vibration isolation.

Meanwhile, metamaterials have also drawn significant attention to improve the crashworthiness in recent years [32,33]. For example, Bonatti and Mohr [34] studied the compressive responses of novel smooth shell lattice metamaterials with face-centered cubic configuration and showed that the shell lattice metamaterials exhibit exceptional specific energy absorption capacity. Zhang et al. [35] fabricated several kinds of 3D structures with triply periodic minimal surface sheet cores, which exhibited high stiffness, plateau stress, and energy absorption capacities. Chen et al. [12] examined the mechanical properties of a shell-lattice metamaterial and indicated that the fabricated metamaterial can absorb very large amounts of energy with a low density. Tancogne-Dejean and Mohr [36] investigated the crushing responses of Body-Centered Cubic metamaterials composed of tapered beams, suggesting that the metamaterials exhibit stable crush response behavior, characterized by a monotonic increase in the engineering stress -strain curve until densification occurs. Yin and co-workers [37 -39] studied the crashworthiness of a series of 3D metamaterials. The results showed that the proposed 3D metamaterials have excellent crashworthiness characteristics. The above studies demonstrate the high potential of lattice metamaterials in terms of crashworthiness.

Previous works demonstrated that lattice metamaterials have advantages and potential in vibration isolation and crashworthiness. Realizing vibration isolation and crashworthiness in one metamaterial has extensive application prospects. For example, in automobiles, trains, aircraft, etc., the 3D metamaterial can suppress vibration during normal driving and meanwhile absorb the energy of collision in the event of an accident. Additionally, integrating vibration isolation and crashworthiness functionalities on a single metamaterial offers numerous advantages compared to using separate metamaterials for vibration isolation and crashworthiness. These benefits include weight reduction, improved material efficiency, and simplified system design. Therefore, it is worth exploring the potential of using a single metamaterial to achieve both vibration isolation and crashworthiness functionalities. To this end, a new type of 3D metamaterial is proposed in this work. The basic unit of the proposed 3D metamaterial is formed by combining a hollow rhombic dodecahedron and six cylindrical tubes. The band gap properties and crushing responses of the 3D metamaterial are analyzed. The multiobjective optimization is adopted to obtain the optimal design of the proposed 3D metamaterial in vibration isolation and crashworthiness.
2. Design and methodology
2.1. Description of the three-dimensional structure

The proposed 3D metamaterial and its corresponding unit cell are illustrated in Fig. 1. The basic unit cell is composed of a hollow rhombic dodecahedron and six cylindrical tubes. The six cylindrical tubes are located at the vertices of the hollow rhombic dodecahedron and paralleled to X -, Y - and Z -axis directions, respectively. Fig. 1 (a) illustrates the mid-surface of the basic unit cell, with its dimensions described by the lengths a , b , and d . The basic unit cell is obtained by thickening the midsurface along the inside and outside normal. The thickness is controlled by the parameter t , as illustrated in Fig. 1 (b) . To further understand the internal construction of the basic unit cell, Fig. 1 (b) also presents a sectional view of the basic unit cell. The 3D metamaterial studied in this

Fig. 1. Three-dimensional lattice metamaterial and the corresponding unit cell: (a) The mid-surface of basic unit cell; (b) The basic unit cell and its sectional view; (c) The 3D lattice metamaterial with 5 × 5 × 5 cells.

work is composed of a periodic arrangement of 5 × 5 × 5 basic unit cells, as depicted in Fig. 1 (c) . The reason for adopting this unique structural form in this work is to facilitate the manufacturing of the 3D metamaterial. In the present work, our aim is to design a 3D metamaterial with vibration isolation performance and crashworthiness simultaneously. To accomplish this objective, we design a structure that incorporates a cavity. However, for 3D printing manufacturing of configurations with cavities, such as spherical or cubic cavities, additional internal supports may be required due to the limitations of the manufacturing process. The cavity designed in this study has a rhombic dodecahedron shape, and the faces of a rhombic dodecahedron have angles of 45 ◦ or 90 ◦ relative to the XY plane. This characteristic enables the 3D printing of the cavity without the need for additional internal support structures.

The material of three-dimensional metamaterial is set as 316L stainless steel. The material properties of 316L stainless steel are given in Table 1. For the crashworthiness analysis of metamaterials, understanding the stress -strain relationship of the materials is essential. The stress -strain relationship of 316L stainless steel was determined through tensile testing conducted at a constant speed of 2 mm/min according to the ASTM E8/E8M standard. The stress -strain curve of 316L stainless steel obtained from the tensile testing is depicted in Fig. 2.
2.2. Analysis methods
2.2.1. Analysis methods of vibration isolation features

To explore the vibration isolation features of the 3D metamaterial, the band structures are computed using the finite element method. The equation that governs the propagation of elastic waves in the periodic 3D metamaterials can be expressed as follows [40,41]:

calculating the band gap of periodic structures. In this work, COMSOL Multiphysics is employed to calculate the band structures. For brevity, the frequency is normalized as Ω = fa / Ct , where f denotes the frequency, a denotes the lattice constant and Ct denotes the transverse elastic wave velocity of the constituent medium.
2.2.2. Analysis methods of crashworthiness

The schematic diagram for the crashworthiness analysis of the 3D metamaterial in this study is shown in Fig. 4. The bottom of the metamaterial is supported by a fixed rigid plane, and a moving rigid plane is used to impact the top of the metamaterial. A constant impact velocity of 15 m/s is applied to the loaded plane. The compression distance of the 3D metamaterial is set to be 60 mm. During the collision compression process, the crushing force of the moving rigid plane on the metamaterial is measured to assess the collision characteristics of the metamaterial. The numerical model of the 3D metamaterial for crushing analysis is established using the finite element (FE) code LS-DYNA. The 3D metamaterial is modeled by discretizing its mid-surface using fournode Belytschko-Tsay shell elements with five integration points across the thickness. The element size of 0.5 mm is used in this work. An ' automatic surface-to-surface ' algorithm is adopted to simulate the contact relationship between the 3D metamaterial and the moving rigid plane. An ' automatic single-surface ' algorithm is also employed to simulate the self-contact behavior of 3D metamaterial during the deformation process. The strain rate sensitivity of 316L stainless steel material is considered by the Cowper-Symonds model. The strain rate parameters c and p are set to be 240 s 1 and 4.74, respectively [39,43].
3. Evaluation indicators and verification of numerical models
3.1. Evaluation indicators
3.1.1. Indicator of vibration isolation features
To evaluate the vibration isolation features of the 3D metamaterial, the gap-midgap ratio ( GR ) is employed [30,44]:
where f U and f L represent normalized frequencies of the upper and lower edge of the band gap in the band structure, respectively.
3.1.2. Indicators of crashworthiness

To assess the crashworthiness of 3D metamaterial subjected to impact load, three indicators, that is, energy absorption ( EA ), specific energy absorption ( SEA ) and peak crushing force ( PCF ), are used in this work. The EA refers to the total crushing energy consumed by the 3D metamaterial through plastic deformation, which can be calculated as [45]:

3.2. Verification of the numerical model
3.2.1. Verification of numerical models for analyzing vibration isolation

To confirm the effectiveness of the numerical model employed in analyzing the vibration isolation, a transmission spectrum of the 3D metamaterial is computed. The transmitting coefficient TC can be obtained by [18]

where A res and A exc represent the acceleration of the response and excitation when an input force is applied, respectively. The propagation state of elastic waves in the 3D metamaterial can be reflected through the transmission coefficient. The smaller the transmission coefficient, the better the isolation effect of the 3D metamaterial on the elastic wave corresponding to the excitation frequency.

Numerical calculations of the band structures are carried out for the 3D metamaterial with the following structural parameters: a = 20 mm, b = 7 mm, d = 2 mm, and t = 1 mm. The obtained band structure is displayed in Fig. 5 (a) . It can be observed that there is a band gap between the 6th and 7th bands. The corresponding transmission spectrum of the 3D metamaterial is plotted in Fig. 5 (b) . It can be found that the proposed 3D metamaterial presents effective vibration isolation characteristics. Moreover, the normalized frequencies of band gap obtained from the transmission spectrum show good agreement with those from the band structure analysis, as shown in Fig. 5. This proves that the numerical model used for vibration isolation analysis has high accuracy.

To further validate the numerical model, numerical results of the metamaterial in the reference [26] are employed and compared with the numerical results of the same structure calculated by the model of the present work. The numerical result in reference [26] are calculated using the experimentally validated model, which is credible. The numerical model settings for comparison are consistent with the reference [26]. The band structures obtained from the model of the present work and reference [26] are illustrated in Fig. 6. The agreement of the results obtained from the numerical model established in this work with those reported in reference [26] can be observed in terms of the number and position of the band gaps. This indicates that the model established in this work has great accuracy and universality, which can be used for the study of different metamaterials.
3.2.2. Verification of numerical models for crash analysis

A quasi-static loading test is commonly used to validate the accuracy of numerical models for crashworthiness analysis [39,43], and such a test was performed in this study. Since the 3D metamaterial is a periodic structure and considering the manufacturing cost, the experimental verification is conducted by extracting a single unit cell of the metamaterial. The geometric parameters of the unit cell are as follows: a = 20 mm, b = 7 mm, d = 2 mm, and t = 1 mm. The specimen for the quasistatic loading test was fabricated using 3D printing technology. During the test, a moving rigid plate with a constant velocity of 2 mm/min was used to compress the specimen.

Fig. 7 presents the force -displacement curves and deformation modes of the unit cell obtained from the experiment and numerical simulation. From the experimental curve in Fig. 7 (a) , it can be observed that the crushing force of the metamaterial increases rapidly at the beginning of the collision. Then, as the collision progresses, the impact force gradually rises within a certain range and shows a steep growth trend in the final stage of the collision. Fig. 7 (a) also demonstrates the agreement between the force -displacement curve from numerical simulation and the experimental test. In addition, the deformation patterns observed in the simulation are consistent with those observed in the experiment, as shown in Fig. 7 (b) . The comparison between the experiment and numerical simulation indicates that the numerical model can effectively simulate the crushing process. The unit cell test verified the effectiveness of the modeling method. Since the metamaterial is formed by the periodic arrangement of unit cells, there is no other variation between the metamaterial and its unit cell except the geometric variation, so the method can be used for the analysis of the metamaterial.
4. Numerical results and discussion

This section investigates the vibration isolation characteristics and crashworthiness of the 3D metamaterial under various structural parameters. The vibration isolation characteristics are reflected by the band gap characteristics between the 6th and 7th bands, and the crushing performance is presented by the crashworthiness indicators. The lattice constant a of the unit cell is set to be 20 mm. The parameters
Fig. 5. (a) Band structure of the 3D metamaterial; (b) Transmission spectrum of the 3D metamaterial.
L. Zhang et al.
Fig. 6. (a) Band structure obtained from the model established in the present work; (b) Band structure in the reference [26].
b , d , and t of the 3D metamaterial unit are taken as the variables.
4.1. Analysis of vibration isolation characteristics
4.1.1. Effects of b on vibration isolation features

To investigate the impact of parameter b on the vibration isolation features of the 3D metamaterial, band structures are computed for various values of b, while keeping d = 2 mm and t = 1 mm. Fig. 8 (a) shows the variation of the objective band gap as b increases. When the band gap is present, the bandwidth of the band gap first increases and then decreases with an increase in b , as shown in the results. To explicitly present the changes in the band gap with increasing b , Fig. 8 (b) display the variation of GR with increasing b . The trend of GR increasing first and then decreasing with the increase of b can be clearly seen in Fig. 8 (b) . The GR reaches a peak value when b = 7 mm. Note that the value of GR is less than 0 when b = 5 mm and b = 10 mm, as shown in Fig. 8 (b), indicating that the band gaps vanish.
4.1.2. Effects of d on vibration isolation features

In this section, the effect of parameter d on the vibration isolation features is investigated. The band structures with different d values at b = 7 mm and t = 1 mm are calculated. The corresponding band gaps are extracted and plotted in Fig. 9 (a) . It can be found that increasing the parameter d decreases the bandwidth of the band gap. When increasing d to a certain degree, the band gap disappears. For example, there is no band gap when d = 4 mm and d = 4.5 mm, as shown in Fig. 9 (a) . Additionally, the GR in Fig. 9 (b) exhibits an obvious decline tendency with increasing d . Therefore, the bandwidth of the band gap can be increased by decreasing d of the 3D metamaterial properly, thereby inhibiting the propagation of the corresponding elastic wave.
4.1.3. Effects of t on vibration isolation features

The effects of parameter t on vibration isolation features are studied in this part. The variation of the bandwidth of the band gap with different t at b = 7 mm and d = 2 mm is shown in Fig. 10 (a) . It can be seen that the central normalized frequency of the band gap increases slightly as the value of t increases. However, the variation of bandwidth of the band gap with the changing t is small. This is also reflected in the changing trend of GR with changing t , as demonstrated in Fig. 10 (b) . In other words, the variation of GR with changing t is relatively small.

According to the above analysis, the band gap of the 3D metamaterial can be flexibly adjusted by controlling the structural parameters. The ability to control the band gap can be utilized for mitigating the propagation of elastic waves with certain frequencies. This indicates the proposed 3D metamaterial is a promising design for vibration isolation.
4.2. Analysis of crushing performance
4.2.1. Effects of b on crushing performance

This section discusses the impact of b on the crushing performance of the 3D metamaterial. The structural parameters are consistent with the corresponding analysis parameters of vibration isolation characteristics. Based on the numerical simulation, the PCF and SEA of the 3D metamaterial with different b values at d = 2 mm and t = 1 mm are obtained and plotted in Fig. 11. It can be found that the PCF and SEA both increase with the increasing b . To improve the energy absorption efficiency of the 3D metamaterial, a higher value of parameter b can be chosen. However, a higher b will lead to an increase in PCF . In addition, as mentioned in section 4.1.1, the band gap does not exist when b exceeds a certain degree. To achieve vibration isolation and energy absorption characteristics simultaneously, parameter b needs to be limited within the range of the existence of band gaps.
4.2.2. Effects of d on crushing performance

This section presents a discussion on the impact of d on the crushing performance of the 3D metamaterial. Fig. 12 depicts the crushing responses of the 3D metamaterial with different d values at b = 7 mm and t = 1 mm. It can be seen from Fig. 12 (a) that the PCF fi rst decreases and then increases with the increasing d . Fig. 12 (b) shows the SEA s of 3D metamaterials under different values of d . It can be found that SEA reaches a maximum at d = 4.5 mm among these parameters. Excluding d = 4.5 mm, the difference in SEA is not significant at different d values. Nevertheless, the band gap disappears when d = 4.5 mm, as mentioned in section 4.1.2. This means that the metamaterial with d = 4.5 mm cannot be used for vibration isolation. Thus, the selection of d should not exceed the scope that the band gap exists for achieving both vibration isolation and energy absorption capacity.
4.2.3. Effects of t on crushing performance

The effect of thickness t of the 3D metamaterial on the crushing performance is investigated in this part. Fig. 13 shows the PCF s and SEA s of the 3D metamaterials with different t under the conditions of b = 7 mm and d = 2 mm. The trend of monotonous increase can be obviously observed in both PCF and SEA with the increasing t . Under the allowing maximum PCF , a larger thickness t is beneficial to the energy absorption efficiency of the 3D metamaterial. In addition, the effect of t on the GR is

relatively small, as presented in section 4.1.3. Therefore, under the allowing maximum PCF , the thickness t with a larger value is preferred for achieving better vibration isolation and energy-absorbing performance.
4.2.4. Deformation patterns analysis

In this section, the case of 3D metamaterials with different parameters are selected as examples to exhibit the deformation patterns of 3D metamaterials under impact loading. These selected cases are based on a reference configuration of the metamaterial with b = 7 mm, d = 2 mm, and t = 1 mm. By varying one of the parameters ( b , d , or t ), the influence of structural parameters on deformation modes is showcased. Fig. 14 displays the deformation diagrams of a total of four 3D metamaterials, including the reference configuration. From Fig. 14, it can be observed that the deformation modes can be broadly classified into two categories: axial-stacking deformation and buckling-stacking deformation. The axial-stacking deformation occurs in the metamaterial with b = 10 mm, d = 2 mm, and t = 1 mm. The buckling-stacking deformation occurs in the metamaterials with parameters: b = 7 mm, d = 4.5 mm, t = 1 mm; b = 7 mm, d = 2 mm, t = 1 mm; and b = 7 mm, d = 2 mm, t = 0.6 mm. For axial-stacking deformation, the metamaterial undergoes axial stacking deformation along the compression direction. For buckling-stacking deformation, initial buckling plastic deformation is observed at the connection between the small cylindrical tubes and the hollow rhombic dodecahedron in the 3D metamaterial. As the compression process progresses, the buckling deformation at the connection becomes more severe, resulting in the buckling of the small cylindrical tubes. In the overall metamaterial, as the small cylindrical tubes undergo buckling deformation, the hollow rhombic dodecahedron undergoes stacking and squeezing deformation.

From Fig. 14, it also can be seen that the reference configuration of the metamaterial with b = 7 mm, d = 2 mm, t = 1 mm undergoes a buckling-stacking deformation. When changing the values of d or t relative to the reference metamaterial, the deformation mode remains buckling-stacking. When the value of b is changed to 10 mm relative to the reference metamaterial, the deformation mode transitions to axial stacking. This could be due to the larger value of b , while the lattice parameters of the metamaterial remain unchanged in this study, resulting in shorter lengths of the small cylindrical tubes. Compared to the initial reference configuration, the shorter length of the small cylindrical tubes makes them less prone to buckling under compressive forces. Therefore, a transition in the deformation mode occurs, shifting from buckling-stacking deformation to axial stacking deformation.

Fig. 14 also depicts the strain distribution of the metamaterial during the deformation process. It can be observed that the pronounced strains initially concentrate at the junction between the small cylindrical tubes and the hollow rhombic dodecahedron. As the deformation progresses, in the case of axial stacking deformation, significant strains occur in

both the small cylindrical tubes and the hollow rhombic dodecahedron. However, in the case of buckling-stacking deformation, the severe strain primarily occurs in the small cylindrical tubes. Furthermore, Fig. 15 illustrates the force -displacement curves of these four metamaterials, showcasing two distinct categories that correspond to the two deformation modes. The metamaterial undergoing axial stacking deformation, with parameters b = 10 mm, d = 2 mm, and t = 1 mm, shows a force -displacement curve that primarily exhibits continuous increase. The force -displacement curves for the metamaterials with parameters: b = 7 mm, d = 4.5 mm, t = 1 mm; b = 7 mm, d = 2 mm, t = 1 mm; and b = 7 mm, d = 2 mm, t = 0.6 mm, correspond to the buckling stacking deformation mode. The force -displacement curve in the buckling stacking deformation mode initially shows a sharp increase at the onset of the collision. It then exhibits fluctuations within a certain range, approximating a plateau. Towards the end of the collision phase, there is a tendency of further increase.

By analyzing the above results, it is evident that the vibration isolation and energy absorption properties of the 3D metamaterial are significantly affected by its structural parameters. Properly adjusting these parameters can enable control over the band gap and crushing response, thus enabling the control of vibration isolation characteristics and energy absorption performance.

5. Multi-objective optimization for the 3D metamaterial
5.1. Optimization problem

The multi-objective optimization is adopted to obtain the optimal design of the proposed 3D metamaterial for simultaneous vibration isolation and energy absorption. As a multi-functional metamaterial, the 3D metamaterial is expected to have high vibration isolation capability and high energy absorption efficiency simultaneously. Therefore, the GR and SEA are taken as optimization objectives. Moreover, in order to ensure the safety of the object that is being protected by the energyabsorbing structure, it is essential to limit the PCF to a certain level during the impact process [47]. Therefore, the PCF is considered to be a constraint in the optimization problem.

In the present work, the lattice constant a of the 3D metamaterial is set to be 20 mm. Then, the geometric configuration of the 3D metamaterial is controlled by parameters b , d , and t . Based on the analysis results in sections 4.1.3 and 4.2.3, it can be seen that the structural parameter t with a larger value is preferred for achieving better vibration isolation capability and energy-absorbing performance under the allowing maximum PCF . Therefore, the structural parameter t is not selected as a structural design variable and is defined as 1 mm to illustrate the optimization procedure. The optimization problem of the 3D metamaterial in vibration isolation and energy absorption is expressed as:

where F max denotes the allowing maximum PCF , and can be adjusted according to specific needs in engineering applications. b L = 6 mm and b U = 9 mm are the lower and upper limits of the structural parameter b , respectively. d L = 2 mm and d U = 3 . 5 mm are the lower and upper limits of the structural parameter d , respectively.
5.2. Optimization methodology

A multi-objective optimization method, combining radial basis function (RBF) metamodels with non-dominated sorting genetic algorithm II (NSGA-II), is used to solve the optimization problem of the 3D metamaterial described in Eq. (8). Metamodels are usually utilized to reduce computational consumption in multi-objective optimization problems [37]. The commonly used metamodels include Radial basis function (RBF), Kriging (KRG), Support vector regression (SVR), Polynomial response surface (PRS), etc. [48]. Among them, RBF is one of the efficient metamodels, which has been extensively used in engineering optimization design. In this work, RBF is employed to construct metamodels for GR , SEA , and PCF .

To construct accurate RBF metamodels, sample points are extracted from the design domain using the Design of Experiments (DOE) method. The typical DOE method includes full factorial, Latin hypercube, Taguchi orthogonal, central composite and et al.[49,50]. The full factorial method is utilized in this work to extract sample points for the RBF metamodels due to its uniform sampling. To evaluate the accuracy of the developed RBF metamodels, the relative error ( RE ) is adopted as the measure. The RE is calculated as follows:

With the RBF metamodels developed, the NSGA-II algorithm can be utilized to solve the multi-objective optimization problem. The NSGA-II has been proven to effectively solve numerous multi-objective optimization problems and is widely used in actual engineering design [49]. Thus, the present study utilizes NSGA-II to obtain the Pareto front for GR and SEA .
5.3. Results

To assess the precision of the constructed RBF metamodels, four verification points are randomly selected within the design space. Table 2 presents the structural parameters, numerical results, RBF approximations, and corresponding relative error (RE) values of these four points. Table 2 illustrates that all RE values for the four randomly generated verification points are below 5%, demonstrating the high accuracy of the developed RBF metamodels in predicting G R , SEA , and PCF . Thus, the constructed RBF metamodels can be used for the following multi-objective optimization of the 3D metamaterial.

Using the accurately constructed RBF metamodels and NSGA-II, the Pareto fronts for the multi-objective optimization problem described in Eq. (8) can be obtained. It should be noted that the value of F max in Eq. (8) mainly depends on practical engineering needs. Herein, F max = 500kN is taken as an example to display the distribution of Pareto solutions. Fig. 16 shows the Pareto front of the 3D metamaterial under F max = 500kN. It can be seen that, under the constraint of F max, the two optimization objectives GR and SEA conflict with each other. Selecting the optimal design with higher GR on the Pareto solutions will cause a decrease in SEA and vice versa. This indicates that there is a certain conflict relationship between vibration isolation characteristics and energy absorption efficiency when selecting the optimal parameters of the 3D metamaterial. Choosing the optimal design parameters to improve one performance, such as vibration isolation or energy absorption, may result in some compromise in the other performance.

Actually, each point in the Pareto solutions corresponds to an optimal design of the 3D metamaterial. Designers can select the optimal design scheme to meet the practical engineering requirements based on the Pareto solutions. In the present work, a ' knee point ' on the Pareto front for balancing GR and SEA is selected by using the minimum distance selection method (TMDSM) [49,51]. The expression of the TMDSM for choosing the ' knee point ' in Fig. 16 is written as:

suppresses the transmission of the elastic wave, indicating that the 3D metamaterial has excellent performance on vibration isolation. Meanwhile, the state of the 3D metamaterial with the optimal design before and after the impact loading is shown in Fig. 18 (b) . The displacement contour in Fig. 18 (b) reflects the displacement variation of the deformed 3D metamaterial relative to its original state. When a collision occurs, the 3D metamaterial can effectively absorb the collision energy through deformation. To summarize, this study achieves multi-objective optimization of 3D metamaterial for vibration isolation and energy absorption, offering useful guidance for the design of multifunctional metamaterials.
6. Conclusion

This study presents the design of a novel 3D metamaterial with comprehensive examination of its vibration isolation capacity and crashworthiness. A multi-objective optimization is conducted to optimize the metamaterial, considering both vibration isolation and crashworthiness. The main conclusions are as follows:

By adjusting the structural parameters of the proposed 3D metamaterial, the band gap and crushing responses can be controlled, thereby controlling the vibration isolation characteristics and energy absorption performance.
The band gap between the 6th and 7th bands opens first and then closes with the increase of b . The GR of the band gap initially

increases and then decreases as b increases. The PCF and SEA both increase with the increase of b .

The band gap between the 6th and 7th bands gradually decreases with the increase of d . The GR exhibits a declining tendency with the increase of d . The PCF fi rst decreases and then increases with the increasing d . The difference in SEA is not significant with the increase of d in general.

The variation of GR with changing t is relatively small. Both PCF and SEA increase with the increase of t . Thus, the structural parameter t with a larger value is preferred for achieving better vibration isolation capability and energy-absorbing performance under the allowed maximum PCF .

Based on the Pareto front, there is a certain conflict relationship between the vibration isolation characteristics and energy absorption efficiency of the 3D metamaterial under the allowed maximum PCF .

1. Introduction

As an energy-absorbing structure, lattice structure can be filled in the cavity of some automotive body structures to improve the structural crashworthiness, such as energy-absorbing boxes, anti-collision beams, etc. [1 -4]. In addition, most cellular structures that makeup lattice structures have excellent energy absorption capacity [5], sound absorption [6], and heat transfer [7]. Moreover, lattice structures have more design possibilities than traditional porous structures, such as metal foams [5]. Lattice structure is formed by periodically transforming the cell structure after a basic cell structure is designed.

Scholars have designed and fabricated many lattice structures, the common ones are 3d-kagome structures [8 -9], pyramid structures [10 -11], tetrahedral structures [12], and octahedral structures [13 -14]. Mori used the punching and folding method to prepare a layer of single-cells of a pyramidal lattice structure [15]. The preparation of lattice structures has the advantages of rapidity, simplicity, and low cost, but it can only be used to prepare lattice structures with simple geometry, and there is a lot of waste of materials. Wang applied the concept of
* Corresponding author. E-mail address: xiechong18@mails.jlu.edu.cn (D. Wang).
A B S T R A C T

A spatial lattice structure is proposed to improve the structural crashworthiness and energy absorption. First, a single-cell structure is proposed via a bionic honeycomb, and then the single-cell structure is arrayed to form a spatial lattice structure. The finite element model of the spatial lattice structure for crashworthiness analysis is established. The theoretical model of the plateau stress of the lattice structure under compressive conditions is derived, and its accuracy is verified by the corresponding finite element analysis. After that, the effect of geometrical parameters of single-cell structure on crashworthiness is analyzed. Then, geometrical parameters of single-cell structure and number of single-cell in the X, Y , and Z directions are used as design variables, the mass, and overall size are used as constraints, and specific energy absorption and collision peak force are used as optimization objectives. The multi-objective crashworthiness optimization of spatial lattice structure is carried out, and the optimal lattice structure design is determined via the gray relational analysis coupling entropy weight method. The validity of the finite element model and optimal design is verified by the drop weight impact test. The crashworthiness optimization of spatial lattice structures has important guiding significance for the design of energy-absorbing structures.

interlocking assembly to the preparation of lattice structures and studied the relationship between the mechanical properties and concave hexagonal angle by uniaxial compression test and numerical simulation [16]. The results show that the mechanical properties of lattice structure are closely related to the geometric parameters of the structure. Wang also used the interlocking process to prepare an X-shaped lattice structure sandwich panel [17] and carried out uniaxial compression and shear tests. The results show that the mechanical properties of the X-shaped lattice structure sandwich panel are better than the sandwich panel of pyramid-shaped lattice structure. Deshpande used the lost-wax casting process to fabricate a lattice structure [18]. The results show that lattice structure is superior to the corresponding foam metal material in stiffness and strength, and the lattice structure can be used as a substitute for foam metal. Queheillalt used the extrusion forming process and wire cutting process to prepare the pyramid-shaped lattice structure sandwich panel [19], thereby avoiding the unstable local mechanical properties caused by the local material change in the welding process, but the material waste during wire cutting is more. The results show that the mechanical properties of sandwich panels are determined by
Fig. 1. Establishment of reinforced symmetrical hexagonal lattice: (a) Bionic honeycomb, (b) Hexagon, (c) Reinforced hexagon, (d) Reinforced spatial lattice, (e) 3 × 4 × 8 spatial lattice.
geometry parameters, mechanical properties of alloy, and loading mode.

As additive manufacturing technology is widely used in the fabrication of structures with a single material, fused deposition modeling, stereolithography appearance, selective laser melting (SLM), and multijet fusion are used for complex structural fabrication of thermoplastic elastomers, engineering plastics, and metals. Del Broccolo proposed a new type of honeycomb structure [20], whose basic single-cell is composed of two kinds of hexagons, and called the honeycomb structure as AuxHex honeycomb structure. Guo creatively proposed the AuxHex lattice structure based on AuxHex honeycomb structure [21], established the theoretical model of elastic constant and quasi-static plateau stress of AuxHex lattice structure, and compared the crushing of AuxHex lattice structure with traditional lattice structure. The results show that the AuxHex lattice structure has better energy absorption performance under quasi-static and dynamic crushing. The AuxHex lattice structure fabricated by MJF technology was subjected to a crush test, which verified the accuracy of the theoretical model and numerical simulation. Tancogne-Dejean used the SLM technique to fabricate an octahedral lattice structure with a single-cell structure consisting of an inner octahedron and an outer tetrahedron [22]. The research results show that when the relative density of the octahedral lattice structure is lower than 30%, the stress-strain response of the octahedral lattice structure is a monotonically increasing function. And the specific energy absorption of the octahedral lattice structure is larger than the traditional honeycomb structure. Zhang fabricated and investigated a circular tubular lattice with enhanced stiffness [23]. Delcuse explored the effect of 3D printing process parameters on the mechanical properties of Auxetic structures [24]. Jiang studied the mechanical properties of 3D-printed auxetic lattice structures under bending [25]. In addition, Carneiro manufactured and studied printing 3D auxetic honeycomb structures [26]. The auxetics fabricated by 3D printing showed excellent mechanical properties.

Moreover, a variety of feasible design schemes are obtained by multiobjective optimization, but the Pareto solution set is non-dominated, and the optimal solution that satisfies all design objectives cannot be obtained. Therefore, the weighting coefficients of each objective function are set by the preference degree for each performance objective through experience in engineering applications, but this process has artificial subjective factors and lacks objective quantitative analysis. To reduce the subjective effect factors, different objective evaluation methods are proposed to determine the optimal solution. Khalkhali proposed a method to determine the optimal solution from Pareto solutions using NIP and TOPSIS methods when performing multi-objective optimization of the sandwich structure, which reduces the subjective effect on the selection of optimal solution [27]. To determine the optimal solution for the comprehensive performance of body in white, Cai Kefang proposed a method using gray correlation analysis (GRA) and gray entropy (GE) method to select the optimal solution from the non-dominated solution set [28], and found that the optimal solution obtained by GRA and GE method has better comprehensive performance compared with the optimal solution obtained by TOPSIS method.

Inspired by these research findings, this paper proposes a novel thinwalled structure inspired by honeycomb structures. Its unit cell is designed as a hexagonal reinforced structure. And a spatial lattice structure is proposed based on 3D printing technology to improve structural crashworthiness and energy absorption ( EA ) properties. The relationship between structural parameters and crashworthiness is discussed. To reduce peak force ( PF ) and improve specific energy
Fig. 2. Tensile test picture.
Table 1 Material property of AA6061-T6.

absorption ( SEA ), a multi-objective optimization design is proposed to achieve the optimal design of spatial lattice structures and further improve structural crashworthiness. Through structural design and optimization, the crashworthiness matching design of spatial lattice structure is quickly and effectively realized.
2. Modeling of a novel spatial lattice structure
2.1. Crashworthiness analysis of spatial lattice structure

The lattice structure is obtained by transforming the single-cell structure of bionic honeycomb hexagon, as shown in Fig. 1. First, the hexagonal single-cell structure is extracted based on the bionic honeycombs. To further improve the crashworthiness of the structure, a novel
Fig. 3. Stress-strain curves of AA6061-T6 material.

single-cell structure of lattice structure is formed by adding a reinforcing column in single-cell structure of honeycomb hexagon. The top and bottom and left and right shapes of the structure are symmetrical, so it is called a reinforced symmetrical hexagonal structure. The geometric structure of single-cell structure is determined by the following parameters: length of the bottom side L , length of the hypotenuse I , length of connecting rod J , angle between the bottom side and hypotenuse θ and cell wall thickness T , of which the cell wall section is square. By copying the single-cell structure and rotating 90 ◦ around the reinforcing column, the basic single-cell of spatial lattice structure can be obtained, and then the basic single-cell can be arrayed in three directions of space. Finally, the reinforcing symmetrical hexagonal can be formed. The number of single-cell structures in X, Y , and Z directions is 3, 4, and 8, respectively, as shown in Fig. 1e. This is called a 3 × 4 × 8 spatial lattice structure.
2.1.1. Material tensile test

The material of the lattice structure is AA6061-T6 aluminum alloy. The tensile test specimens are designed according to the standards GB/T 228.1 -2010 and GB/T 30,069.2 -2016, as shown in Fig. 2. Then, the quasi-static material tensile test is carried out by electronic universal testing machine, and the dynamic material tensile test with a strain rate of 100 s 1 and 300 s 1 is carried out by high-speed tensile testing machine
Fig. 4. FEM of lattice structure.
Fig. 5. Four kinds of lattice structures.

of STGL-50, respectively. The stress-strain relationship of AA6061-T6 material under quasi-static and dynamic tension tests are obtained as shown in Fig. 3. The stress-strain curves of AA6061-T6 materials with different strain rates are almost coincident, which means that the stress of AA6061-T6 materials is not sensitive to the strain rate. The material property values of AA6061-T6 are shown in Table 1.
2.1.2. Establishment of lattice structure model

The lower end of the lattice structure is fixed on an immovable rigid plate. Taking a small electric vehicle with a curb weight of 800 kg as a reference model, a rigid plate weighing 800 kg is selected at the upper end of the lattice structure to impact the lattice structure vertically downward. The lattice structure can be filled in energy-absorbing boxes of automotive body to improve the structural crashworthiness. Therefore, according to the regulations of Research Council of Automotive Repairs, the velocity of upper rigid plate is set to 15 km/h. LS-DYNA software is used as the solver for finite element analysis (FEA). The automatic contact algorithm of the FEA is used between the lattice structure elements, and the point-surface contact algorithm is used between the lattice structure and the upper and lower rigid plates, respectively. And dynamic and static friction coefficients of the contacts are set to 0.15 and 0.2, respectively. The material model of Mat 24 in LSDYNA is used to define AA6061-T6 aluminum alloy material. MATL24 is a material model that can reflect the elastic-plastic mechanical properties of materials, and the data in Fig. 3 are input into the MAT24 keywords. The FEM of the low-velocity dynamic load of the lattice structure is shown in Fig. 4.
2.2. Indicators for crashworthiness analysis

The lattice structure is derived from the honeycomb structure. EA, SEA, PF , and average force ( AF ) are commonly used evaluation indicators [29]. It can also be evaluated by plateau stress σ m and its corresponding equivalent strain interval [30], and platform stress means that the equivalent stress corresponding to a period of equivalent strain is always around a certain value when the structure is crushed. The computation of plateau stress σ m is shown in Eq. (1).

where ε 0 is strain corresponding to initial peak stress; σ ( ε ) is the function of stress on strain; ε d is densification strain, which is the initial strain of structure entering densification stage during the compression process [31].

ε d is a velocity-sensitive quantity, which mainly depends on the topology of cell structure and impact velocity [32]. The deconsolidation strain is calculated based on the principle of energy absorption efficiency [33], which is defined as the ratio of absorbed energy to corresponding nominal stress at a given nominal strain [34].
The densification strain ε d can be determined by Eq. (3).
From Eq. (2) and Eq. (3), the densification strain of different multicellular structures can be determined through the equivalent stressstrain curve.
2.3. Comparison of crashworthiness of several lattice structures

To verify the rationality of the proposed reinforced symmetrical hexagonal lattice structure for improving structural crashworthiness, the FEMs of the lattice structure and several common lattice structures are established, as shown in Fig. 5. To eliminate the effect of single-cell size on computation results as much as possible, the width of each single-cell is set to 30 mm, and the height of the other single-cells is set to 18.32 mm except for the double-arrow single-cell. And the side length of the square beam section of all single cells is T = 1 mm. To eliminate the effect of the overall scale of lattice structure on the response, the outline dimensions of all lattice structures are designed to be 180mm*240mm*142 mm in the shape of a cuboid.

According to the loading and constraint requirements in Section 2.1.2, the initial velocity of the upper rigid plate impacting the lattice structure is set to 15 km/h, and the SEA indicators of different lattice structures are compared and evaluated. The FEA of these four lattice structures is carried out. Then the SEA s of the four lattice structures are extracted, as shown in Table 2. The SEA of lattice structure (d) is the

2.4. Theoretical analysis and verification of plateau stress

In collapse process, when the bending moment of beam in single-cell of lattice structure is equal to the plastic bending moment, the single-cell begins to enter the plastic collapse stage, as shown in Fig. 6. The singlecell of lattice structure forms a total of 19 plastic hinges in the plastic collapse. Due to the symmetry of lattice structure, only 11 plastic hinges are marked with circles in Fig. 6a.

According to energy conservation law, the work done by the external force on a single cell is equal to the work done by the plastic collapse during the collapse process of the single-cell lattice structure, as shown in Eq. (4).

where Mp represents plastic bending moment; σ s is yield stress of the material; Fp is force of single-cell structure during crushing; σ p is plateau stress; Δ y is the displacement in crushing direction after the single-cell is completely crushed; φ i , φ v , and φ v represent final rotation angle of the inclined cell wall, two ends of the vertical cell wall and plastic hinge in the middle of the vertical cell wall, respectively, after the single-cell is completely collapsed.
The theoretical plateau stress of lattice structure can be obtained by substituting φ i = 60 ∘ , φ v = 90 ∘ , and φ m = 180 ∘ , as shown in Eq. (8).

The moving rigid wall is loaded at a constant speed of V = 15 km/s, and the loading displacement is 80% of the height of the lattice structure. 2.5. Effect of structural parameters on performance

The geometric parameters of single-cell structure can be determined by the five basic dimensions of single-cell structures, which are T = 1 mm, L = 12 mm, I = 12 mm, J = 8 mm, and θ = 120 ◦ . When studying the effect of single cell parameters on the collision resistance of lattice structure, only one single cell parameter is changed, and the other four single-cell parameters remain unchanged. The single cells are arranged in a 3 × 4 × 8 lattice structure in the x, y, and z directions, and the parameter settings of FEM are the same as those in Section 2.4. The SEA, PF , and AF are selected as the evaluation indicators of the lattice structure.
2.6. Effect of beam section size on performance

The side lengths T of the beam section are set to 1 mm, 2 mm, 3 mm, 4 mm, and 5 mm, respectively. Through the FEA of lattice structures with different side lengths of beam sections, the computation results are shown in Fig. 8.

As can be seen from Fig. 8, the larger the beam section size, the greater the SEA of the structure. When T is greater than 4 mm, the SEA begins to decrease. The PF and the AF increase continuously with the increase of T . The main reason is that when the beam section size increases, the lattice structure can enter the densification stage when the upper rigid plate is crushed for a shorter distance, that is, the densification strain of the lattice structure decreases, and the lattice structure is compacted after densification. The EA is drastically reduced.
Fig. 10. Effect of base length L on crashworthiness indicators.
Fig. 11. Single-cell and lattice structure with different length I .
slowly, and when L is greater than 4 mm, the AF began to decrease slowly.
2.8. Effect of hypotenuse length on performance

The single-cell length I is the length of hypotenuse of symmetrical hexagonal structure. Changing its size can change the height of symmetrical hexagonal structure. The single-cell and lattice structures are generated by different hypotenuse lengths I , as shown in Fig. 11.

The results of FEA of lattice structures with different single-cell hypotenuse lengths I are shown in Fig. 12. When the single-cell hypotenuse length I decrease from 16 mm to 2 mm, the SEA gradually increases, and the SEA reaches the maximum value at 2 mm. However, when the singlecell length I decreased from 16 mm to 6 mm, the PF and AF increased slightly. When the single-cell length I was less than 6 mm, the PF and AF increased rapidly. This is also due to the increase of plastic hinges in unit space.
2.7. Effect of base length on performance

The single-cells and lattice structures are generated by different single-cell base lengths L , as shown in Fig. 9. Changing the length L can change the width of single-cell structures. Therefore, the effect of the width of single cells on structural crashworthiness is studied.

The FEA of the lattice structure composed of different single-cell base lengths L is carried out. The results are shown in Fig. 10. When L varies in the range of 4~16 mm, the increase of base length L will gradually decrease SEA . When L is between 0 and 4 mm, the increase of the base length L will lead to a gradual increase in the SEA . In general, the SEA increases as the length L of the bottom edge decreases. This is because after L is reduced, the volume density of the material per unit space will become larger, which improves the utilization rate of the material. Moreover, the plastic hinges of the lattice structure in the unit space increase, and the energy absorption will be more when L is reduced. The PF fi rst decreased and then increased with the increase of the base length of single-cell. When L increased from 0 mm to 4 mm, the AF increased
Fig. 12. Effect of length I on crashworthiness indicators.
Fig. 13. Single-cell and lattice structure with different connecting rod lengths J .
2.9. Effect of connecting rod length on performance

Since the hexagonal structure formed by connecting rod and adjacent single-cells also has certain impact resistance, the effect of the length J of the connecting rod on the impact resistance of the structure is investigated. The single-cells and lattice structures are generated by different connecting rod lengths J , as shown in Fig. 13.

When the length J of the single-cell connecting rod is 0 mm, 2 mm, 4 mm, 6 mm, and 8 mm respectively, the crashworthiness indicators of the lattice structure are calculated, as shown in Fig. 14. As the length of connecting rod decreases, the SEA gradually increases, and when the connecting rod length is 0, that is, when the connecting rod is canceled, the SEA of lattice structure is the largest. However, the change of length J of the single cell has little effect on the PF and AF .
2.10. Effect of angle on performance

Changing the angle θ between the hypotenuse and bottom edge of single-cell structure will affect the work done by single-cell in the process of completely flattening the inclined beam during the crushing process. The greater work is done by the corresponding plastic hinge. The single-cell and lattice structures are shown in Fig. 15 when the angle θ is changed, respectively.

Through FEA of lattice structures with different angles θ , the crashworthiness indicators of each model can be extracted, as shown in Fig. 16. The SEA increases continuously when the single-cell angle θ decreases from 140 ◦ to 110 ◦ , and when θ decreases to 100 ◦ , the SEA decreases rapidly. However, the PF did not change much when the single-cell angle θ decreased from 140 ◦ to 110 ◦ . But the PF increased sharply when θ decreased to 100 ◦ . And the change of the angle has little effect on the AF .

3. Multi-objective optimization of spatial lattice structure

To further improve the crashworthiness of the lattice structure, the basic parameters of single cells that make up the lattice structure will be considered, and the optimal lattice structure will be obtained. The multiobjective optimization technical route of lattice structure is shown in Fig. 17.
3.1. Design variable

The lattice structure is formed by single cells arrayed in space, and the outline size of lattice structure is limited to the design space of 80mmx100mmx130mm in length, width, and height. To maximize the use of design space, the number of single cells should be selected as much as possible to approach the maximum size limit when establishing the lattice structure. The number of cells in X, Y , and Z directions, namely, nx, ny , and nz , is taken as the design variable. The initial values and upper and lower limits of design variables are shown in Table 3.
3.2. Design of experiment

The design of experiment method adopts the optimal Latin hypercube sampling (OLHS), and a total of 50 groups of sample points are established. The dynamic analysis condition of the lattice structure is consistent with Section 2.1.2. Then, the FEA of each sample point is established and analyzed, and mass ( M ), PF , and EA are obtained, respectively, as shown in Table 4.
3.3. Approximation model

A radial basis function neural network approximation (RBFNN) model is established based on 50 sample points, and 20 sample points are used for the cross-precision tests. The coefficient of determination R 2 and root mean square relative error (RMSRE) of the three responses are calculated, as shown in Table 5. R 2 are all greater than 0.95, and RMSRE is less than 0.05. Therefore, the RBFNN model has high accuracy and meets the design requirements. Among them, the predicted values are derived from approximate models, and the simulated values are derived from the FEA.

The NSGA-II method has high efficiency and convergence when searching for non-dominated solutions. Therefore, NSGA-II is widely used to solve multi-objective optimization problems [35 -36]. The mathematical model of multi-objective optimization of lattice structure is optimized by the NSGA-II algorithm. In the NSGA-II algorithm parameters, the initial population size is set to 40, the maximum number of iterations is set to 80, and the crossover coefficient is set to 0.9.
After 3200 analyzes and calculations, the Pareto frontier solutions are obtained, as shown in Fig. 18.

To verify the accuracy of the optimization, the simulation values of Design 1 and Design 2 are calculated respectively, as shown in Table 6. The optimized values are very close to the simulated values, and the maximum relative error is 4.78%, which appears in SEA indicator of Design 1. Therefore, the multi-objective optimization method has high accuracy in solving spatial lattice structure optimization.

3.6.2. Drop weight impact test

To verify the accuracy of the simulation analysis of the optimized lattice structure, the drop weight impact test is designed and verified. The 3 × 4 × 5 lattice structure printed with SLM is shown in Fig. 20a. First, the 3 × 4 × 5 lattice structure is placed on the platform of the drop hammer impact testing machine. Then, according to the impact test requirements of spatial lattice structure, the test impact conditions are set as follows: the drop hammerhead is a spherical surface with a radius of 150 mm, the drop weight The total weight of drop weight (including the counterweight) is 166.7 kg, and then based on the upper plane of the

lattice structure, the drop weight is lifted to a height of 0.885 m. The hammerhead is released to impact the lattice structure on the platform. The speed of the drop weight when it falls freely to the lattice structure is 15 km/h, and the relationship curve between impact force and displacement of the lattice structure is obtained, as shown in Fig. 21.

At the same time, the FEA of the 3 × 4 × 5 lattice structure of the AlSi10Mg material is established, as shown in Fig. 20c. Combined with the stress-strain relationship in Fig. 19, the MAT24 material model is adopted for AlSi10Mg material. The simulation analysis conditions of the lattice structure are set to the same as the experimental conditions. The FEA of the lattice structure under the drop-weight impact condition is carried out, and the impact force-displacement curve is obtained, which is compared with the test results, as shown in Fig. 21.

The initial PF values obtained by simulation and test are very close, as shown in Fig. 21. The force-displacement curves obtained by simulation and test are integrated to obtain the EA as shown in Table 10. The relative error between the simulation and test of PF is 0.47%, and the

4. Conclusions
A novel spatial lattice structure is designed, and a multi-objective optimization method is carried out for the lattice structure employing GRA coupling EW. The main conclusions are as follows:

(1) A single-cell structure is designed based on a biomimetic honeycomb structure. By comparing several typical lattice structures, it shows that the SEA of the proposed structure is higher. The plateau stress formula of the structure is derived, and the accuracy of the theoretical model is verified by numerical computation.

(2) The five size parameters of the single-cell structure have a great influence on the structural crashworthiness of the spatial lattice structure. With the increase of the cross-sectional size T of the single-cell beam, the SEA of the lattice structure first increases and then decreases. When the length L of the bottom edge of the single cell is in the range of 0 -8 mm, the SEA is better. The hypotenuse length I of the single cell is within 2~8 mm, and the SEA gradually increases with the decrease of I. When the length J of the unit cell connecting rod decreases from 4 mm to 0 mm, the SEA gradually increases, that is, when the single

cell connection structure is canceled, the SEA is the highest. The SEA is higher when the angle θ between the bottom edge and the hypotenuse of the single cell is in the range of 100 ◦ ~120 ◦ . Therefore, choosing the appropriate single-cell geometric structure parameters can effectively improve the crashworthiness of the lattice structure.

(3) Based on the optimal Latin hypercube and RBFNN model, a multiobjective optimization design is carried out for the spatial lattice structure. Comparing the optimal solutions screened by TOPSIS, and HMCDM methods, the optimal design scheme of the spatial lattice structure is determined using the GRA coupling EW method. The results show that the SEA of the optimized lattice structure increases from 6.15 kJ/kg to 24.45 kJ/kg. According to the optimal design, the spatial lattice structure with AlSi10Mg material is printed through 3D printing technology. And the optimal lattice structure is carried out through the dropweight impact test. The maximum relative error is 9.32%, which verifies the validity of the simulation analysis results.

Response of hybrid plate-rod lattices to static and dynamic compression -An experimental study
Zhuang Cui a,b , Zhengping Sun a , Jiayun Zhao a , Yuanyuan Ding a,* , V.P.W. Shim a,c,*
a Key Laboratory of Impact and Safety Engineering, Ministry of Education, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, Zhejiang, China
b Institute of Advanced Equipment, College of Energy Engineering, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou 310027, Zhejiang, China
c Impact Mechanics Laboratory, Department of Mechanical Engineering, National University of Singapore, 9 Engineering Drive 1, Singapore 117575, Singapore
A R T I C L E I N F O
Keywords: Lattice structure Dynamic behavior Deformation pattern Energy absorption Additive manufacturing
1. Introduction

The load-deformation characteristics of lattice structures under quasi-static and impact loading can be adjusted through prescription of geometrical parameters [1 -3]. Because of their favorable load-bearing, energy absorption, and impact cushioning capabilities, lattices have been supplanting traditional monolithic structures for effective shielding of objects from impact [4 -6]. Depending on the structure of their constituent cells, lattices can be categorized as open-celled [7 -9] and close-celled [10 -12], which generally comprises rods and plates respectively. Rod-based and plate-based lattices possess different
A B S T R A C T

In general, lattices are frequently composed of cells that are defined by struts or plates (shells), and each has its unique advantages and limitations in terms of load-bearing and energy dissipation under gross deformation. The hybrid plate-rod lattice structure in this study combines favorable features of these two basic constituent topologies, to achieve superior energy absorption properties under impact. The hybrid structure is established by combining a semi-open Octet-Plate lattice (SOPL) and a strut or rod-based open-cell lattice, to form a hybrid plate-rod lattice (HPRL), amenable to fabrication via selective laser melting (SLM). To elicit the quasi-static and dynamic mechanical response of the structures investigated, planar compression was applied to both SOPL and HPRL specimens using a universal testing machine, a high-speed compression tester, and a direct-impact Hopkinson pressure bar (DHPB); these generated quasi-static, medium strain rate, and high strain rate deformation respectively. Applicability of the DHPB and single wave data processing technique in obtaining valid experimental results was verified by digital image correlation (DIC). The test results show that the HPRL exhibits superior mechanical behavior and energy absorption compared to its SOPL counterpart, for both quasi-static and impact compression. Significant rate sensitivity of the responses was also observed. For impact at 57 m/s, the plateau stress and energy absorption of the HPRL both increase by approximately 30 %, above their respective values for quasi-static deformation. By using two specimen mounting approaches in DHPB tests, this study also demonstrates fulfillment of specimen stress equilibrium, inertia effects, and strain-rate sensitivity of HPRL lattices during high-speed compression. For impact velocities below 57 m/s, inertia effects are not obvious, and the elevation in stress can be attributed primarily to material strain rate sensitivity; beyond 57 m/s, inertia effects and stress non-uniformity begin to be manifested. In essence, this effort highlights the advantages of combining cells of dissimilar geometrical characteristics to attain enhanced responses, and the potential of applying this approach to other cell configurations.

inherent advantages. Specifically, rod-based lattices are lighter, more flexible in terms of design, and easier to fabricate, while plate-based lattices display a higher load-bearing and energy-absorption capacity for the same mass. Cui et al. [13] compared the compressive response of rod-Octet and plate-Octet lattices with the same relative density and found that the plateau stress of the plate-Octet lattice was almost 75 % higher than that of the rod-Octet lattice. Consequently, this motivates exploration of the possibility of combining high-strength plate lattices with lightweight rod lattices to establish hybrid lattices with mechanical properties that can be tuned and improved, and also to achieve superior energy absorption.

Recently, considerable attention has been directed towards hybrid lattice structures to enhance their energy absorption and mechanical performance, by amalgamating different basic lattice topologies to generate novel configurations such as rod hybrid [14 -16], plate hybrid [17 -19], and plate-rod hybrid [13,20,21] lattices. By integrating the characteristics of their constituent structures, hybrid lattice configurations exhibit notable enhanced mechanical properties, which include stiffness, yield strength, and specific energy absorption under static and dynamic compression. For instance, Sun et al. [22] combined tension-dominated and bending-dominated rod lattices to obtain a novel rod-rod hybrid lattice, and verified experimentally that this results in a more stable and higher plateau stress. It has been noted that hybrid lattices are sensitive to the rate of loading, whereby static and dynamic compression result in different deformation modes and force-deflection responses. Andrew et al. [19] explored various plate hybrid lattice configurations by integrating simple cubic, body-centered cubic, and face-centered cubic plate lattices, and assessed their impact response and energy absorption via drop-hammer tests. They found that plate hybrid structures exhibit a higher impact resistance, as a result of more modes of cell interaction within the structures, consequently also improving their energy absorption capacity. Similarly, Wu et al. [21] proposed coalescing the mechanical properties of plate-based and hollow rod lattice structures to develop three variants of hybrid plate-rod lattices, and concluded that plate-based and hollow rod lattice structures can synergistically promote wider distribution of stress and more effective material utilization via a hybrid configuration. Consequently, hybrid lattices appear to display superior responses under both quasi-static and impact crushing. The hybrid plate-rod lattice structure is relatively new, and comprises plates for strength in load bearing, complemented by lightweight rods to adjust the deformation modes. Interaction between these components enhances the mechanical properties of the resultant hybrid lattice. This observation constitutes a major portion of the motivation to derive deeper insights into the impact response and energy absorption characteristics of such structures.

As with conventional monolithic materials, the dynamic response of lattice structures generally differs from their static behavior due to inertial resistance and strain rate effects. Current research on the dynamic response of lattices often involves drop hammer [23 -25] or Split Hopkinson Press Bar (SHPB) experiments [26 -29]. Li et al. [26] employed a drop hammer and SHPB dynamic loading on layered hybrid lattices composed of octet-truss and modified re-entrant hexagonal cells, and showed that there was significant velocity-dependence in their mechanical response. Cao et al. [29] conducted impact tests on rhombic dodecahedron lattices using an SHPB and compared the quasi-static and dynamic stress-strain curves within a strain range of 0.2. This demonstrated that rhombic dodecahedron lattices exhibited increased energy absorption and higher compressive strength as the rate of deformation increased. Drop hammer devices have an inherent velocity limit due to restrictions in height and space [29,30]; on the other hand, SHPB-based testing has challenges in inducing large deformations, thus limiting the application of a constant global strain rate to elicit specimen behavior and energy absorption properties up to full compaction at desired strain rates. To overcome these challenges, the Direct Impact Hopkinson Bar (DHPB) experimental technique has been developed, and found capable of inducing significant deformation in specimens [31 -34] at high strain rates. Wang et al. [32] employed this approach for aluminum foam specimens and utilized Digital Image Correlation (DIC) to derive the compressive strain. They confirmed the presence of both strain-rate and inertia effects for high-speed impacts. ˇ Sleichrt et al. [33] examined three different types of aluminum alloy-based cellular materials fabricated in-house in terms of dynamic penetration by employing a two-sided DHPB. Their results show that wave propagation-related and inertia effects have a significant influence on the strength and deformation behavior of cellular materials during low-velocity crushing. Although Direct Impact Hopkinson Bar experiments have been carried out on cellular materials, hybrid lattices have seldom been tested this way, especially in studying the dynamic behavior and energy absorption characteristics of hybrid plate-rod lattice structures.

The present effort proposes a novel hybrid plate-rod lattice (HPRL) structure and examines its mechanical response to quasi-static and dynamic gross compression. A semi-open Octet-plate lattice (SOPL) was selected for combination with a rod-based lattice structure to form a hybrid HPRL, whereby each rod is connected to the center of a plate in the SOPL structure. 17 -4PH stainless steel powder was used to fabricate HPRL specimens by selective laser melting (SLM). The deformation pattern and mechanical response for various speeds of compression were elicited using an MTS universal testing machine, high-speed compression tester, and DHPB device. Accuracy of the single-wave analysis in processing dynamic strain data was verified by combining it with DIC. Impact tests were also conducted by mounting specimens onto either the output bar or on the striker, to compare the response for different impact modes and velocities. The present study also reveals differences in mechanical behavior and energy absorption characteristics between pure plate-based lattice structures and hybrid plate-rod lattice structures, under quasi-static and impact crushing.
2. Specimen geometry and preparation
2.1. Design of lattice cell architecture

A survey of literature [35,36] on the mechanical performance of lattice materials shows that the Octet-Plate lattice exhibits good specific stiffness and strength. However, when powder-based 3D printing is employed to fabricate closed Octet-Plate cells, unprocessed powder remains within them. Therefore, a semi-open Octet-Plate lattice (SOPL) structure was proposed in this study by introducing holes in a diagonal pattern, as shown in Fig. 1. By incorporating a rod structure into the semi-open Octet-Plate lattice structure, plate-rod lattice cells are established, whereby a rod provides support perpendicular to the center of each plate in the SOPL, thereby constraining deformation of the plate, and imposing additional restraint to increase resistance of the hybrid structure to deformation. A detailed description of the plate-rod hybrid design can be found in [13]. Using SolidWorks software, 4 × 4 × 4 cell assemblies of the semi-open Octet-plate lattice (SOPL) and hybrid plate-rod lattice (HPRL) were established by connecting four unit cells along three orthogonal directions. After careful consideration of the printing powder size, structural integrity, and ease of powder removal, semi-open Octet-plate and plate-rod hybrid cell topologies measuring 6 × 6 × 6 mm³ with holes of 1.8 mm diameter (D), were selected for fabrication. The design parameters of the SOPL and HPRL, corresponding to a common relative density of 22 %, are listed in Table 1.
