2.2. Additive manufacturing of cell-assembly lattices

Lattices specimens were fabricated utilizing an EOS M280 metalpowder 3D printer employing SLM (Selective Laser Melting) technology [37,38]. As shown in Fig. 2, the powder feed scraper sweeps 17 -4PH stainless steel metal powder from the powder feed bin, layer by layer onto a table. A laser then selectively melts the powder at specified areas, gradually forming the specimen. The printer used operated with a processing power of 400 W, a scanning speed of 1m/s, powder delivery at 80 mm/s, powder layer thickness of 20 μ m, and an oxygen content below 0.5 %. Moreover, the substrate temperature was maintained at 80 ◦ C throughout the printing process. After printing, powder remaining within the fabricated lattices was removed using air jets, and the lattice specimens were detached from the substrate via wire-cutting. To ensure consistency among specimens and minimize variability arising associated with possible unanticipated small changes in printing parameters and environmental factors, all specimens were produced in a single batch. The specimens produced are shown in Fig. 3(a).

To ascertain the overall quality of the printed samples and integrity of their internal structure, the detailed surface and internal morphology of HRPL samples were observed using an optical microscope. The lattices were cut to observe the presence of possible defects at various locations, as depicted in Fig. 3(b). This revealed some unevenness and fine particles on both the plate and rod surfaces, suggesting the presence of potential defects. Despite these surface imperfections, the connections between plates, as well as between the plates and rods, appear consistently good. The tensile engineering stress-strain curves of the specimen matrix material (17 -4 PH stainless steel) from which the specimens were made, are shown in Fig. 4. From these, the elastic modulus was estimated to be about 38 GPa, and the yield stress is 535 MPa. 17 -4PH stainless steel exhibits a slight decrease in stress after yielding, followed by gradual hardening until fracture, and the toughness derived from the area under the curve up to the point of fracture, is 150 MPa. The actual geometrical parameters, mass, and relative density of the semi-open Octet-Plate lattice (SOPL) and hybrid plate-rod lattice (HPRL) specimens fabricated, are listed in Table 2. The relative density of both the proposed SOPL and HPRL was 0.22, and they had an average mass of

3. Planar crushing tests at different compression rates
3.1. Quasi-static and moderate-speed compression

Quasi-static planar compression of the cubic lattice specimens printed were performed using an MTS universal testing machine with a 250 kN load cell. Fig. 5(a) shows a specimen placed between two metal platens, whereby the upper one is fixed and the lower one moves upward at a constant speed of 0.024 mm/s, to the limit of compression; this speed of compression corresponds to a global nominal strain rate of 0.001 s 1 . The compressive force and displacement of the moving platen were recorded, while optical images of the specimen deformation were captured by a video camera at a framing rate of 20 Hz.

Fig. 5(b) shows a servo-hydraulic high-speed tester (ZwickRoell HTM 5020) capable of compressing at 0.01 m/s to 20 m/s, utilized to study the moderate-rate (global strain rates in the order of 10 2 /s) compression response of the lattices; it was fitted with a 50 kN piezoelectric force
Table 2 Lattice geometries and actual printed specimen dimensions.
Fig. 5. (a) Quasi-static compression; (b) Moderate-speed dynamic compression testing.

sensor (ZwickRoell Xforce). For low and moderate-speed compression, lattice specimens were placed at the center of the fixed lower platen, and compressed by the upper platen at 0.024 m/s, 0.24 m/s, and 2.4 m/s, which correspond respectively to compressive engineering global strain rates of 1 s 1 , 10 s 1 , and 100 s 1 . Lubrication in the form of grease was applied to the surface of the loading platens to reduce friction. A Photron SA-1 high-speed camera was connected to the testing machine to enable synchronization of recording of the force-displacement signals and videographic images during specimen compression. Data was captured at 10 4 Hz, respectively, to facilitate finer analysis of the later phase of compression. Three samples of each lattice design were tested to ascertain consistency of results.
3.2. Impact compression

To generate dynamic gross compressive deformation of the lattice specimens, modification of a conventional split Hopkinson pressure bar to a direct impact Hopkinson pressure bar (DHPB) arrangement was undertaken. The DHPB comprises three primary components: a striker launcher, a target arrangement, and a data collection system, as depicted in Fig. 6(a). The striker launcher component comprises a gas gun that utilizes high-pressure nitrogen to propel a cylindrical striker with a length of 20 cm. The target arrangement comprises the specimen, output bar, and a protective case. The data acquisition component encompasses strain gauges on the output bar, dynamic signal conditioners, an oscilloscope, a high-speed camera, and a velocity measurement device. To exceed the diagonal length of the cubic specimen surface, the diameters of the striker and DHPB output bar were both selected to be 40 mm. To prevent superposition of the transmitted wave and its reflection from the free end of the output bar, the output bar system was an assembly of a 2.5 m long bar touching a second 2 m long bar, such that the latter acts as a momentum trap. A pair of diametrically opposed strain gauges was mounted 200 mm from the interface between the specimen and the output bar. Photographs of the experimental setup are shown in Fig. 6 (b).

The experimental procedure entailed affixing a specimen to the output bar using quick-drying adhesive, then launching the striker to impact the specimen. The velocity of the striker was measured by a velocity detector. During impact, the oscilloscope recorded the voltage associated with the strain gauge signal from the output bar, while the high-speed camera captured optical images of the specimen deformation at a framing rate of 10 5 Hz. Alternatively, the specimen could also be mounted and fixed to the striker bar, so that they move together to impact the output bar. This dual approach enables examination of whether there is a difference in the specimen mechanical response and deformation pattern arising from the two impact modes.

The specimen global stress-strain relationship was derived from the strain gauge signal ε 0 ∗ ( t ) from the output bar, the initial velocity v 0 of the striker, the specimen loading force F ( t ) and particle velocity v 2( t ) of the output bar interface with the specimen as follows:

where A 0 and E 0 are respectively the cross-sectional area and elastic modulus of the output bar; C 0 is the elastic stress wave speed in the output bar, defined by ( E 0/ ρ 0) 1/2 , and ρ 0 is the density of the output bar material. Assuming that the forces at the impact and supported ends of the specimen are in equilibrium during testing [31,32,39,40], the global engineering stress-strain relationship ( σ s , ε s ) and strain rate ˙ ε s of the lattice specimens can be obtained via the following expressions:

where As and l s denote respectively the cross-sectional area and length of the specimen, v 1 is the velocity at the impact interface between the specimen and the striker and ˙ ε ( τ ) is the global strain rate of the specimen. It is noted that the velocity of the striker-specimen interface does not remain constant during specimen compression using a DHPB, because of elastic wave propagation and reflection within the striker bar. If the initial impact velocity of the striker is v 0 and the forces at both ends of the specimen remain in equilibrium, the velocity of the strikerspecimen interface can be obtained by considering the internal

propagation and reflection of elastic waves in the striker [39]. Hence, if T = l / C 0 is the time taken for the stress wave to travel the length l of the striker bar during the time interval 2 NT ≤ t ≤ 2 ( N + 1 ) T , where N is the integer denoting that time interval ( N = 1, 2, … ), the velocity of the striker-specimen interface is given by:

This theoretical derivation assumes ideal conditions, and its applicability in actual experiments needs to be verified. Consequently, the theoretical predictions were compared with data obtained from DIC to ascertain the degree of concurrence.

The DHPB data processing technique utilized in the experiments is based on the assumption of stress equilibrium between the two ends of the specimen, prompting deliberation on the accuracy of the experimental processing. Digital image correlation (DIC) is well suited for derivation of strain values in high-speed impact of lattice structures, but necessitates employment of experimental equipment with higher-level specifications and images of high pixel quality. However, employing Eqs. (1 -3) for DHPB data processing enables direct acquisition of stressstrain data during impact. If results obtained this way are confirmed to be accurate, then data from the direct impact tests can be immediately utilized to elicit the dynamic stress-strain curves for lattice specimens, without complex data processing. Adoption of reliable DIC processing is preferred for validating the theory. Optical images of dynamic compression of the specimen were obtained using a Photron SA-1 highspeed camera. To enhance accuracy in using DIC (digital image correlation) to derive displacement and deformation data, speckles were sprayed onto the striker and output bar 1 as well, so that their motion was also captured, in addition to that of deforming portions within the specimen. In a typical test with the specimen mounted on output bar 1, the striker was prescribed a velocity of 19 m/s by regulating the air pressure, and the camera framing rate was set at 3 × 10 4 Hz. Appropriate illumination was provided by high-intensity LED lamps, and the highspeed camera aperture and focus were adjusted for maximum clarity. Tests at higher speed impacts were limited by the capacity of the camera to capture an adequate number of images with sufficient resolution for DIC processing. The length of the calibration sample in the image imported into the Vic-2D software was set at 24 mm, with a subset size of 25, a step size of 5, followed by Gaussian weighting applied for DIC processing of the image. The speed of the striker and motion of the output bar were derived from DIC processing of the images captured.

The velocity fields at different instants during a typical impact compression are shown in Fig. 7(a). The velocity field at 0.26 ms after impact contact shows that the undeformed layer in the specimen and the striker are moving together to the right at approximately the same speed, while the layer of collapsed cells is almost stationary. Between them, there are several colored bands denoting different velocities, and these indicate ongoing crushing of cells in those layers. Fig. 7(b) depicts the axial strain field in the lattice specimen, output bar and striker, along the impact direction. At each instant depicted, the axial strain ε xx in the specimen is non-uniform, and displays localized deformation under dynamic compression. The region of maximum axial strain, shown in red, is concentrated in the vicinity of the impacted left end of the specimen, and propagates to the right.

In DHPB testing, calculation of the interface velocity at both ends of the specimen enables derivation of the specimen global stress-strain relationship. The velocity of the specimen-output bar interface can be calculated from the wave propagation equation in Eq. (1), while the velocity at the striker-specimen interface is determined using Eq. (3) together with the striker impact speed, measured by the velocity detector. To verify the velocity-time curves obtained from the DHPB approach, the velocity history derived by differentiating the displacement-time curves obtained by DIC, were obtained for comparison, as shown in Fig. 8(a). Close agreement between the DHPB-based and DIC-based results, from commencement of impact to specimen densification after 0.72 ms, is observed. However, divergence between the two curves emerges from 0.72 to 1.0 ms after impact contact; this is likely attributed to the assumption of ideal stress equilibrium conditions for specimens in DHPB theory. The specimen global engineering strain can be calculated from Eq. (2) for the DHPB approach, and the average strain in the specimen can also be obtained by DIC. Given that densification of HPRL specimens occurs prior to reaching a global engineering strain of 0.51, an average strain value of 0.52 based on DIC measurement is adopted as a reference for evaluating the difference between the two approaches. In Fig. 8(b), a comparison between the DHPB-derived and the DIC-derived strains is presented. At 0.77 ms, the strain from DIC processing is 0.52. The strain calculated using DHPB theory is 0.53, corresponding to only a 1.7 % difference, indicating that the DHPBbased calculations are substantiated by optical measurements.

4. Results of compression tests on hybrid plate-rod lattices
4.1. Quasi-static compression

Quasi-static compressive engineering stress-strain curves for SOPL (semi-open Octet-Plate lattice) and HPRL (hybrid plate-rod lattice) configurations, derived from experimental force-displacement data and the initial cross-sectional area and height of the samples are shown in Fig. 9, which depict consistency in results from two repeated tests. Both SOPL and HPRL configurations display similarities in terms of three phases in their response: initial elasticity to the first stress peak, a postyield plateau (albeit with stress fluctuations) during which the bulk of the energy applied goes into plastic deformation, and final densification. The seven oscillations observed in the post-yield plateau correspond to layer-by-layer plastic collapse of cells in the structure. Subsequently, the collapsed cells are compacted, and the stress rises sharply, signifying final densification. The average plateau phase stress for the HPRL configuration consistently exceeds that of its SOPL counterpart, because of interaction between cell plates and their rod supports, which stiffens and strengthens this plate-rod hybrid arrangement. However, the HPRL reaches densification at a slightly smaller strain, because the inclusion of internal rods reduces the volume of voids to accommodate deformation.
It has been well-established that each layer of a lattice structure can withstand a specific maximum load, which is linked to deformation via
Fig. 9. Quasi-static compressive engineering stress strain responses for SOPL and HPRL specimens at a global strain rate of 10 3 s 1 .

layer-by-layer collapse accompanied by sequential stress peaks. Although four stress peaks may be expected because of the four cell layers, seven peaks are actually observed. This is caused by the presence of holes in the four cell layers and at the three cell layer interface regions, which results in seven horizontal zones prone to localized collapse, and consequently seven stress peaks and troughs. Deformation of the SOPL and HPRL specimens was captured by optical photography and analyzed using DIC, as shown in Fig. 10, whereby both structures undergo discernible layer-by-layer compression and collapse, as evidenced by the horizonal colored strain contours. For the SOPL specimen, each collapsing layer is sequentially crushed from the lower to the upper end, accompanied by respective sharp drops in stress. However, when each collapsed layer becomes compacted, the stress begins to increase again. This process repeats until all collapsed zones are severely crushed; this progression corresponds to the oscillations observed in the stressstrain curves. Fig. 10 shows that a similar layer-wise deformation progression is observed in HPRL specimens. At a strain of 0.05, the structure begins to collapse from both sides of the lower cell layer interface, because of localized weakness here. Localized gross crushing propagates to the top and bottom of the HPRL until the final layer with central rods connected to the center of the plates within cells is compacted; the image of the HPRL specimen corresponding to compression to 0.3 global strain depicts this. Unlike the deformation pattern of the SOPL, collapse in the HPRL specimen initiates simultaneously at both the upper and lower sides of the cell interface zone, before the cell layer between them collapses. This observation suggests that the internal rods connected to the plate centers of cells, have a significant influence on the deformation mode and deformation resistance. The stress-strain curve for the HPRL configuration also reflects this layer-by-layer deformation, as it exhibits the same oscillatory profile as the SOPL. Nevertheless, there is a noticeable difference between the post-yield stress plateaus of the SOPL and HPRL lattices, as shown in Fig. 9. When the specimens are compressed to a global strain of 0.45, both enter their densification phase. However, inclusion of rods within the cells of the HPRL lattice provides additional resistance to plate deformation, consequently increasing the stress level, as shown in Fig. 9. Cell collapse in both SOPL and HPRL lattices start from the lowest layer, because the bottom surface of the specimens is slightly uneven and thus weaker; this is the result of the wire cutting process employed to detach specimens from the base-plate, which first needs to be established in the 3D printing process. At a global engineering strain of 0.45, all the cells in the specimens have collapsed, and the stress increases rapidly subsequently, as further compression densifies the crushed cells.
4.2. Moderate-velocity dynamic compression of HPRL lattices

Fig. 11 shows typical stress -strain curves of HPRL specimens crushed dynamically at a low strain rate using a ZwickRoell HTM 5020 accelerated drop weight tester set to at an impact speed of 2.4 m/s. However, as compression progresses, the deformation rate gradually decreases due to resistance from the specimen, such that when the test sample approaches densification, the crushing rate decreases from 2.7 m/s to about 1.5 m/s. Since the original height of the specimen is 24 mm, these speeds translate to global engineering strain rates of 113/s and 62.5/s

Fig. 11. Global compressive engineering stress-strain response of HPRL, corresponding to moderate strain rates in the order of ~ 10 2 s 1 , together with the variation of compression speed with specimen strain.
Fig. 10. Evolution of lattice deformation during compression.

respectively, which corresponds to an average engineering strain-rate in the order of 10 2 s 1 . The profiles of the stress-strain curves show similarities with their quasi-static counterparts, but under dynamic compression, the initial yield or collapse stress of about 54 MPa for HPRL lattices is much higher, by 46 %, indicating obvious ratesensitivity. Following yield, HPRL specimens display a series of stress fluctuations as horizontal zones of collapse are activated sequentially. When the global engineering strain exceeds 0.5, the specimens start to densify and generate a steep monotonic increase in stress.

Fig. 12 depicts the deformation patterns and the DIC-derived distribution of axial strain in the vertical direction for HPRL specimens compressed dynamically at 2.4 m/s. It shows that at a global engineering strain of 0.05, the deformation of HPRL specimens involves both localized collapse of the bottom cell layer and localized collapse in the cell layer interface region above that, as indicated by the DIC strain color band. Thereafter, the lattice displays layer-by-layer crushing, similar to that observed in quasi-static compression, accompanied by stress oscillations in the post-yield plateau. When the compressive strain reaches 0.35, the HPRL structure begins to compress uniformly. Subsequently, the stress increases more sharply as the crushed cells are further compacted. The DIC strain color bands coalesce to a single color when the strain reaches 0.35, indicating uniform deformation in the lattice.
4.3. Direct Hopkinson pressure bar (DHPB) impact

High-speed direct impact compression was applied to semi-open Octet-plate lattice (SOPL) specimens and its hybrid counterpart (HPRL) using the DHPB device. The specimens were mounted on the output bar, and impacted by the striker at 31 m/s, corresponding to an impact energy of 942 J. Global engineering stress-strain curves, as well as corresponding deformation patterns are depicted in Figs. 13 and 14; both SOPL and HPRL specimens also exhibited seven distinct peaks in their stress-strain responses, corresponding to the collapse of cell layers and cell layer interface regions. As expected, the stress levels associated with the HPRL geometry are higher than that of SOPL lattices, due to strengthening via incorporation of rods into the cells. Compared with quasi-static loading, the dynamic stress-strain curves display larger oscillations and sharper peaks.

As deformation progresses, the magnitude of the stress peaks for SOPL specimens gradually decreases until the specimen is fully compressed. This trend contrasts with the behavior observed for quasi-static compression, as depicted in Fig. 9, whereby the stress peaks are approximately the same in magnitude. The reduction in peak stress during direct impact tests can be attributed to a decrease in the speed of compression, which drops by approximately 13 % from the onset of impact to the densification strain (31.4 m/s to 27.3 m/s), as shown in Fig. 13. Dynamic deformation in SOPL specimens also occurs via
Fig. 13. Global engineering stress-strain curves for SOPL and HPRL specimens subjected to impact at 31 m/s; variation of striker speed with strain.

progressive layer-wise crushing, as shown by the high-speed camera images in Fig. 14, where deformation commences from the end affixed to the output rod, and propagates to the impacted end. For dynamic deformation of HPRL specimens, Fig. 13 shows that the first four stress peaks exhibit a decline in magnitude, followed by a sharp increase in the fifth peak that exceeds the stress peaks of SOPL specimens. This significant rise in stress is primarily because subsequent layers collapse by crushing within cell layers (shown in Fig. 14), which are reinforced by the incorporation of rods to support the plates, thus elevating resistance to deformation. When all the cells and cell layer interface regions have been crushed, compaction of the crushed material causes the stress to rise sharply; this occurs later for the SOPL configuration, which can deform to a larger global strain before densification.

Both HPRL and SOPL configurations exhibit significantly higher peak stresses under impact loading compared to quasi-static compression. Table 3 presents a comparison of key parameters characterizing the quasi-static and impact response of the two lattice types. The HPRL variant yields a higher plateau stress and specific energy absorption for both static and impact compression, whereby the plateau stress of the HPRL exceeds that of the SOPL by 33.5 % for quasi-static compression, and has a specific energy absorption value 25.9 % higher than that of the SOPL. For impact at 31 m/s, the plateau stress of the HPRL configuration surpasses that of the SOPL by 42.8 %; this is also accompanied by a specific energy absorption that is 34.0 % higher. Consequently, with regard to energy absorption, the HPRL generally displays superior performance to the SOPL under both quasi-static and dynamic compression.

5. Discussion
5.1. Stress equilibrium for DHPB tests

To ascertain whether there is a difference in stress between the two ends of lattice specimens in DHPB impact testing, the DHPB testing arrangement was modified to examine the evolution of stress the impact end; this is illustrated in Fig. 15. In the modified arrangement, the sample was mounted onto the striker, and impacts the output bar together with the striker. The stress history at the impacted end of the sample was determined through strain gauges attached to the output bar. By assuming stress equilibrium in the specimen, its stress-strain response was also obtained using Eqs. (1) -(3).

Fig. 16 depicts the global stress-strain response and deformation characteristics of a HPRL specimen mounted on the striker during DHPB testing at an impact speed of 31 m/s. The stress variation also exhibits seven distinct peaks, linked to the influence of inclusion of holes into the lattice architecture. Compared to the response when HPRL specimens are mounted on the output bar, as shown in Fig. 13, the magnitude of the initial yield stress when the specimen is mounted on the striker appears to be higher, because the output bar experiences inertial resistance of
Fig. 14. Deformation in SOPL and HPRL specimens under impact compression from the left side at 31 m/s.
Fig. 16. Stress-strain relationship and deformation characteristics of HPRL specimen mounted on the striker during DHPB compression.

the specimen in addition to the force required to initiate plastic structural collapse of the specimen. The high-speed camera images in Fig. 16 show that plastic collapse in the specimen commences from the interface where the specimen is affixed to the striker, and propagates towards the impacted surface. This is similar to the deformation process when the specimen mounted onto the output bar, whereby gross crushing also starts from the fixed end of the sample and propagates to the impacted end. This common observation may be because at this impact velocity, the initial elastic wave generated travels to the fixed boundary and its reflection superposes constructively with the loading wave, causing the resulting stress magnitude to exceed the yield stress, thus initiating plastic deformation from the fixed end.

The dynamic global stress-strain curves derived from the two alternative methods of specimen mounting are presented in Fig. 17 to illustrate similarities in the responses after the initial phase, and demonstrate that either arrangement yields approximately the same result for gross deformation of the specimen. When the specimen is mounted on the
Fig. 15. DHPB test arrangement for specimens mounted on the striker.
Fig. 17. Global stress-strain curves of HPRL specimens derived from DHPB tests with the specimen mounted on the striker and on the output bar.

striker, it makes direct contact with the output bar upon impact, and thus the strain gauge on the output bar measures this contact force, which comprises inertial resistance of the specimen in addition to the force required to deform it. When the specimen is attached to the output bar, the signal from the strain gages on the output bar corresponds to the stress on the striker after propagating through the specimen, and inertial resistance of the specimen is absent. Prior to a strain of 0.075, there is a difference in the stress derived from the two methods of mounting the specimen, because during this initial phase, apart from inertial resistance, wave propagation within the specimen has yet to achieve stress equilibrium. Following this, the stress derived from the two mounting arrangements converge, indicating stress uniformity within the specimen. To examine whether specimen mounting location has other influences, the average plateau stress, densification strain and specific energy absorption were also compared, and these values are presented in Table 4. The results show that except for the first peak stress, which has a noticeable difference, disparities in the other parameters are < 2 %, which can be considered insignificant. Therefore, assumption of stress equilibrium in specimens after the initial response phase, is acceptable in DPHB tests.
5.2. Inertia effects

In dynamic crushing of lattice structures, there are two influences generating enhancement of stress that require consideration, namely inertial resistance and material rate sensitivity. For the HPRL specimens studied, inertial resistance is associated with acceleration of the mass of structural components as they deform dynamically, while rate sensitivity is linked to the properties of the material the lattice is made from.
To demonstrate the effects of inertial resistance and material rate sensitivity, compression of HPRL specimens at higher impact speeds
Table 4 Comparison of response parameters for HPRL specimens from DHPB tests with different specimen mounting locations.

using the DPHB device was undertaken. Taking into consideration the limitation of the experimental hardware, an HPRL specimen mounted on the output bar was impacted by the striker at 57 m/s, corresponding to an impact energy of 3184 J. The resulting stress-strain curve and deformation development are shown in Fig. 18(a). A notable observation is that the stress-strain curves do not have seven distinct oscillations, which characterize quasi-static deformation; instead, there are fewer fluctuations. This is because the specimen no longer deforms according to a layer-by-layer crushing fashion at this high impact velocity. Examination of the deformation process shows that under high-speed impact, several layers of cells in the vicinity of the impacted (left) end deform simultaneously, followed by deformation in the next few layers. This indicates that under sufficiently high impact speeds, inertial resistance becomes significant, deformation no longer starts and progresses from the structurally weakest layer, but impulsive acceleration of stronger regions can trigger collapse in them first.

A comparison of the stress-strain curves derived from tests on HPRL specimens mounted on the output bar and on the striker, both corresponding to impact at 57 m/s, is presented in Fig. 18(b). It shows that the stress magnitudes and profiles exhibit differences. Calculation of the average stress before all cell layers collapse indicates that the value obtained from mounting the specimen on the striker, is 9.3 % higher than that when the specimen is mounted on the output bar. This is most likely caused by additional inertial resistance in decelerating the material that comes into sudden contact with the output bar which measures the transmitted force, whereas when the specimen is mounted on the output bar, the striker is resisted by both the inertia of the contacting layer which has to accelerate impulsively, as well as the force to deform the cells, which is what is detected by the output bar. Under dynamic loading, the significance of inertial resistance depends on the degree of difference in speed compared to static loading. For example, for a honeycomb with a relatively low yield stress or plateau stress, lateral impact at 14 m/s is sufficient to generate obvious inertial resistance [44]. However, the HPRL lattice studied has a higher yield and plateau stress, so it is surmised that only when the impact speed reaches a magnitude such as 57 m/s, do inertia effects become obvious. When inertial resistance becomes excessive, validity of DPHB testing is lost, because the stress at impacted end is larger than the stress in the structure behind it, and stress equilibrium within the specimen no longer exists. In such situations, the nominal strain is generally derived from image analysis methods, such as DIC [32].
5.3. Energy absorption and strain rate effects

When inertia effects are not significant, the increase in stress under impact loading can be attributed to rate sensitivity of the lattice material. This material rate sensitivity is demonstrated in Fig. 19(a) by the global stress-strain curves of HPRL specimens mounted on the output bar, subjected to quasi-static and dynamic compression at increasing impact speeds. An increase in stress level with loading rate is clearly discernible, even for low velocity impact at 2.4 m/s, where inertial resistance is not significant. However, for impact at 57 m/s, inertial resistance becomes very evident, necessitating the consideration of both inertia and material rate sensitivity. Below this speed, the increase in overall stress is attributed primarily to material rate sensitivity. It is noted that the global compressive strain rate experienced by lattice specimens is not constant during impact deformation, but decreases slightly with striker displacement. Consequently, the average strain rate from the commencement of compression to the point of densification, is used to define the deformation rate. Fig. 19(a) shows that the initial yield and plateau stress both exhibit an increase with deformation rate, as a result of both inertial resistance and material rate sensitivity growing in significance.

Fig. 19(b) shows how the average plateau stress and specific energy absorption vary with global strain rate, and an increase in both with the speed of deformation is evident, whereby the plateau stress displays a

Fig. 18. (a) Stress-strain curves and deformation patterns for HPRL specimens mounted on the output bar; (b) comparison of stress-strain responses derived from HPRL specimens mounted on the striker and output bar in DHPB tests.

Fig. 19. (a) Average global stress-strain curves for HPRL corresponding to various average strain rates; (b) Comparison of average plateau stress and specific energy absorption values for different average nominal strain rates.

29.8 % increase, from 38.7 MPa at a global strain rate 0.001 s 1 , to 50.3 MPa at an average global strain rate 2400 s 1 ; the corresponding respective specific energy absorption increases by 29.9 % from 11.24 J/ g to 14.6 J/g.
6. Conclusions

In this study, a semi-open octet-plate lattice (SOPL) and a hybrid plate-rod lattice (HPRL) were designed, then fabricated utilizing the Selective Laser Melting (SLM). They were subjected to quasi-static and dynamic compression to elicit their global stress-strain response, deformation mode, and energy absorption properties. With respect to impact testing using a direct Hopkinson pressure bar (DHPB), verification of strain data processing accuracy based on Hopkinson bar equations, was substantiated by DIC analysis. This implies that for such tests, the relevant data can be derived directly from calculations following the approach presented. The dynamic responses of specimens corresponding to different rates of compression were also examined, and the influence of inertial resistance and rate-sensitivity observed, were discussed. The primary findings are:
Both quasi-static and dynamic compression of the SOPL and HPRL specimens studied generate seven discernible stress oscillations in the post-yield plateau phase, and these correspond to

layer-by-layer plastic collapse within the structure. The average plateau stress of the HPRL configuration is consistently higher than that of its SOPL counterpart, because the rods incorporated into the constituent cells proved effective in enhancing deformation resistance of the plates they were attached to.

Validity of the dynamic global stress-strain curves obtained from direct impact Hopkinson pressure bar (DHPB) tests was verified by comparison with results from DIC imaging; the maximum difference in strain between the two approaches, up to the stage of specimen compaction, was an essentially negligible 1.7 %.

The assumption of stress equilibrium within specimens beyond the initial ' elastic ' phase of response in DHPB testing, was verified by conducting tests with two different modes of specimen mounting -i.e., on the striker and on the output bar. With respect to inertial resistance, inertia effects are not significant for impact below 57 m/s (corresponding to a global strain rate of 2400/s for the specimens tested), and the elevation in stress can be attributed primarily to material rate sensitivity; beyond 57 m/s, inertia effects and specimen stress non-uniformity begin to be discernibly evident.

The average plateau stress and specific energy absorption of HPRL lattices increase with impact velocity, whereby the plateau stress and specific energy absorption of the HPRL configuration are higher than that of its SOPL counterpart, by 42 % and 34 %,
respectively for impact at 31 m/s as an example, compared to their quasi-static response, indicating significant rate sensitivity.

This study demonstrates the potential and feasibility of combining cells of dissimilar geometrical characteristics to attain enhanced mechanical responses, and that this approach can be considered for application to other cell types and configurations.

Development of novel hybrid TPMS cellular lattices and their mechanical characterisation
Nejc Novak a, * , Oraib Al-Ketan b, ** , Matej Borovin  sek a , Lovre Krstulovi  c-Opara c , Reza Rowshan b , Matej Vesenjak a , Zoran Ren a

Keywords: Cellular materials Triply periodical minimal surface Hybrid lattices Experimental testing Computational modelling Multi-morphology
1. Introduction

Cellular structures are being comprehensively investigated regarding their mechanical and physical properties for use in modern applications, e.g. technology, medicine, fashion, and other areas [1,2]. They are one of the most promising building
* Corresponding author.
** Corresponding author.
E-mail addresses: n.novak@um.si (N. Novak), oraib.alketan@nyu.edu (O. Al-Ketan).
a b s t r a c t

Uniform lattices composed of one type of lattice structure repeated periodically have been extensively investigated in literature for their mechanical and physical properties. Their promising properties, which include a desirable combination of high strength, stiffness and toughness, suggest that hybrid structures made of two or more lattice types can exhibit even moreadvantageousanddesiredproperties.Inthiswork,themechanical properties ofhybrid cellular structures designed using implicit functions are investigated both experimentally and numerically. Two proposed samples are investigated comprised of a Gyroid and a Diamondunit cells hybridised linearly and radially. First, a finite element computational model was utilised in LS-DYNA to capture the mechanical properties of the additively manufactured constituent lattices (i.e., Gyroid and Diamond) made of stainless steel 316L and tested under dynamic and quasi-static loading conditions. The model was validated for three different relative densities. Then, the validated computational model was then tested to predict the mechanical behaviour of the proposed hybrid lattices. Finally, the proposed hybrid lattices were fabricated and mechanically tested to obtain their mechanical properties. A good agreement between experimental and computational results was achieved. The validated computational models will be used to evaluate other designs of TPMS lattices and their crashworthiness performance for protective equipment applications.

materials for future products due to their attractive combination of lightweight properties, high energy absorption capacity, damping and thermal insulation [3,4]. The use of cellular materials in functional components is growing increasingly due to significant advances in manufacturing processes and the lowering of fabrication costs [3].
Available online at www.sciencedirect.com
j o urnal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/jmrt

The development and fabrication of periodic cellular structures, commonly known as lattices, has been made possible by recent advances in additive manufacturing [5]. Regular lattices are simple to design using computer-aided design software, and their behaviour under loading is easier predictable because of their periodicity. The behaviour of several types of strut-based unit cells under quasi-static and dynamic loading has been thoroughly studied [6 e 8]. Some new types of periodic closed-cell lattices have been developed and mechanically characterised recently. Examples include the plate-type lattices [9 e 11] and the shell-based triply periodic minimal surface (TPMS) lattices designed based on implicit functions [12 e 17]. While plate-type lattices demonstrated desirable properties with stiffness values that may reach the Hashin-Shtrikman upper bounds in low relative densities, their closed-cell topology mandates adding additional holes for resin or powder removal in AM process which may deteriorate their properties due to stress concentration. On the other hand, TPMS-based lattices are partially open lattices with an interconnected network of pores that allows easy removal of resin and powder. Al-Ketan et al. [7] showed that the sheet-based TPMS structures exhibit enhanced mechanical properties compared to the strut-based TPMS lattices and other standard strut-based lattices, such as the octet-truss and the Kelvin unit cells due to the contribution of cell wall stretching. Other lattices have also been derived from TPMS lattices, such as functionally graded TPMS lattices [18] and honeycomb Gyroid [19]. While uniform lattices composed of one type of lattice structure repeated periodically have been extensively investigated in literature for their mechanical and physical properties, their promising properties suggest that hybrid structures made of two or more lattice types can exhibit even more advantageous and desired properties. This was indeed the focus of several recent studies [20 e 23]. For example, Alberdi et al. [20] investigated multimorphology lattices composed using FCC and BCC unit cells and showed improved plastic energy absorption under quasistatic compressive loading as compared to the individual unit cells. Moreover, they showed that a rational design approach could inspire multi-morphology lattices which exceed rule-ofmixtures expectations. Chen et al. [22] built on the fact that certain TPMS lattices exhibit high Young ' s modulus on the expense of shear modulus and vice versa to propose hybrid structures combining the different TPMS. They showed that the two individual moduli could complement each other in a hybrid structure yielding an ideal isotropic unit cell with high Young ' s, shear, and bulk moduli. White et al. [21] used the concept of multi-body interpenetrating lattices where two or more lattices intertwine through the same volume without any direct connection to each other to create a composite-like structure but made of the same constituent material. These previous studies shed light on the importance of studying multi-morphology lattices for different applications.

Computationally, numerical simulations enable detailed deformation analysis of lattices and allow predicting their mechanical and physical properties [24 e 26]. Several material models and simulation approaches have been investigated recently for sheet-based TPMS lattices. Some studies have focused only on the elastic properties of TPMS-sheet lattices [14,27], while other studies used elastic-ideal plastic constitutive models [28 e 33]. A few studies have used more complex constitutive models to study the deformation behaviour of TPMS-based lattices at large strains. For example, the plastic model within the finite element analysis (FEA) package Abaqus with isotropic hardening was used to capture the post-yield properties of sheet-networks lattices [32]. The thickness of sheets was adjusted to achieve a good correlation with experimental data. Abueidda et al. [12,34] used the Arruda-Boyce model, an elastic-viscoplastic constitutive model [35] and the flow evolution network (FEN) model and a hyperelastic-viscoplastic model [36] to investigate the mechanical properties of polymeric sheet-networks TPMS lattices. Despite their robustness, the simulations were conducted on a few unit cells only due to their computational cost and could not capture the entire behaviour until densification. Abu Ali et al. [37] also used the FEN model to investigate the mechanical properties of sheet-based TPMS lattices up to 17% strain. The computational simulations results showed good agreement with experimental evidence at low relative densities, while it overestimated the properties at high relative densities. The computational model with unit-cell approach and periodic boundary conditions was also used to determine the yield strength and energy absorption of shell lattices under limited strains [38,39]. Most of the computational studies of sheet-based TPMS lattices analyse their behaviour only at lower strains, e.g. to investigate elastic properties and yield strength [38]. Only a few studies are concerned with TPMS lattices ' behaviour up to the densification using solid finite elements (FE), which consume enormous computational time and resources [39].

This work aims to (1) investigate the mechanical properties of multi-morphology (hybrid) lattices with spatially variable cell types designed based on implicit functions, and (2) utilise and validate computational models using shell elements in order to decrease the computational costs. The new computational models can be used to evaluate other designs of implicit functions-based lattices and their crashworthiness performance and increased energy absorption capacity.
2. Methods
2.1. Geometry and fabrication of specimens

First, uniform lattices were designed for the purpose of experimental testing and validation of the material model. Two different lattices were considered in this work: Schwarz Diamond and Schoen Gyroid. The lattices were designed and generated using the in-house developed software MSLattice [40]. Each of the analysed lattices was designed with three relative densities ~16%, ~19%, and ~21%. The generated geometries are shown in Fig. 1.

The hybrid TPMS lattices were computer designed, computationally evaluated, fabricated and experimentally tested. The samples are a combination of Gyroid and Diamond lattices in longitudinal and radial loading direction with 16% relative density. The mathematical-based design procedure is described in [40], and the mathematical implementation is explained in details in Appendix 1. Figure 2 shows the CAD generated designs and 3D printed samples.
Fig. 1 e Computer-aided design models of the sheet-based TPMS lattices and additively-manufactured samples: Schwarz Diamond, b) Schoen Gyroid.
2.2. Sample fabrication

The generated lattices shown in Figs. 1 and 2 were fabricated using the powder bed fusion system EOS M280. The additive manufacturing machine uses a 400W Ytterbium fibre laser with a beam diameter between 100 m mand500 m m, and a scan speeds up to 7 m/s. Gas atomised stainless steel 316L powder was used to fabricate the plate samples with consistent and repeatable geometry [41].

The fabricated sample plates were blasted with highpressure air to remove the surrounding residual powder. The final testing samples with dimensions of ∅ 20 mm -20 mm were cut from the fabricated plates using the electric wire discharge system. The samples were weighed, and the actual relative densities were calculated in the range of 15% e 21.9%. As-built samples show a minor deviation of relative density from the CAD design. The deviation was 4 e 6% without a clear trend in relation to the relative density. This deviation is attributed to the combination of defects in density and partial adhesion of powder to the surfaces. Five specimens of each lattice group were fabricated and tested.
2.3. Experimental testing
Uniaxial compression tests following the standard ISO 13314:2011 [42] were performed by using a servo-hydraulic

INSTRON 8801 testing machine with the position-controlled cross-head rate of 0.1 mm/s. The testing conditions were the same for all analysed groups of specimens. The recorded load e displacement data were converted to engineering stress e strain data, using the initial specimen ' s dimensions. The samples shown in Fig. 1 were tested in previous work [41], and the results are used only for the validation of the computational model. The samples shown in Fig. 2 were developed and tested in this work. The plateau stress was calculated as average stress in the range of 20 e 40% compressive strain [42], while the specific energy absorption (SEA) was calculated with the integration of compression response up to 40% strain and divided by the density of specimens.

Additionally, the deformation behaviour was evaluated using infrared (IR) thermography, where loading velocity was increased to 284 mm/s to increase IR deformation signature. The Flir SC 5000 high-speed cooled middle-wave IR thermal camera(framerate 608 Hz with 0.02 K sensitive cooled middlewave InSb detector) was used for IR thermography to observe the evolution of plastic deformation of specimens during dynamic compression testing. This approach has proved to be sufficient to determine crushing deformation patterns in different cellular materials subject to complex loading conditions [43,44] and proves to be a reliable tool for the validation of computational models [6].

The stress e strain responses of the analysed specimens are shown in Section 3, where the experimental and computational results are compared. Only the average relationships for each group of specimens are plotted since the responses ' difference was negligible. The standard deviation values for plateau stress and SEA, which are deducted from stress e strain responses and provided in [41], confirm very high results reproducibility.
2.4. Computational modelling

The computational models of TPMS lattices were generated using the shell finite elements (FE) using the own MSLattice code to generate the fundamental lattice geometry of TMPS samples [40]. Meshing was performed by the PrePoMax v1.0.0 software [45], and the boundary conditions were defined in the LS-PrePost software. The manufacturing imperfections resulting in plate thickness variation analysed in our previous work [41] were considered indirectly through the used material parameters determined by inverse parametric
Fig. 2 e Computer model and fabricated specimens of (a) longitudinal and (b) radial hybrid TPMS lattices.
computational simulations. The explicit solver of the LSDYNA finite element software system [46] was used for all reported computer simulations.
2.4.1. Material model

The elastoplastic material model (MAT_024) was used to describe the base material ' s constitutive behaviour of the analysed TPMS lattices [46]. The model is a rate-dependent, three-piecewise-linear elastic-ideal plastic model [47]. Inverse computational simulations of loaded samples were performed to retrieve the same macroscopic simulation results as those measured in experimental testing for all TPMS geometries and an entire range of strains up to densification. The material parameters of the material model MAT_024 are given in Table 1. The values of yield stresses for AISI 361L are similar to those reported in [48], where the material parameters were determined using different standard tensile and shear specimens. The parameters for the material model are as follows: density r , Young ' s modulus E , Poisson ' s ratio n , initial yield stress s yield, the definition of linear hardening with the second point in the stress e strain diagram ( s 2, ε pl,2). Ideal plasticity after the ε pl,2 was assumed to avoid non-physical removal of FE in the case of considered failure.

The top and bottom compression plates were modelled as linear-elastic (material model MAT_ELASTIC) with the following material parameters: density r ¼ 7850 kg/m 3 , Young ' s modulus E ¼ 210 GPa and the Poisson ' s ratio n ¼ 0.3.
2.4.2. Boundary conditions and finite element mesh

Fully integrated shell finite elements with two throughthickness integration points were used to discretise the specimens ' geometry. The mesh sensitivity analysis was performed with three different FE meshes and three different numbers of through-thickness integration points. The approximate global size of FE was 0.25 mm, which results in approx. 90,000 FE for each lattice. The Belytschko-Tsay shell FE with two through-thickness integration points and the thickness of 1 mm was used to model the compression plates [46].

The following boundary conditions were used: the bottom compression plate with all degrees of freedom fixed, the top compression plate with prescribed constant velocity (2 m/s) towards the bottom plate to speed up the computation time (Fig. 3). The increase in quasi-static testing velocity (from 0.1 mm/s to 2 m/s) was confirmed as acceptable by parametric computational analysis, where the reaction forces on the bottom and upper plate were compared at different loading velocities.

The penalty based node to surface contact formulation with friction was defined between plates and the cellular structure, while the general contact with friction was defined between the TPMS cellular structure plates (shell FE). The coefficient of friction was set to 0.36 and 0.34 for static and dynamic case, respectively. The latter was slightly lower to account for the sliding conditions.

The average thickness of shell FE in computational models was determined using weight comparison between the CAD model, fabricated specimens (micro-computed tomography), and discretised computational models.
3. Computational simulations of TMPS lattices and results validation

The computational results of the constituent TMPS lattices were compared to quasi-static experimental data from [41] for each analysed geometry and relative density in mechanical response (stress e strain relationships) and deformation behaviour. The computational models ' deformation behaviour was validated with experimental observations recorded by IR thermography and HD video camera.
3.1. Diamond TMPS lattice

The experimental and computational stress e strain relationships of Diamond TPMS lattices with different relative densities are shown in Fig. 4. The overall response is quite comparable, where a typical response of cellular material is observed in experimental in computational results. Experimentally, the slope of the plateau stress increases with increasing the relative density. This is due to the local densification that takes place at earlier strain values in larger relative densities. Computational results show flat plateau stress until a strain value of around 35%, after which plateau will start to increase. The densification is captured well enough by the model. The results are further discussed in detail in Section 3.2.

Computationally and experimentally observed deformation behaviours are shown in Fig. 5. An excellent correlation of the highest plastic strain can be observed for the lowest and highest analysed relative density. The computational results offer a very precise analysis of deformation behaviour, where it can be observed that the layers of unit cells on top and bottom are collapsing first due to boundary effects. After that, the deformation is localised on one side of the specimens and propagates through the structure with increasing global deformation. The deformation is more localised in samples with lower relative densities, which results in limited transverse deformation of the structure. The surfaces of the lattices with lower relative densities can buckle and deform in smaller areas and therefore provide more local deformation. The location of the local deformation is influenced by the fabrication defects, which have a larger influence on the mechanical and deformation behaviour at lower relative densities. The deformation is less localised for higher relative densities, which results in more significant transverse deformation of the structure.
3.2. Gyroid TMPS lattice

The computational and experimental mechanical response of Gyroid TPMS lattices with different relative densities is shown in Fig. 6. Experimentally, the plateau stress is almost flat until a strain value of 35% where the effect of local densification starts to become apparent, and the slope of the plateau starts
Fig. 3 e Computational model of TPMS lattice.
Fig. 4 e Comparison of computational and experimental results for Diamond TPMS lattices with different relative densities.

increasing. The computationally predicted mechanical response shows a more pronounced stress drop in the plateau region and takes place at an earlier strain level (25% e 29%). The stress drop is caused by the collapse of one layer of unit cells in the lattice. The stress is more pronounced in the case of the computational model since it ' s geometry is ideal, and the deformation is more localised as in the case of experiments, where already some parts of other cells fails before and the deformation is not so localised. Overall, a good correlation can bededuced.Ahigherdiscrepancyofresultsisagainevidentfor higherrelativedensities,whichwillbediscussedinSection3.2. The model captures the structure densification well.

Very accurate deformation behaviour is captured in the computational model compared to the IR thermography recordings, as shown in Fig. 7. The deformation is localised in the centre of the structure and then spreads to the boundaries with increasing global deformation.
3.3. Results analysis
Expected mechanical response of cellular lattices was observed in all analysed samples, where the initial elastic area

Fig. 5 e Deformation behaviour of Diamond TPMS lattices with the lowest and highest relative density (top to bottom: computational simulations, IR thermography and video sequences of experimental testing).
Fig. 6 e Comparison of computational and experimental results for Gyroid TPMS lattices with different relative densities.

is accompanied by substantial plateau stress and final structure densification. The observed responses reveal a smooth transition from elastic to plastic region in all samples, which is a characteristic of sheet-based TPMS lattices.

The robustness of the computational model is evaluated in terms of capturing the plateau stress (average 20 e 40% strain) and SEA (up to 40% strain) in comparison to the experimental results in Tables 2 and 3.

The plateau stresses and SEA are in most cases underestimated in the computational models up to 24% error which takes place at the highest relative density of the tested Gyroid samples. The results are generally computationally overestimated for lower relative densities in the case of the Diamond and underestimated in the case of the Gyroid. For high relative densities, the computational results are always underestimated compared to experimental values. The results discrepancy originates from internal defects, e.g. micro-voids and micro-cracks, which have a stronger influence in structures with low relative densities. These defects reduce the sheets ' load-bearing capacity, showing lower values of mechanical properties compared to the computational model that assumes an ideal, defect-free material. The micro defects have a lower influence in samples with higher relative densities due to the smaller relative size of defects compared to the sheet thickness, leading to better load-bearing capacity.

The difference between the computational and experimental results and the possibility of capturing the correct densification strain is also related to the use of shell finite elementsindiscretisingthegeometry.Itshouldbenotedthatthe sheet-based TPMS lattice model used to fabricate the samples and conduct the experiments has been designed using levelset approximation equations. The minimal surface was designed at two iso-values, which define the smooth sheet geometry between them [15,40]. However, a thickening procedure involving offsetting the mid surface in both normal directions to define a shell thickness in building the FE model yields a slightly different structure geometry. This is illustrated in Fig. 8, where the two thickening procedures are visualised. At low relative densities, both thickening procedures yield almost identical geometries. However, at large relative densities, e.g. 50% relative density, the difference becomes more noticeable. This was also observed in other authors ' studies that used the volume FE to discretise the TMPS lattice models [32,49]. The use of solid finite elements to discretise the TMPS lattice geometry results in more accurate geometry representation and hence better description of densification but at the expense of very long computational times.

It should be noted that the shell FE are not suitable for very high relative densities (above 50%), where the walls become too thick, and the geometry representation becomes unrealistic. In that case, the thick shell formulation [46] can be used and will be studied in further work. In this study, the results showed that the relative densities in a range of 15 e 20% were best represented by the computational model using the shell FE.

Fig. 7 e Deformation behaviour of Gyroid TPMS lattices with the lowest and highest relative density (top to bottom: computational simulations, IR thermography and video sequences of experimental testing).
4. Hybrid TPMS lattice

After validating the computational models for two different fundamental TPMS lattices, the mechanical behaviour of hybrid TPMS lattices was analysed. The experimental tests in this study were performed the same way as for the uniform lattices [41]. The deformation behaviour of the longitudinal and radial hybrid lattices is shown in Fig. 9. In terms of deformation behaviour, an overall good agreement between the experiment and computational simulations can be observed. In longitudinal hybrid samples, the deformation is

initiated within the Gyroid lattice, which is less stiff than Diamond lattice (Fig. 9a). While the computational models suggest plastic deformation localisation on the interface between the two lattices, the IR images show evidence of a shear band formation within the Gyroid lattice. The majority of the deformation is taking place in the Gyroid lattice up until 15% strain, after which plastic deformation starts to propagate to the diamond lattice almost uniformly. In the case of radial hybrid lattice, where stiffer Diamond geometry is placed in the centre of lattice and is surrounded radially with the less stiff Gyroid lattice, the deformation is distributed more uniformly. Both computational simulations and IR images
Fig. 8 e Difference between TPMS sheet-lattices obtained through thickening of a shell by offsetting in both normal directions and using level-set equations to define the domain.
Fig. 9 e Deformation responses of longitudinal (a) and radial (b) hybrid lattices in computational simulations and experiment (IR thermography and video).
suggest stress localisation at the interface between both lattices (Fig. 9b).

The deformation of the radial hybrid lattice was more detailly analysed using the computational simulations, which enabled to plot the plastic strains at different cross-sections of the lattice (Fig. 10). The cross-sections are evenly spaced from the centre of the lattice at a distance of 3.3 mm. The results show that the deformation is more localised in the centre of the specimen built from the Diamond lattice. However, the interface between the two lattice types also shows the stress localisations due to the structure ' s abrupt stiffness change.

Stress e strain relationships are shown in Fig. 11 for the hybrid as well as uniform lattices for comparison purposes. The longitudinal hybrid lattice response at lower strains is comparable to that those of the uniform Gyroid and uniform Diamond structures. At higher strains, the early densification of the gyroid lattice and the increased contribution of the Diamond lattice drive the plateau stress to increases. This hardening behavior in the plateau region results in higher energy absorption especially beyond the 30% strain level which marks an inflation point where the plateau of the hybrid lattice surpasses that of the diamond lattice. Such behavior can be beneficial in different engineering applications, such as crashworthiness, blast, impact, and ballistic protection. In the case of longitudinal hybrid lattices (Fig. 11a), it can be observed that the simulation response is less stiff than the experimental response, especially at lower strains, which is a consequence of computationally underestimated plateau stresses for Gyroid geometry (Table 2).

The mechanical response of the radial hybrid lattice is shown in Fig. 11b. The plateau stress is much more uniform and constant (flat) than the longitudinal hybrid lattice. The computational and experimental results in the case of radial hybrid lattices are in better correlation than in longitudinal hybrid lattices, which is a consequence of the combined deformation of both lattice geometries. The densification issues in computational models, which were discussed in the previous section, are also present in hybrid lattices.

Comparing the experimental plateau stresses of uniform and longitudinal hybrid structures show up to 19% and 11% enhancementcomparedtotheGyroidandDiamondstructures, respectively. In radial hybrid structures, plateau stress is up to 11% higher than in Gyroid structures and 1% higher than in Diamond structures. The SEA of longitudinal and radial hybrid lattices is 10% and 7% higher than in uniform Gyroid lattice and 5% and 8% lower than in uniform Diamond lattice. A large
Fig. 10 e Strain distribution at 15% strain in the cross-sections at different depths of the lattice.
Fig. 11 e Mechanical response of longitudinal (a) and radial (b) hybrid lattices and comparison to computational results.

discrepancy between the computational and experimental results up to 22% underestimation in longitudinal hybrid lattices can be observed, which is attributed to underlying underestimation of the plateau stresses already in the uniform Gyroid lattices as well as observed significant deformation localisation. As shown in Fig. 9a, the deformation localisation at lower strains in the Gyroid lattice is much more pronounced in the simulations than observed in the experiment.
5. Conclusions

In this work, hybrid additively manufactured lattices were investigated for their mechanical behaviour both numerically and experimentally. First, computational models of the TPMS lattices were developed and validated up to the densification strain. The validation of computational models was done using the quasi-static experimental results from previous work [41]. The development of a computational model for explicit LS-DYNA analysis consisted of generating the geometries in MSLattice, meshing in PrePoMax and importing to LSPrePost, where boundary conditions were defined. The validated computational models can predict the correct deformation behaviour. The computed mechanical responses (stress e strain relationships) are also comparable to experimental results in general. The plateau stress and SEA differ up to 24% and 12%, respectively, for analysed high relative densities. The main reason for the discrepancy is the generation of computational models based on the shell finite element. This could be alleviated by using the volume FE instead of shell FE, which would result in significantly increased computational times and divergence issues for larger deformations. The use of both shell and volume FE leads to delayed densification of the computational model [49]. This study shows that the shell FEs are appropriate for computational simulations of TPMS lattices up to the densification.

Longitudinal and radial hybrid TPMS lattices consisting of Diamond and Gyroid lattices were then developed, fabricated and evaluated using both experiments and computational simulations. Longitudinal hybrid lattices exhibit an inclined plateau region with hardening behaviour due to consecutive deformationanddensificationofthelessstiffGyroidlatticeina layer-wise manner first followed by more controlled deformation and densification of stiffer Diamond lattice next. The plateau stress in radial hybrid lattices is much more uniform and constant due to the concurrent deformation of radially spaced lattices. The plateau region with progressive characteristic (longitudinal hybrid) andconstantcharacteristic(radial hybrid) can be achieved using the hybrid lattices. Such properties can be beneficial in different engineering applications, e.g. crashworthiness, blast, impact and ballistic protection.

Anisotropic structure-property relations of FDM printed short glass fiber reinforced polyamide TPMS structures under quasi-static compression
Prince Jeya Lal Lazar a , Jeyanthi Subramanian b , Elango Natarajan c,d, * , Kalaimani Markandan c , S. Ramesh e
a Department of Mechanical Engineering, Rajalakshmi Institute of Technology, Chennai 600124, Tamil Nadu, India
b School of Mechanical Engineering, VIT Chennai Campus, Chennai 600127, Tamil Nadu, India
c Faculty of Engineering, Technology and Built Environment, UCSI University, Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia
d Department of Mechanical Engineering, PSG Institute of Technology and Applied Research, Coimbatore 641062, Tamil Nadu, India

a r t i c l e i n f o
Article history: Received 31 March 2023 Accepted 18 May 2023
Available online 23 May 2023
Keywords:
Triply minimal surface lattice (TPMS)
ePA-GF
Quasi-static compression Structure-property relations Gibson-Ashby constants Deformation mechanisms
a b s t r a c t

Triply minimal surface structures of short glass fiber reinforced polyamide with different relative densities were prepared and the effects of induced anisotropy, cell topology, and relative density on the compressive properties were evaluated. Schwarz Diamond, Schoen Gyroid, and Schwarz Primitive structures were 3D printed with relative densities ranging from 0.2 to 0.4, and compression properties along the axial and lateral build directions were determined. Gipson-Ashby numerical parameters required to establish a relation between the cell topology and the compressive properties of the lattice structures were estimated. The plastic deformation and failure mechanisms were analyzed. Results revealed that the compression properties are dominated by the cell topology rather than the relative density. Besides, the present work confirmed that the compressive properties of the lattice structures fabricated with short fiber reinforcement are significantly affected by anisotropy. These structures exhibited a higher compressive modulus and lower peak compressive stress in the lateral direction, which can be attributed to the inline orientation of the short glass fibers. Schwarz Diamond proved to be the stiffest structure, followed by Schoen Gyroid and Schwarz Primitive under axial and lateral compression. Likewise, Schwarz Primitive generated low stresses compared to Schoen Gyroid and Schwarz Diamond. All cell topologies deformed in a controlled manner layer by layer, minimizing undulations in the load-bearing capacity of the structures. D and G structures compressed in the axial direction exhibited significant strain hardening and identical structures compressed in the lateral direction exhibited stable post-yield behavior with negligible undulations which is highly preferred for crashworthiness applications.
